description: Detects requests to Calendaromatic API endpoints used by the Calendaromatic application. Activity matching this query may indicate a client fetching JSON that contains Unicode homoglyphs which are decoded by the application into bits and reconstructed into executable code. This may be a covert channel that can be abused for command-and-control, payload delivery, or update-based backdoor activation.
name: Calendaromatic API Access via Homoglyph Domain Suggesting Covert C2
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and (url.address contains ('/api/calendar', '/health') and url.address contains 'calendaromatic')

description: Detects blocked attempts to misuse credentials or bypass access controls. Events include denied brute-force logins, blocked privilege misuse, and prevented policy-violating access. Although these actions were successfully stopped, they reveal efforts to compromise identities or escalate access. Monitoring blocked access abuse provides early warning of account takeover attempts or malicious insider behavior.
name: Cato Networks Access Protection IPS Events
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cato Networks' and event.type = 'Security' and metadata.log_name = 'IPS' and vulnerabilities\[0\].desc contains ('Brute Force', 'Privilege Escalation', 'Policy Violation')

description: Detects file transfers that matched a configured anti-malware policy rule in Cato Network. A rule match typically indicates deliberate blocking based on file characteristics, behavior patterns, or policy-defined restrictions, helping enforce organizational security controls.
name: Cato Networks Anti-Malware Rule Match
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cato Networks' and event.type = 'Security' and metadata.log_name contains 'NG Anti Malware' and risk_details = 'match'

description: Detects DNS queries associated with known cryptocurrency mining pools, mining services, or infrastructure commonly used by cryptomining malware. Such activity often indicates unauthorized resource usage resulting from compromised hosts, malicious scripts, or insider abuse. Cryptomining can degrade system performance, increase operational costs, and serve as a foothold for broader malicious campaigns. Monitoring cryptomining-related DNS activity helps identify infected assets, enforce acceptable use policies, and prevent prolonged exploitation of computing resources.
name: Cato Networks Crypto Mining DNS Events
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cato Networks' and event.type = 'Security' and metadata.log_name contains 'DNS Protection' and resources\[1\].data.dns_protection_category contains ('Crypto Miners')

description: Detects attempts to expose, exfiltrate, or improperly access sensitive information. This covers information disclosure activities such as data leakage, exposure of credentials or internal resources, and unintended data sharing over network channels. Even when blocked, these events indicate adversary reconnaissance or data harvesting efforts and may signal preparation for further compromise. Monitoring information disclosure attempts helps identify weak points in data handling and prevent loss of sensitive assets.
name: Cato Networks Data Protection IPS Events
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cato Networks' and event.type = 'Security' and metadata.log_name = 'IPS' and vulnerabilities\[0\].desc contains 'Information Disclosure'

description: Detects destination IPs that were blocked by DNS protection but later allowed by the Internet firewall. This correlation highlights potential attempts to bypass DNS security controls, indicating suspicious or unauthorized access that may be used for data exfiltration, command-and-control communication, or policy evasion.
name: Cato Networks DNS Blocked IP Observed in Allowed Traffic

description: Detects DNS requests that triggered DNS Protection detections but were ultimately allowed by policy. These events indicate communication with domains associated with malicious, suspicious, or policy-violating activity that was not blocked due to configuration, reputation thresholds, or temporary allowlisting. Even when permitted, such DNS activity can signal early-stage compromise, command-and-control beaconing, or user interaction with risky infrastructure. Monitoring allowed DNS Protection events helps identify gaps in enforcement, assess exposure to known threats, and uncover stealthy or misclassified malicious activity before escalation.
name: Cato Networks DNS Protection Allowed Events
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cato Networks' and event.type = 'Security' and metadata.log_name contains 'DNS Protection' and action != 'Block'

description: Detects files that use double extensions such as invoice.pdf.exe to masquerade as benign documents while hiding executable content. This is a widely used social engineering and malware distribution technique. In Cato Networks, identifying such disguised downloads helps detect phishing delivery chains and malicious payload staging early.
name: Cato Networks Double Extension File Download
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cato Networks' and event.type = 'Security' and metadata.log_name contains 'NG Anti Malware' and observables\[7\].value matches '\\.[A-Za-z0-9]+\\.[A-Za-z0-9]+$'

description: Detects encrypted file transfers that anti-malware inspection could not analyze. Encryption prevents inspection engines from evaluating content, creating an opportunity for malware distribution through password-protected archives or encrypted payloads.
name: Cato Networks Encrypted File Detected
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cato Networks' and event.type = 'Security' and metadata.log_name contains 'NG Anti Malware' and risk_details contains 'encrypted' and action = 'Allow'

description: Detects executable file downloads transferred over unencrypted HTTP. Malicious servers commonly distribute payloads over plaintext channels to avoid certificate requirements and make interception harder to detect. In Cato Networks, an executable received over HTTP is a strong indicator of unsafe hosting infrastructure or malicious distribution activity.
name: Cato Networks Executable Delivered Over Plain HTTP
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cato Networks' and event.type = 'Security' and metadata.log_name contains 'NG Anti Malware' and (observables\[5\].name = 'url' and observables\[5\].value contains 'http://') and (observables\[7\].name = 'file_name' and observables\[7\].value matches '.*\\.(exe|sh|dll|aspx|php|zip|7z|bin|ps1|js)$') and action = 'Allow'

description: Detects file downloads originating from domains that use disposable, high-risk, or commonly abused top-level domains. Many malware distribution infrastructures rely on such TLDs due to low cost, weak registration validation, and short operational lifetime. In Cato Networks, file downloads from these domains regardless of engine indicate a heightened risk of phishing, malware staging, or malicious redirections.
name: Cato Networks Files from Suspicious or Disposable TLDs
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cato Networks' and event.type = 'Security' and metadata.log_name contains 'NG Anti Malware' and (observables\[5\].name = 'url' and observables\[5\].value matches '\\.(xyz|biz|top|click|blog|zip|live)\\/') and action = 'Allow'

description: Detects high-risk suspicious activities identified by Cato’s SAM (Suspicious Activity Monitoring) engine. These events represent behaviors strongly associated with malicious intent, such as lateral movement, abnormal network usage, or exploitation attempts, based on behavioral analysis rather than static indicators. High-risk SAM alerts often indicate active attacker presence or advanced threat activity within the environment. Monitoring these detections enables early identification of sophisticated attacks, reduces dwell time, and supports rapid incident response before significant impact occurs.
name: Cato Networks High Risk Suspicious Activity
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cato Networks' and event.type = 'Security' and metadata.log_name = 'Suspicious Activity' and severity = 'high'

description: Detects file transfers flagged as malicious by Cato Network’s anti-malware inspection. A malicious verdict indicates the file matches known malware characteristics or high-confidence indicators and was blocked before reaching the destination. This behavior typically reflects an attempted malware delivery, compromise attempt, or lateral propagation.
name: Cato Networks Malicious File Detected
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cato Networks' and event.type = 'Security' and metadata.log_name contains 'NG Anti Malware' and risk_details = 'virus_found'

description: Detects when the same file hash appears in anti-malware events across multiple hosts in a short time window. This strongly indicates a widespread distribution vector, central staging site, or malware propagation attempt. In Cato Networks, repeated detections of the same hash across different devices is a high-value indicator of coordinated distribution or internal spread.
name: Cato Networks Malware Hash Detected Across Multiple Endpoints

description: Detects multiple anti-malware events generated by the same device within a short period. A rapid series of detections may indicate an active infection, automated malware propagation, or repeated attempts to download malicious content. In Cato Networks, multiple alerts in quick succession from the same host strongly suggest ongoing compromise activity or a user repeatedly interacting with dangerous content.
name: Cato Networks Multiple Anti-Malware Alerts from Single Host

description: Detects repeated IPS alerts involving the same source and destination hosts, regardless of traffic direction (inbound or outbound). This pattern often reflects focused malicious activity such as automated exploit kits, coordinated scanning, multi-stage reconnaissance, or active compromise attempts. When multiple different IPS signatures fire between the same two endpoints, it suggests persistent targeting or ongoing malicious communication. Monitoring for this behavior in both inbound and outbound flows helps quickly identify attacker and victim pairs, command-and-control exchanges, or lateral movement requiring immediate investigation.
name: Cato Networks Multiple IPS Events between same hosts

description: Detects multiple different IPS threat categories triggered by the same host within a short time window. This pattern suggests the host is attempting multiple stages of an attack sequence—such as reconnaissance, brute force, exploitation, and malware activity. In Cato Networks, several diverse IPS events from the same internal or external IP strongly indicate malicious automation or an active compromise.
name: Cato Networks Multiple Threat Categories Triggered by Single Host

description: Detects blocked attempts to exploit systems or deliver malicious content.Detects blocked traffic originating from suspicious or probing sources. This includes scanning attempts, vulnerability enumeration, anonymizer traffic, and connections from low-reputation hosts. These activities are stopped before reaching internal systems, but they still indicate that external actors are mapping the environment or testing defenses. Monitoring blocked reconnaissance helps the SOC understand targeting patterns and anticipate potential exploitation attempts.
name: Cato Networks Network Protection IPS Events
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cato Networks' and event.type = 'Security' and metadata.log_name = 'IPS' and vulnerabilities\[0\].desc contains ('Scanner', 'Network Scan', 'Vulnerability Scan', 'DoS', 'Anonymizer', 'Reputation', 'Spam')

description: Detects file transfers where the anti-malware engine reported no scannable content. Attackers may distribute placeholder files, stagers, or files disguised as legitimate downloads that contain no direct payload but trigger secondary malicious retrieval.
name: Cato Networks No-Content File Event
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cato Networks' and event.type = 'Security' and metadata.log_name contains 'NG Anti Malware' and risk_details contains 'no scan' and action = 'Allow'

description: Detects internal hosts generating an unusually high number of outbound IPS alerts within a short time window. Excessive outbound malicious traffic commonly results from malware infection, botnet activity, crypto mining, phishing tools, or remote code execution attempts. This typically indicates a compromised or misused system requiring immediate investigation.
name: Cato Networks Outbound IPS Alert Flood Indicating Potential Host Infection

description: Detects cases where an external IP that previously triggered an inbound IPS alert later receives outbound allowed traffic from an internal host. This pattern may indicate a compromised system initiating a callback to a malicious IP that previously probed or attacked the network. Correlating inbound IPS detections with subsequent outbound connections helps expose early-stage compromise, lateral movement setup, or command and control establishment.
name: Cato Networks Potential Callback to IPS Detected External IP

description: Detects when the same endpoint repeatedly attempts to download the same file identified as potential malware within a short window. This behavior often indicates a user ignoring warnings, social engineering influence, or malware delivery attempts where the attacker prompts multiple downloads. In Cato Networks, such repeated interactions highlight risky user behavior or ongoing attack attempts.
name: Cato Networks Repeated Malicious File Download Attempts

description: Detects files that bypassed anti-malware scanning because the file exceeded Cato Network’s size scanning limits. Attackers may intentionally use oversized payloads to avoid inspection, which can enable delivery of malicious archives, installers, or encrypted containers that circumvent security controls.
name: Cato Networks Scan Bypass Due to Large File Size
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cato Networks' and event.type = 'Security' and metadata.log_name contains 'NG Anti Malware' and risk_details contains 'bypass by size'

description: Detects files that were not scanned due to being below the anti-malware engine’s minimum size threshold. Very small payloads, stagers, or scripts may intentionally fall below scanning requirements to evade detection and initiate larger follow-on downloads or attack stages.
name: Cato Networks Scan Bypass Due to Small File Size
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cato Networks' and event.type = 'Security' and metadata.log_name contains 'NG Anti Malware' and risk_details contains 'bypass by min size'

description: Detects downloads of script-based file types commonly used as initial-stage malware droppers, such as JavaScript, VBScript, WSF, HTA, or PowerShell files. These scripting formats are frequently abused to execute payloads, bypass security controls, or retrieve secondary-stage malware. In Cato Networks, detecting these file types is a strong early indicator of phishing or malware installation attempts.
name: Cato Networks Script-Based File Downloaded
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cato Networks' and event.type = 'Security' and metadata.log_name contains 'NG Anti Malware' and (observables\[7\].name = 'file_name' and observables\[7\].value matches '.*\\.(vbs|js|wsf|hta|ps1|sh|bat|cmd)$') and action = 'Allow'

description: Detects sudden increases in a specific IPS signature across multiple events. A spike in one threat_name often indicates widespread exploitation campaigns, opportunistic scanning, or newly weaponized vulnerabilities. This behavior suggests the organization is being targeted as part of a larger internet-wide attack wave. Monitoring signature-level surges helps with early awareness and response.
name: Cato Networks Spike in Specific IPS Threat Category

description: Detects destination IPs that appear in both Anti-Malware alerts and DNS Protection events specifically classified as “Newly Registered Domain.” This correlation highlights attempts by malware to communicate with or resolve infrastructure hosted on fresh domains commonly used for command-and-control setup, staging servers, or initial callback channels. By combining Anti-Malware findings with NRD DNS protection alerts, the detection raises confidence in identifying early-stage or emerging malicious infrastructure.
name: Cato Networks Suspicious Activity Involving Newly Registered Domain

description: Detects file transfers classified as suspicious by Cato Network’s anti-malware analysis. Suspicious verdicts represent files exhibiting incomplete or low-confidence malicious traits, often associated with early-stage malware, obfuscated payloads, or atypical content. Such activity may indicate reconnaissance, staged attacks, or attempted evasion.
name: Cato Networks Suspicious File Detected
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cato Networks' and event.type = 'Security' and metadata.log_name contains 'NG Anti Malware' and risk_details = 'suspicious'

description: Detects destination IPs where outbound HTTPS traffic (port 443) is allowed by the Internet Firewall but TLS inspection reports certificate validation problems such as unknown certificate authority, bad certificate, or expired certificate. This correlation highlights potentially malicious or untrusted encrypted communication attempting to evade inspection, which is commonly used by malware, unauthorized tools, or adversary infrastructure that relies on self-signed, misconfigured, or deliberately invalid certificates. Identifying these combinations helps reveal suspicious encrypted C2 channels and unsafe HTTPS traffic that would otherwise appear legitimate by port alone.
name: Cato Networks Suspicious TLS Errors on Allowed HTTPS Traffic

description: Detects blocked attempts to exploit systems or deliver malicious content. This covers web attacks, remote code execution attempts, malware delivery, and unwanted or malicious software that was prevented from executing. Even when blocked, these events signal that external actors are actively attempting compromise. Monitoring blocked exploitation helps identify targeted assets and assess ongoing threat campaigns.
name: Cato Networks Threat Prevention IPS Events
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cato Networks' and event.type = 'Security' and metadata.log_name = 'IPS' and vulnerabilities\[0\].desc contains ('Malware', 'Remote Code Execution', 'Web Application Attack', 'Crypto Mining', 'Malicious Browser Extension', 'PuP', 'Phishing')

description: Detects suspicious commands executed with certutil.exe, a legitimate Windows utility often abused by adversaries to facilitate malicious activity. Certutil.exe can be used to download files, decode base64-encoded payloads, or perform other tasks while evading detection by leveraging its trusted status. Attackers may exploit this tool to establish command and control channels, retrieve additional payloads, or exfiltrate sensitive data in a stealthy manner.
name: CertUtil Command Execution
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('certutil.exe') or tgt.process.displayName = 'CertUtil.exe') and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('decode', 'encode', 'urlcache', 'verifyctl') and not (src.process.user contains ('SYSTEM', 'AUTORITE NT', 'SISTEMA'))

description: Detects the execution of the pkill utility to terminate the cfprefsd daemon, which manages application and system preferences on macOS. Adversaries may target cfprefsd to manipulate or reset user preferences, evade detection, or interfere with system behavior. Unexpected termination of this daemon could indicate an attempt to disrupt security controls or alter user settings stealthily.
name: Cfprefsd Daemon Termination via Pkill Utility
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'osx'  and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.image.path matches 'bin/pkill$' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'cfprefsd'

subQueries.subQuery: tgt.process.name contains:anycase '7z'

tgt.process.name in:anycase ('curl.exe') and tgt.process.cmdline contains ' -X POST' and (src.process.parent.cmdline contains ('aHR0cHM6Ly', 'aHR0cDovL', 'aAB0AHQAcABzADoALwAvA', 'aAB0AHQAcAA6AC8ALw', 'Expand-Archive', 'ExtractToDirectory', 'TryFromBase64Chars', 'TryFromBase64String', 'FromBase64CharArray', 'FromBase64String', 'Decompress', 'DeflateStream', '-E ', '-En ', '-Enc ', '-Enco ', '-Encod ', '-Encode', '-Ec ') or src.process.cmdline contains ('aHR0cHM6Ly', 'aHR0cDovL', 'aAB0AHQAcABzADoALwAvA', 'aAB0AHQAcAA6AC8ALw', 'Expand-Archive', 'ExtractToDirectory', 'TryFromBase64Chars', 'TryFromBase64String', 'FromBase64CharArray', 'FromBase64String', 'Decompress', 'DeflateStream', '-E ', '-En ', '-Enc ', '-Enco ', '-Encod ', '-Encode', '-Ec '))

description: Detects execution of 7z to create an archive immediately followed by a curl.exe POST command. Adversaries can abuse this chained behavior to compress and package sensitive files and then exfiltrate the archive to a remote C2 or file-hosting endpoint via HTTP(S). This technique enables efficient bulk data theft while reducing the number of outbound requests, and may be used to bypass simple content filters or detection rules that look for individual file transfers.
name: Chained 7z Compression and Curl Exfiltration

subQueries.subQuery: event.type = 'IP Connect' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('curl.exe') or src.process.displayName = 'The curl executable') and src.process.cmdline contains 'http' and src.process.cmdline contains '-o' and src.process.cmdline contains '.zip'

event.type = 'Command Script' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe', 'pwsh.exe', 'powershell_ise.exe') or src.process.displayName in ('Windows PowerShell')) and cmdScript.content contains 'Expand-Archive' and cmdScript.content contains ('-Path', '-LiteralPath', '-PSPath') and cmdScript.content contains '-DestinationPath' and cmdScript.content contains '.zip'

event.type in ('Process Creation', 'Module Load') and (src.process.name in:anycase ('cscript.exe', 'wscript.exe') or src.process.displayName in:anycase ('Microsoft ® Console Based Script Host', 'Microsoft ® Windows Based Script Host')) and src.process.cmdline matches '\\.vbs'

description: Detects sequential execution of multiple native Windows utilities indicative of a multi-stage attack chain. This behavior is often leveraged by adversaries to facilitate initial access, privilege escalation, lateral movement, or payload delivery using trusted system binaries. Chained use of built-in utilities can bypass traditional security controls and evade detection by blending with legitimate activity. Such patterns are associated with advanced threat actors employing 'living off the land' techniques to maintain stealth and persistence within target environments.
name: Chained Execution of Native Windows Utilities Script Host

description: Detects events matching known patterns associated with the Checkmarx TeamPCP supply chain compromise. This activity is security-relevant because adversaries can leverage tampered trusted software to introduce malicious code and gain execution within otherwise authorized enterprise workflows. Supply chain compromise is a common initial access technique that abuses vendor trust to establish a foothold and expand impact across dependent environments.
name: Checkmarx TeamPCP Supply Chain Compromise Activity
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and ((src.process.cmdline contains '.sh' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'curl' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'POST' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'tpcp.tar.gz' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'checkmarx') or (src.process.cmdline contains 'git' and src.process.cmdline contains 'checkout' and src.process.cmdline contains '-b' and src.process.cmdline contains ('docs-tpcp', 'tpcp-docs')) or (tgt.process.cmdline contains 'git' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'checkout' and tgt.process.cmdline contains '-b' and  tgt.process.cmdline contains ('docs-tpcp', 'tpcp-docs')))

description: Detects events where the Check Point next-generation firewall permits traffic to applications or destinations categorized as 'spyware' or 'malicious sites'. Such allowed flows can indicate policy gaps or misconfigurations that enable potential command-and-control, malware delivery, or further compromise. Occasional false positives may occur due to category misclassification or sanctioned security testing.
name: Check Point NGFW Allowed Access to Spyware or Malicious Sites
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Check Point Next Generation Firewall' and (unmapped.app_properties contains 'Spyware' or unmapped.app_properties contains 'Malicious Sites') and (unmapped.app_properties contains 'Critical Risk' or unmapped.app_properties contains 'High risk') and unmapped.action in ('Allow','Accept')

description: Detects Check Point Next Generation Firewall events where traffic rated as critical severity was permitted by policy. This condition indicates risk acceptance or misconfiguration that allows high-impact threats or high-risk applications to traverse protected segments, increasing the likelihood of compromise or data loss. In some environments, these events may reflect sanctioned business exceptions or temporary change windows.
name: Check Point NGFW Allowed Critical Risk Traffic
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Check Point Next Generation Firewall' and unmapped.app_properties contains 'Critical Risk' and unmapped.action in ('Allow','Accept')

description: Detects allowed downloads of executable or script files as reported by Check Point NGFW telemetry. This activity can indicate potential malware delivery, weak content filtering, or policy gaps enabling risky file transfer from untrusted sources. The alert highlights exposure points where threat actors could deliver payloads during initial access, with common benign cases including sanctioned software distribution or administrative transfers.
name: Check Point NGFW Allowed Download of Executables or Scripts
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Check Point Next Generation Firewall' and (evidences\[0\].connection_info.direction = 'outbound') and unmapped.action in ('Allow','Accept') and event.type in ('URL Filtering', 'Application Control', 'Anti Malware', 'New Anti Virus', 'Smart Defense') and (unmapped.resource contains ('.exe','.dll','.scr','.ps1','.bat','.msi','.vbs') or finding_info.src_url contains ('.exe','.dll','.scr','.ps1','.bat','.msi','.vbs'))

description: Detects Check Point NGFW events where outbound access to anonymizer services such as public VPNs, web proxies, or Tor is allowed by policy. Such egress can enable command-and-control obfuscation, data exfiltration, and evasion of network-based controls by masking source attribution. This activity is security-relevant in environments that restrict anonymizer usage and may indicate policy gaps or misuse. Limited false positives may occur for sanctioned research, privacy tooling, or break-glass access under approved exceptions.
name: Check Point NGFW Allowed Egress to Anonymizer Services
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Check Point Next Generation Firewall' and (evidences\[0\].connection_info.direction = 'outbound') and unmapped.action in ('Allow','Accept') and unmapped.app_properties contains 'Anonymizer'

description: Detects firewall events where a Check Point NGFW permits outbound SMB traffic from internal hosts to external networks. Exposing SMB beyond the perimeter is high risk and can facilitate data exfiltration, credential exposure, or malware propagation, often indicating weak egress controls or policy drift. In some environments this may reflect sanctioned file transfer or remote management, but it is uncommon on internet-bound paths and warrants review.
name: Check Point NGFW Allowed Outbound SMB Traffic
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Check Point Next Generation Firewall' and event.type = 'VPN-1 & FireWall-1' and evidences\[0\].connection_info.direction = 'outbound' and unmapped.action in ('Allow','Accept') and (evidences\[0\].connection_info.protocol_name in ('SMB','CIFS') or evidences\[0\].src_endpoint.svc_name = 445)

description: Detects outbound traffic on Check Point Next Generation Firewalls that is flagged as malicious by threat prevention yet permitted by the access policy. This condition indicates a potential egress control gap or detect-only posture that could enable command and control, malware retrieval, or data exfiltration. Limited false positives may occur during approved security testing or when low-severity detections are configured to 'allow and log'.
name: Check Point NGFW Allowed Outbound Threat Events
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Check Point Next Generation Firewall' and evidences\[0\].connection_info.direction = 'outbound' and unmapped.action in ('Allow','Accept') and unmapped.attack = *

description: Detects events where a Check Point Next Generation Firewall permits peer-to-peer application connections. This activity is security-relevant because P2P traffic can enable data exfiltration, shadow IT, and evasion of traditional network controls, indicating a potential policy gap or risky exception. In some environments this may occur during sanctioned distribution workflows or controlled testing, but it should be validated against policy.
name: Check Point NGFW Allowed Peer-to-Peer Application Connections
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Check Point Next Generation Firewall' and (unmapped.app_properties contains 'P2P File Sharing' or unmapped.app_properties contains 'BitTorrent protocol') and (unmapped.app_properties contains 'High risk' or unmapped.app_properties contains 'Critical Risk') and unmapped.action in ('Allow','Accept')

description: Detects Check Point Next-Generation Firewall events where network traffic rated as high-severity risk was permitted by policy. This condition indicates a potential control gap or misconfiguration allowing likely malicious or highly suspicious communications, increasing exposure to compromise or data loss. In some environments, sanctioned exceptions or temporary policy overrides may generate benign matches.
name: Check Point NGFW High-Severity Threat Traffic Permitted
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Check Point Next Generation Firewall' and unmapped.app_properties contains 'High risk' and unmapped.action in ('Allow','Accept')

description: Detects HTTP traffic permitted by Check Point NGFW policies on non-standard ports, indicating permissive or misconfigured access controls. Such allowances expand the attack surface and can enable protocol evasion, command and control, or data exfiltration over atypical service ports. Legitimate use cases such as bespoke web services, reverse proxies, or administrative interfaces running on alternate ports may appear as false positives.
name: Check Point NGFW HTTP Traffic Permitted on Non-Standard Ports
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Check Point Next Generation Firewall' and event.type = 'VPN-1 & FireWall-1' and unmapped.action in ('Allow','Accept') and evidences\[0\].connection_info.protocol_name = 'HTTP' and not (evidences\[0\].src_endpoint.svc_name in (80,443,8080,8443))

description: Detects instances where the Check Point next-generation firewall allowed outbound Remote Desktop Protocol traffic from internal hosts to external destinations. This behavior is security-relevant because RDP egress can enable unauthorized remote access, facilitate lateral movement beyond the perimeter, or bypass intended egress controls. Legitimate remote administration to approved jump hosts or managed service providers may generate benign matches.
name: Check Point NGFW Permitted Outbound RDP Traffic
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Check Point Next Generation Firewall' and event.type = 'VPN-1 & FireWall-1' and evidences\[0\].connection_info.direction = 'outbound' and unmapped.action in ('Allow','Accept') and (evidences\[0\].connection_info.protocol_name = 'RDP' or evidences\[0\].src_endpoint.svc_name = 3389)

description: Detects a Check Point next-generation firewall configuration or event state where remote administrative access is allowed, elevating exposure of management services. This condition is security-relevant because it expands the attack surface and can enable unauthorized control-plane access, configuration tampering, or policy manipulation. In some environments this may occur during sanctioned maintenance windows or vendor support sessions, but persistent allowance of remote administration represents a high-risk posture.
name: Check Point NGFW Remote Administration Allowed (Critical Risk)
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Check Point Next Generation Firewall' and evidences\[0\].connection_info.direction = 'outbound' and unmapped.app_properties contains 'Remote Administration' and unmapped.app_properties contains 'Critical Risk' and unmapped.action in ('Allow','Accept')

description: Detects the modification of registry values via command-line execution to disable Application-Bound Encryption in Chromium-based browsers. Adversaries may abuse this feature to bypass Application-Bound Encryption, a security measure implemented in newer versions of Chromium browsers to protect cookies from credential access attacks. This detection helps identify potential suspicious activity that could indicate an attempt to exploit this feature for malicious purposes.
name: Chromium Browser App-Bound Encryption Disabled via Command Line

description: Detects the execution of Chromium-based browsers with remote debugging enabled, a feature that adversaries may abuse to extract sensitive information such as cookies and credentials. Attackers can leverage remote debugging to bypass Application-Bound Encryption, a security measure designed to protect stored authentication tokens. This technique is often used by info-stealer malware to gain unauthorized access to user accounts. Monitoring for this behavior helps identify attempts to exploit this feature for credential theft and unauthorized access.
name: Chromium Browser Info Stealer via Remote Debugging

description: Detects when an administrator account is deleted in Cisco Duo. Deleting an admin account is a high-risk action that could remove oversight or hinder incident response capabilities. If performed maliciously, it may be part of an attempt to weaken security controls or cover up unauthorized activities.
name: Cisco Duo Administrator Account Deletion
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Duo' and unmapped.action = 'admin_delete' and unmapped.eventtype = 'administrator'

description: Detects failed login attempts to a Cisco Duo administrator account. Repeated or unexpected administrator login failures may indicate unauthorized access attempts or credential misuse. Adversaries may try to gain administrative access to modify authentication settings, disable protections, or monitor user activity.
name: Cisco Duo Administrator Multiple Login Failures

description: Detects when a password reset email is sent to a Cisco Duo administrator account. While this is a legitimate action for account recovery, it could also indicate a potential account takeover attempt. Adversaries may trigger password reset emails to compromise administrative access, especially if they have access to the associated email account. Repeated or unexpected reset requests should be investigated to ensure administrator accounts remain secure
name: Cisco Duo Admin Password Reset Email Sent
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Duo' and unmapped.action = 'admin_send_reset_password_email'

description: Detects the creation or deletion of an application integration in Cisco Duo. While these actions are often part of normal setup or decommissioning processes, they may also indicate unauthorized changes that introduce unapproved access points or remove MFA enforcement from protected applications.
name: Cisco Duo Application Integration Creation or Deletion
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Duo' and unmapped.action in ('integration_create' ,'integration_delete')

description: Detects when an authentication attempt is made from an endpoint that is not trusted according to Duo’s endpoint security policies. This may indicate the use of unmanaged, potentially insecure devices. Monitoring such events helps enforce device compliance and prevents unauthorized access from unknown or non-compliant systems.
name: Cisco Duo Authentication Attempt from Untrusted Endpoint
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Duo' and unmapped.event_type = 'authentication' and status_detail contains ('endpoint_is_not_trusted') and status = 'success'

description: Detects the creation, modification, or deletion of an Azure Active Directory (Entra ID) integration within Cisco Duo. These actions may support legitimate identity synchronization or SSO setup, but could also indicate attempts to compromise the authentication pipeline such as injecting unauthorized users, modifying sync scopes to include risky groups, or disabling identity federation entirely.
name: Cisco Duo Azure Directory Integration Modification
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Duo' and unmapped.action in ('azure_directory_create', 'azure_directory_delete', 'azure_directory_update')

description: Detects when a bypass code is created in Cisco Duo. Bypass codes are temporary one-time passcodes typically issued by administrators to allow users to authenticate without their enrolled MFA device. While useful in emergencies, they can be abused by attackers or malicious insiders to bypass MFA protections.
name: Cisco Duo Bypass Code Created by Administrator
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Duo' and unmapped.action = 'bypass_create' and unmapped.eventtype = 'administrator'

description: Detects when a child customer account is created within Cisco Duo. This event typically occurs in managed service provider (MSP) or Duo MSP Partner Portal environments, where a parent organization manages multiple customer tenants. While this action may be part of normal MSP operations, it could also indicate unauthorized creation of a new Duo environment, resource sprawl or mismanagement, or potential abuse by a compromised administrator in a multi-tenant environment. Adversaries who gain access to high-privilege accounts may create child accounts to establish persistence, isolate malicious activity, or evade detection.
name: Cisco Duo Child Customer Account Creation
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Duo' and unmapped.action = 'create_child_customer'

description: Detects when an administrator downloads the device API script from the Duo management system. This script is used to interact with enrolled devices programmatically. While often part of legitimate device management setup (e.g., MDM integration), unscheduled downloads may indicate preparation for unauthorized automation or enumeration of managed endpoints.
name: Cisco Duo Device API Script Access
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Duo' and unmapped.action = 'management_system_download_device_api_script'

description: Detects the creation, update, or deletion of a group in Cisco Duo. Groups are used to assign authentication policies and application access to sets of users. Unauthorized modifications can weaken access restrictions, remove users from protected groups, or introduce excessive permissions.
name: Cisco Duo Group Configuration Modification
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Duo' and unmapped.action in ('group_create', 'group_delete', 'group_update')

description: Detects when a group policy is added to or removed from a Duo application integration. These actions control access and enforcement policies for specific user groups.
name: Cisco Duo Group Policy Modification
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Duo' and unmapped.action in ('integration_group_policy_add', 'integration_group_policy_remove')

description: Detects when a hardware token is created and assigned to a user in Cisco Duo. Hardware tokens provide an alternative to app-based push MFA, typically for users without smartphones or in high-security environments. While legitimate, attackers with admin access could register unauthorized tokens for persistence or MFA bypass.
name: Cisco Duo Hardware Token Assigned to User
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Duo' and unmapped.action = 'hardtoken_create' and unmapped.eventtype = 'administrator'

description: Detects when a secret key for a Duo application integration is viewed individually or in bulk. These keys are used for authenticating API-based integrations and should be protected as sensitive credentials. Viewing them may be legitimate during setup or troubleshooting, but can also signal preparation for credential misuse, unauthorized API access, or secret exfiltration. Access to these keys should be tightly controlled and monitored.
name: Cisco Duo Integration Secret Key Viewed
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Duo' and unmapped.action in ('integration_skey_bulk_view', 'integration_skey_view')

description: Detects the creation, deletion, or modification of LDAP directory synchronization connections in Cisco Duo. LDAP directory sync is used to manage users and groups from an external directory like Active Directory. Unauthorized changes to these settings could lead to unauthorized access provisioning, directory poisoning, or service disruption.
name: Cisco Duo LDAP Directory Connection Modified
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Duo' and unmapped.action in ('ldap_directory_conn_create', 'ldap_directory_conn_delete', 'ldap_directory_conn_modify')

description: Detects when a user explicitly marks a Duo Push authentication request as fraudulent. This usually indicates that someone attempted to log in using the user’s credentials without their consent. The user flagged the MFA prompt via the Duo Mobile app as a security concern. This event may be evidence of a compromised password, phishing attack, or credential stuffing attempt. Immediate investigation is strongly recommended.
name: Cisco Duo MFA Attempt Flagged as Fraudulent
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Duo' and unmapped.event_type = 'authentication' and status = 'fraud'

description: Detects when a successful multi-factor authentication (MFA) occurs using a Duo bypass code. Bypass codes are temporary passcodes generated by administrators for specific users, typically used as backup when enrolled devices are unavailable (e.g., lost device, no signal, travel constraints). While legitimate in many scenarios, unexpected use of bypass codes may indicate compromised credentials, social engineering, or insider threat activity.
name: Cisco Duo MFA Login via Bypass Code
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Duo' and unmapped.event_type = 'authentication' and unmapped.factor = 'bypass_code' and status_detail = 'valid_passcode'

description: Detects when a security policy is deleted in Cisco Duo. Policies govern access controls such as MFA requirements, allowed devices or networks, and application-specific settings. The policy_delete action in Cisco Duo logs indicates that an MFA or access policy has been removed. This is a high-risk action—deleting a policy can weaken or entirely remove security enforcement, potentially allowing unauthorized access to protected applications or users. Adversaries with administrative access may delete security policies to disable multi-factor authentication or bypass access controls.
name: Cisco Duo MFA Policy Deletion
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Duo' and unmapped.action = 'policy_delete'

description: Detects when a Cisco Duo administrator updates the single sign-on (SSO) certificate. This certificate is used to validate authentication assertions from identity providers (IdPs) like Azure AD, Okta, or ADFS. Updating the SSO certificate changes the trust relationship between Duo and identity providers, which may impact user authentication across applications. While this may be a routine maintenance task, adversaries with administrative access may abuse it to redirect authentication flows, intercept credentials, or disrupt legitimate access.
name: Cisco Duo SSO Certificate Update by Administrator
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Duo' and unmapped.action = 'admin_single_sign_on_cert_update'

description: Detects a successful authentication to Cisco Duo originating from an anonymous IP address, such as those associated with VPNs, proxy services, or anonymizing networks like Tor. Adversaries may use anonymous IPs to conceal their true location, evade geographic or network-based access controls, and avoid detection. Successful logins from such IP addresses may indicate credential misuse, session hijacking, or other unauthorized access attempts.
name: Cisco Duo Successful Login from Anonymous IP Address
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Duo' and unmapped.event_type = 'authentication' and status_detail contains ('anonymous_ip') and status = 'success'

description: Detects when a U2F (Universal 2nd Factor) hardware token is deleted from a user account in Cisco Duo. U2F tokens are physical devices used to perform strong multi-factor authentication. The removal of such tokens may reduce account security and increase the risk of unauthorized access. Adversaries with administrative access may delete registered U2F tokens to weaken authentication protections, disable secure access methods, or replace them with attacker-controlled credentials.
name: Cisco Duo U2F Token Deletion
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Duo' and unmapped.action = 'u2ftoken_delete'

description: Detects when a user is deleted from Cisco Duo. This action could be routine (e.g., user offboarding), but may also indicate malicious activity if performed unexpectedly or by an unauthorized administrator. Deleting a user removes their MFA protection, which could be used to evade access controls or cover tracks.
name: Cisco Duo User Account Deletion
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Duo' and unmapped.action = 'user_delete'

description: Detects when a WebAuthn (FIDO2) credential is deleted from a user account in Cisco Duo. WebAuthn credentials are commonly used for passwordless or hardware token–based multi-factor authentication (MFA), providing strong, phishing-resistant security. Deleting these credentials can reduce the security posture of the user account and may indicate an adversary attempting to weaken authentication mechanisms or disrupt legitimate access.
name: Cisco Duo WebAuthn Credential Deletion
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Duo' and unmapped.action = 'webauthncredential_delete'

description: Detects allowed network connections identified by Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FTD) that are associated with remote administration or remote desktop protocols. Adversaries may abuse remote access tools and protocols to gain interactive access, perform lateral movement, or maintain persistence within the environment. Monitoring allowed remote admin or remote desktop activity helps identify potentially unauthorized remote access and misuse of legitimate management channels.
name: Cisco FTD Allowed Remote Access Software Detected
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'Open' and status = 'Allow' and ((unmapped.Client contains ('remote admin', 'remote desktop')) or (unmapped.ApplicationProtocol contains ('remote admin', 'remote desktop')))

description: Detects file download events through Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FTD) involving executable file types such as scripts, binaries, shortcut files, and installer formats. Adversaries frequently use these file types to deliver malware, droppers, or secondary payloads through web downloads, or file-sharing services. Excluding known trusted sources such as Microsoft Update helps reduce false positives. Monitoring suspicious executable downloads can assist in identifying initial access attempts, malware delivery, or user-driven compromise activity.
name: Cisco FTD Binary File Type Download
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'File events' and unmapped.FileDirection = 'Download' and unmapped.FileType in:anycase ('ISHIELD_MSI', 'BINHEX', 'BINARY_DATA', 'ELF', 'MACHO', 'JARPACK', 'TORRENT', 'AUTORUN', 'EICAR', 'SCR', 'UNIX_SCRIPT') and not (unmapped.WebApplication in ('Microsoft', 'Microsoft Update')) and not (unmapped.SHA_Disposition in:anycase ('Clean'))

description: Detects outbound connections identified by Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FTD) to known public file hosting, pastebin, temporary storage, and content delivery services. Adversaries commonly abuse these platforms to host malware payloads, stage tools, exfiltrate data, or retrieve command-and-control content while blending in with legitimate web traffic. Monitoring access to such services can help identify potential malware delivery, data leakage, or post-compromise activity.
name: Cisco FTD File Sharing Domain Accessed
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'Open' and url.url_string contains ('objects.githubusercontent.com', 'anonfiles.com', 'cdn.discordapp.com', 'ddns.net', 'dl.dropboxusercontent.com', 'ghostbin.co', 'glitch.me', 'gofile.io', 'hastebin.com', 'mediafire.com', 'mega.nz', 'onrender.com', 'pages.dev', 'paste.ee', 'pastebin.com', 'pastebin.pl', 'pastetext.net', 'privatlab.com', 'privatlab.net', 'send.exploit.in', 'sendspace.com', 'storjshare.io', 'supabase.co', 'temp.sh', 'transfer.sh', 'trycloudflare.com', 'ufile.io', 'w3spaces.com', 'workers.dev')

subQueries.subQuery: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'Open' and status contains 'block'

description: Detects a high number of blocked network connection attempts reported by Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FTD). A surge in blocked connections may indicate reconnaissance, port scanning, brute-force attempts, or automated attack activity targeting the environment. Monitoring this behavior helps identify hostile sources and assess whether further defensive actions, such as blocking or rate-limiting the source, are required.
name: Cisco FTD High Volume Blocked Connection Attempts

subQueries.subQuery: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'Intrusion event' and unmapped.SID = * and not (status_details contains 'Attempted')

description: Detects a high frequency of intrusion events reported by Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FTD) within a defined time window. A large volume of intrusion alerts may indicate coordinated attack activity, automated exploitation attempts, scanning behavior, or sustained malicious traffic targeting the environment. This rule helps identify aggressive or persistent threats that warrant immediate investigation and response.
name: Cisco FTD High-Volume Intrusion Events Detected

description: Detects outbound TCP connections identified by Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FTD) to legacy and insecure service ports such as Telnet (23), POP3 (110), and IMAP (143). These protocols transmit data in cleartext and are commonly targeted or abused by adversaries for credential harvesting, unauthorized access, or legacy system exploitation. Monitoring their usage can help identify weak security configurations, potential credential exposure, or suspicious access to outdated services.
name: Cisco FTD Insecure Legacy Protocol Accessed
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'Open' and connection_info.protocol_name = 'tcp' and dst_endpoint.port in (143, 23, 110) and status contains 'Allow'

description: Detects attempts to exploit known client-side vulnerabilities as identified by Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FTD). Adversaries may target web browsers, email clients, or other client applications to execute malicious code on the endpoint. Monitoring for these events helps identify exploitation attempts that could lead to malware execution, data exfiltration, or initial foothold establishment within the network.
name: Cisco FTD Known Client Side Exploit Attempt Detected
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'Intrusion event' and status_detail = 'Known client side exploit attempt' and unmapped.InlineResult = 'Alert'

description: Detects network traffic identified by Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FTD) as communicating with known malware command and control (C2) servers. This activity may indicate a compromised host attempting to establish or maintain contact with an attacker-controlled infrastructure. Monitoring such alerts helps in early detection of malware infections, containment of threats, and prevention of data exfiltration or further malicious actions within the network.
name: Cisco FTD Known Malware C2 Traffic
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'Intrusion event' and status_detail = 'Known malware command and control traffic'

description: Detects attempts to exfiltrate sensitive information from the network as identified by Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FTD). Large-scale information leak activity may involve repeated access to confidential data, emails, or files over various protocols. Monitoring for these patterns helps identify potential data breaches, insider threats, or automated exfiltration campaigns targeting critical assets.
name: Cisco FTD Large Scale Information Leak
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'Intrusion event' and status_detail = 'Large Scale Information Leak'

description: Detects intrusion events generated by Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FTD) that match a set of intrusion signature IDs associated with Lumma Stealer malware activity. Lumma Stealer is a credential-harvesting and information-stealing malware commonly used to exfiltrate browser data, credentials, cookies, cryptocurrency wallets, and system information. Monitoring detections of these signatures can help identify infected hosts and active data theft operations within the network.
name: Cisco FTD Lumma Stealer Activity
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'Intrusion event' and unmapped.SID in (64797, 64798, 64799, 64800, 64801)

description: Detects the identification of a malicious file by Cisco Secure Firepower Threat Defense (FTD). This may indicate the presence of malware or potentially unwanted software within the network. Monitoring these detections helps identify compromised hosts, prevent further infection, and maintain the security posture of the environment.
name: Cisco FTD Malicious File Detected
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'File events' and unmapped.FileDirection = 'Download' and unmapped.SHA_Disposition = 'MALWARE'

description: Detects intrusion events where Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FTD) identifies a known malicious file or file-based exploit. Such activity may indicate attempts to compromise endpoints or servers using malware, trojans, or exploit kits. Monitoring these events helps in early detection of malicious payloads and prevents potential breaches or system compromise.
name: Cisco FTD Malicious File or Exploit Detected
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'Intrusion event' and status_detail = 'Known malicious file or file based exploit' and unmapped.InlineResult = 'Alert'

subQueries.subQuery: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'File events' and unmapped.FileDirection = 'Download' and unmapped.SHA_Disposition = 'MALWARE'

description: Detects multiple file download events identified as malware by Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FTD). A high volume of malicious file downloads within a short period may indicate active malware delivery, drive-by downloads, or automated payload retrieval by compromised hosts. Adversaries commonly use repeated download attempts to ensure successful infection or to retrieve additional tools and updates. Monitoring and correlating repeated malicious download activity can help identify infected systems and prevent further compromise or lateral movement.
name: Cisco FTD Multiple Malicious File Downloads

subQueries.subQuery: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'Intrusion event' and status_detail in ('Attempted Administrator Privilege Gain', 'Attempted User Privilege Gain')

description: Detects multiple intrusion events reported by Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FTD) indicating attempted privilege escalation activity. Adversaries may attempt to exploit vulnerabilities or misconfigurations to gain elevated privileges, enabling deeper system access, lateral movement, or execution of privileged operations. Repeated privilege escalation attempts can be a strong indicator of active exploitation or post-compromise behavior and warrant immediate investigation to prevent further impact.
name: Cisco FTD Multiple Privilege Escalation Attempts Detected

description: Detects multiple suspicious file download activity identified by Cisco Secure Firepower Threat Defense (FTD), where executable or archive file types are downloaded over common network protocols such as HTTP, SMB, FTP, or SMTP. The rule triggers when five or more such download events are observed from the same source, excluding known legitimate Microsoft update traffic. This behavior may indicate malware delivery, staged payload transfer, or preparation for lateral movement.
name: Cisco FTD Multiple Suspicious File Downloads

subQueries.subQuery: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'File events' and unmapped.ApplicationProtocol contains ('HTTP', 'SMB', 'FTP', 'SMTP') and unmapped.FileDirection = 'Upload' and unmapped.FileType in ('MSEXE', 'LNK', 'RAR', 'ZIP', 'PDF', 'DOCX', 'REG') and not (unmapped.WebApplication in ('Microsoft', 'Microsoft Update'))

description: Detects multiple attempts to upload potentially malicious or suspicious files through Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FTD). Frequent uploads of unusual file types or files may indicate data exfiltration attempts, malware deployment, or misuse of network resources. Monitoring such patterns helps in identifying potential insider threats or automated malicious activity targeting the organization's network.
name: Cisco FTD Multiple Suspicious File Upload Activity

description: Detects network scanning activity identified by Cisco Secure Firepower Threat Defense (FTD) devices. Network scans may indicate reconnaissance efforts by attackers attempting to discover active hosts, open ports, or vulnerabilities within the network. Monitoring these alerts helps identify potential threats early and supports proactive network defense.
name: Cisco FTD Network Scan Detected
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'Intrusion event' and status_detail = 'Detection of a Network Scan' and unmapped.InlineResult = 'Alert'

description: Detects network traffic identified by Cisco Secure Firepower Threat Defense (FTD) as indicative of a trojan infection. This may signal the presence of malware attempting to establish command and control, exfiltrate data, or perform other malicious activities. Monitoring these detections helps identify compromised hosts, prevent lateral movement, and mitigate potential threats within the network environment.
name: Cisco FTD Network Trojan Detected
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'Intrusion event' and status_detail = 'A Network Trojan was Detected' and unmapped.InlineResult = 'Alert'

description: Detects allowed outbound LDAP or LDAPS connections from internal hosts to external IP addresses using Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FTD) telemetry. LDAP (port 389) and LDAPS (port 636) are typically used for directory services within trusted internal networks. Adversaries may abuse outbound LDAP connections to enumerate directory information, perform credential harvesting, or communicate with malicious directory services outside the organization. Legitimate use of LDAP over the internet is uncommon, making such activity a strong indicator of potential misconfiguration, policy violation, or malicious reconnaissance and should be investigated promptly.
name: Cisco FTD Outbound LDAP Connection to External Network
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'Open' and status contains 'Allow' (dst_endpoint.port = 389 or dst_endpoint.port = 636 or unmapped.ApplicationProtocol = 'LDAP') and not(dst_endpoint.ip matches '^(?:10\\.|192\\.168\\.|172\\.(?:1[6-9]|2\\d|3[0-1])\\.)')

description: Detects outbound SMB network connections identified by Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FTD) originating from internal private IP addresses and communicating with external, non-private destinations. SMB traffic over ports 139 or 445 is typically intended for internal network communication and is rarely required over the internet. Adversaries may exploit outbound SMB connections for lateral movement, data exfiltration, remote command execution, or interaction with malicious infrastructure. Monitoring outbound SMB or NetBIOS activity to external networks can help identify misconfigurations, policy violations, or malicious activity indicative of compromise.
name: Cisco FTD Outbound SMB Connection to External Network

description: Detects potential exploitation attempts targeting the Cisco Secure Firewall ASA and Firepower Threat Defense (FTD) WebVPN component. CVE-2025-20362 is a missing-authorization path-traversal flaw that allows unauthenticated access to restricted WebVPN endpoints via path normalization sequences. CVE-2025-20333 is a heap buffer overflow in the WebVPN Lua file-action handler that yields root-level remote code execution when triggered. Chained together, the two CVEs allow an unauthenticated remote attacker to achieve full device compromise.
name: Cisco FTD Potential WebVPN Exploitation Attempt
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and ((event.type='Intrusion event' and unmapped.SID in (65340, 46897)) or (event.type = 'Open' and unmapped.URL contains ('+CSCOU+/../+CSCOE+', '+CSCOU+//../+CSCOE+', '+CSCOU+%2f..%2f+CSCOE+', '+CSCOU+/%2e%2e/+CSCOE+', '+CSCOU+/..%2f+CSCOE+')) or (unmapped.URL contains ('/+CSCOE+/files/file_action.html', '/+CSCOE+/files/file_list.json') and unmapped.URL contains ('mode=upload', 'mode=delete', 'mode=rename')))

subQueries.subQuery: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'Intrusion event' and status_detail = 'Attempted Information Leak'

description: Detects multiple attempts to exfiltrate sensitive information or data through the network, as identified by Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FTD). Repeated information leak attempts may indicate reconnaissance, automated data exfiltration, or insider threat activity. Monitoring for such patterns helps identify potential breaches and prevent unauthorized disclosure of critical information.
name: Cisco FTD Repeated Attempts of Information Leak

description: Detects a successful privilege escalation activity identified by Cisco Secure Firepower Threat Defense (FTD) where an account has gained elevated privileges (administrator or user). Such activity may indicate that an adversary has successfully exploited a vulnerability, misconfiguration, or credential compromise to obtain higher-level access, enabling lateral movement, system modifications, or execution of sensitive operations. Monitoring these events helps identify confirmed compromises and potential insider threats.
name: Cisco FTD Successful Privilege Escalation
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'Intrusion event' and status_detail in ('Successful Administrator Privilege Gain', 'Successful User Privilege Gain')

description: Detects network connections initiated by the Background Intelligent Transfer Service (BITS) as identified by Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FTD). Adversaries may abuse BITS to stealthily download or upload malicious payloads, evade detection, and maintain persistence by leveraging this trusted Windows service. BITS traffic can indicate suspicious file transfer activity associated with malware delivery or command-and-control communications.
name: Cisco FTD Suspicious BITS Network Activity
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'Open' and unmapped.Client contains 'BITS' and not (url.url_string contains '//msedge.b.tlu.dl') and not (unmapped.WebApplication in ('Microsoft Update', 'Microsoft'))

description: Detects file download activity identified by Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FTD) where high-risk or executable file types are transferred over non-standard ports. Adversaries may leverage uncommon ports to evade network security controls and deliver malicious payloads such as executables, scripts, installers, or shortcut files. Monitoring this behavior can help identify malware delivery, command-and-control staging, or covert data transfer attempts.
name: Cisco FTD Suspicious File Download Over Non-Standard Port
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'File events' and unmapped.FileDirection = 'Download' and not (dst_endpoint.port in (443, 80, 445)) and unmapped.FileType in:anycase ('MSEXE', 'LNK', 'ISHIELD_MSI', 'BINHEX', 'BINARY_DATA', 'ELF', 'MACHO', 'JARPACK', 'TORRENT', 'AUTORUN', 'EICAR', 'SCR', 'UNIX_SCRIPT') and unmapped.FileSandboxStatus != 'File Size Is Too Small' and not (unmapped.WebApplication in:anycase ('Microsoft', 'Microsoft Update')) and src_endpoint.port != 445

description: Detects network connections identified as TOR traffic by Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FTD). The Tor network is commonly used to anonymize network activity and evade monitoring controls. Adversaries may leverage Tor to conceal command-and-control communications, exfiltrate data, or bypass network security restrictions. Monitoring Tor connections can indicate policy violations, anonymization attempts, or malicious activity and should be investigated to determine intent and risk.
name: Cisco FTD TOR Network Connection Detected
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'Open' and status contains 'Allow' and (unmapped.Client = 'TOR' or unmapped.ApplicationProtocol = 'TOR')

description: Detects the deletion of Windows event logs using wevtutil.exe. An adversary can abuse this command-line utility to erase critical system and security logs, effectively eliminating evidence of their activities and hindering detection efforts. By clearing event logs, attackers can disrupt incident response, impede forensic analysis, and make it more difficult for defenders to trace malicious actions. This technique is often used in conjunction with other defense evasion strategies to maintain a low profile and avoid detection during or after an attack.
name: Clearing Windows Event Logs via Wevtutil
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Behavioral Indicators' and indicator.name = 'EventViewerTampering' and (src.process.name contains 'wevtutil' or src.process.displayName = 'Eventing Command Line Utility') and src.process.cmdline matches '(cl|clear-log)\\s+(system|application|security)' and (src.process.parent.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe', 'cmd.exe') or src.process.parent.displayName in ('Windows Command Processor', 'Windows PowerShell'))

description: Detects suspicious use of the dmesg utility to clear the kernel ring buffer, which stores diagnostic messages about the system kernel. Adversaries can abuse this action to erase evidence of malicious activity, such as the installation of a Linux Kernel Module (LKM). By clearing the kernel ring buffer, attackers attempt to evade detection by removing traces of unauthorized modifications or kernel-level changes that may have been logged.
name: Clear Kernel Ring Buffer via Dmsg Utility
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and tgt.process.name in:anycase ('dmesg') and tgt.process.cmdline matches 'dmesg.+(\\-C|\\-c|\\--clear|\\--read\\-clear)(\\s+|$)' and not (src.process.cmdline contains ('/shColetaDadosBRT', '/usr/local/bin/logdmesg.sh','/dmesg-logger.sh')) and not (src.process.parent.cmdline contains ('/usr/flexfs/'))

description: Detects the execution of the wevtutil command to clear the Microsoft-Windows-TerminalServices-RDPClient/Operational event log on Windows endpoints. This activity typically uses command-line utilities such as powershell.exe or cmd.exe targeting the RDP client operational log. Adversaries may clear RDP logs to erase evidence of remote access or lateral movement. The rule triggers when process creation or command script events contain wevtutil with the cl flag and specify the RDP client log. This behavior is suspicious and may indicate attempts to cover tracks following unauthorized RDP activity.
name: Clear Windows RDP Event Log via Wevtutil Command Execution
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type in ('Process Creation', 'Command Script') and (src.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe', 'cmd.exe') or src.process.displayName in:anycase ('Windows PowerShell', 'Windows Command Processor')) and (src.process.cmdline contains ('wevtutil ', 'EventLogSession') or cmdScript.content contains ('wevtutil ', 'EventLogSession')) and (src.process.cmdline contains ('cl ', 'GlobalSession.ClearLog') or cmdScript.content contains ('cl ', 'GlobalSession.ClearLog')) and (src.process.cmdline contains 'Microsoft-Windows-TerminalServices-RDPClient/Operational' or cmdScript.content contains 'Microsoft-Windows-TerminalServices-RDPClient/Operational')

description: Detects PowerShell command script content consistent with ClickFix social engineering attacks. ClickFix campaigns lure users into pasting malicious commands from fake CAPTCHA or verification pages, resulting in download cradles that retrieve and execute secondary payloads from attacker-controlled infrastructure.
name: ClickFix Command Script Activity

description: Detects activity consistent with ClickFix social engineering attacks where a user is lured into pasting a malicious command from a fake CAPTCHA or verification page into a system dialog. ClickFix campaigns typically result in the execution of PowerShell download cradles that retrieve and run secondary payloads.
name: ClickFix Payload Execution

description: Detects event patterns consistent with PowerShell content being obfuscated through bitwise XOR operations. This behavior is security-relevant because it is commonly used to conceal malicious logic and payload material from content inspection and static detections. Such obfuscation can hinder triage and delay containment by reducing the fidelity of telemetry and analyst visibility. Adversaries leverage XOR-based obfuscation to evade defensive controls during execution and staging.
name: ClickFix PowerShell BXOR String Obfuscation Activity
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Command Script' and (src.process.name contains ('powershell.exe','pwsh.exe') or src.process.displayName contains ('Windows PowerShell','pwsh')) and ((src.process.cmdline matches '(?i)\\[convert\\]::toint32\\(.*?,\\s*16\\)+\\s*-bxor.*?\\[int\\]\\[char\\]' and src.process.cmdline matches '[a-z0-9]{200,}') or (cmdScript.content matches '(?i)\\[convert\\]::toint32\\(.*?,\\s*16\\)+\\s*-bxor.*?\\[int\\]\\[char\\]' and cmdScript.content matches '[a-z0-9]{200,}')) and not (src.process.integrityLevel='SYSTEM')

description: Detects suspicious process activity associated with ClickOnce application abuse. Adversaries may leverage ClickOnce deployment technology to execute arbitrary commands on target systems, bypassing security controls and achieving code execution through trusted Microsoft components.
name: ClickOnce Cmd Shell

description: Detects DNS activity originating from ClickOnce application directory, where the resolved domain belongs to a list of TLDs associated with malicious activity. This pattern can indicate initial access or payload retrieval where the application deployment flow is used to reach attacker-controlled infrastructure. Monitoring this behavior helps surface attempts to stage malicious content or establish external connectivity from a trusted execution context. Adversaries commonly abuse application deployment mechanisms to blend command-and-control or delivery traffic into normal network resolution activity.
name: ClickOnce Suspicious DNS Activity
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'DNS Resolved' and ((src.process.image.path contains '\\AppData\\Local\\Apps\\2.0\\' and src.process.image.path contains '.exe') or (src.process.parent.image.path contains '\\AppData\\Local\\Apps\\2.0\\' and src.process.parent.image.path contains '.exe')) and event.dns.request matches ('\\.xyz$', '\\.top$', '\\.sh$', '\\.ru$', '\\.su$', '\\.icu$', '\\.pw$', '\\.ga$', '\\.club$', '\\.click$', '\\.rest$', '\\.shop$', '\\.fun$', '\\.trycloudflare\\.com$') and not (event.dns.request contains ('api.keygen.sh','www.deepmarket.top'))

description: Detects command-line processes creating DLL files within user-writable data directories. This behavior is indicative of payload staging or DLL sideloading, enabling execution, persistence, or defense evasion outside hardened system locations. This activity is typically associated with info stealers and trojans. While legitimate installers, updaters, or developer tools may write DLLs in these areas, such events warrant scrutiny when coupled with unusual parent processes or atypical process lineage.
name: CLI-Initiated DLL Creation in User-Writable Directories
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'File Creation' and (src.process.parent.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe', 'cmd.exe', 'mshta.exe', 'cscript.exe', 'wscript.exe', 'rundll32.exe') or src.process.parent.displayName in ('Windows PowerShell', 'Windows Command Processor', 'Microsoft (R) HTML Application host', 'Microsoft ® Console Based Script Host', 'Microsoft ® Windows Based Script Host', 'Windows host process (Rundll32)')) and (src.process.name in:anycase ('cmd.exe', 'powershell.exe') or src.process.displayName in ('Windows Command Processor', 'Windows PowerShell')) and tgt.file.path matches '[A-Za-z]:\\\\(ProgramData|.*\\\\AppData\\\\LocalLow)\\\\[^\\\\]*\\.dll'

description: Detects the use of common download utilities such as 'curl' or 'wget' to retrieve the Cloudflared binary directly from GitHub repositories. This activity may indicate initial access or lateral movement attempts by adversaries seeking to establish covert tunnels or bypass network controls. Threat actors often leverage legitimate tools and public repositories to blend malicious actions with normal administrative behavior. Monitoring for direct downloads of tunneling tools like Cloudflared is critical for identifying potential command and control setup or data exfiltration channels.
name: Cloudflared GitHub Download via Command Line Utilities
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os in ('windows', 'osx', 'linux') and event.type = 'IP Connect' and src.process.cmdline contains 'github.com' and src.process.cmdline contains 'cloudflared' and src.process.cmdline matches '[\\s"\\.]*\\b(Net\\.WebClient|curl|invoke-webrequest|iwr|DownloadString|DownloadFile|wget)\\b[\\s"\\.]*'

description: Detects the establishment of a persistent Cloudflared tunnel connection, which may indicate the use of tunneling to bypass network controls or enable unauthorized remote access. Adversaries can leverage Cloudflared tunnels to maintain covert communication channels, exfiltrate data, or evade perimeter defenses. This activity is significant as it can facilitate lateral movement and persistence within the environment. Monitoring for unexpected or unauthorized Cloudflared tunnel activity is critical for identifying potential abuse of remote access mechanisms.
name: Cloudflared Persistent Tunnel Establishment Detected
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os in ('windows', 'osx', 'linux') and event.type = 'IP Connect' and src.process.name contains 'cloudflared' and src.process.cmdline contains 'tunnel' and src.process.cmdline contains 'run' and src.process.cmdline contains '--token'

description: Detects the establishment of temporary tunnels using the Cloudflared service. This activity may indicate attempts to create unauthorized external access paths or bypass network security controls. Adversaries can leverage temporary tunnels to exfiltrate data, maintain remote access, or circumvent monitoring solutions. Monitoring for such connections helps identify potential misuse of tunneling services within the environment.
name: Cloudflared Temporary Tunnel Establishment Activity
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os in ('windows', 'osx', 'linux') and event.type = 'IP Connect' and src.process.name contains 'cloudflared' and src.process.cmdline contains 'tunnel' and src.process.cmdline contains '--url'

description: Detects the execution of cloud utility commands related to Identity and Access Management (IAM), such as creating or modifying roles, users, policies, or access keys. Attackers may exploit cloud IAM commands to escalate privileges, establish persistence, or gain unauthorized access to cloud resources. Monitoring IAM command execution can help identify potential account compromise or privilege escalation attempts.
name: Cloud Utility IAM Command Execution
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'osx' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (tgt.process.image.path matches 'bin/aws$' or tgt.process.image.path matches 'bin/gcloud$') and tgt.process.cmdline contains ' iam '

description: Detects the creation of an unsigned process initiated by a component commonly associated with Universal Plug and Play (UPnP) management functionality. Adversaries may abuse this behavior to execute arbitrary code within a trusted context, potentially bypassing security controls and gaining elevated privileges. By leveraging legitimate system components to launch unsigned or untrusted executables, attackers can evade detection mechanisms that rely on process lineage or code signing validation. This technique can be used to establish persistence, move laterally within a network, or execute malicious payloads while appearing to originate from a trusted source. The execution of unsigned processes in this manner poses significant security risks, as it may allow threat actors to operate with higher privileges and reduce the likelihood of their activities being flagged by traditional endpoint protection solutions.
name: Code execution via IUPnPContainerManager
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation'  and src.process.name = 'upnpcont.exe' and tgt.process.signedStatus = 'unsigned'

description: Detects a chain of commands that subsequently launches the VBScript engine. This chain is frequently abused to pivot from a benign-looking file to script-based code execution, aiding payload staging, execution, or persistence while blending with interactive activity. In some environments this may occur during legitimate administrative maintenance when users manually run internal scripts, but it is atypical for standard user workflows.
name: Command Chain Spawning VBScript Execution
