s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.parent.name in:anycase ('explorer.exe') or src.process.parent.displayName = 'Windows Explorer') and (src.process.name in:anycase ('cmd.exe', 'cscript.exe', 'wscript.exe') or src.process.displayName in ('Windows Command Processor', 'Microsoft ® Console Based Script Host', 'Windows Based Script Host')) and src.process.cmdline contains 'start' and src.process.cmdline contains 'exit' and src.process.cmdline contains ('.vbs', '.vbe', 'VBScript.Encode') and src.process.cmdline contains ('&', 'appdata', 'roaming', 'temp', 'attrib', 'xcopy')

description: Detects the execution of shell commands inside a container by making direct connections to the Kubernetes Kubelet API using the curl utility. This activity may indicate an attempt to exploit the Kubelet API for remote command execution, container escape, or lateral movement within the cluster. Adversaries often leverage exposed or misconfigured Kubelet endpoints to run arbitrary commands inside containers, potentially leading to privilege escalation or persistent access. Monitoring for curl-based interactions with the Kubelet API that attempt to execute shell commands helps identify unauthorized access and mitigates the risk of cluster compromise.
name: Command Execution in Container via Curl

description: Detects command-line activity indicative of a file transfer client performing outbound uploads to remote destinations. This behavior is relevant for security monitoring because adversaries commonly leverage CLI-based transfer utilities to move data off host systems for exfiltration or staging. Similar patterns may appear during sanctioned administration or backup workflows in some environments.
name: Command-Line File Upload Activity via Cyberduck Client
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and src.process.name in:anycase ('cyberduck.exe', 'cyberduck', 'cyberduck-cli.exe', 'cyberduck-cli', 'duck.exe', 'duck') and src.process.cmdline contains ('--upload', '--synchronize')

description: Detects obfuscated command execution where adversaries insert quotes into command names to evade detection. This technique is combined with network or download activity to identify malicious intent. Commonly observed in malware droppers and C2 communications.
name: Command Line Obfuscation via Quote Manipulation
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.name in:anycase ('cmd.exe', 'powershell.exe', 'pwsh.exe', 'curl.exe') and tgt.process.cmdline matches ('(c|cm|cmd|cu|cur|curl|po|pow|powe|power|powershell|pw|pws|pwsh)""[a-z]') and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('http', 'ftp', 'download', 'invoke', 'iex', 'webclient', 'bypass', 'hidden', 'encoded')

description: Detects the execution of the Windows Command Shell (cmd.exe) or PowerShell (powershell.exe) by runnerw.exe, a legitimate process associated with the JetBrains IntelliJ IDEA development environment. The runnerw.exe binary is used to execute applications and build scripts from within the IDE. While there are legitimate development and testing scenarios where this parent-child process relationship may occur, it is also a known method of Living Off the Land (LOLBIN) abuse. An adversary can leverage this trusted developer utility to proxy the execution of arbitrary commands or scripts, potentially bypassing application control and evading defenses that trust the parent runnerw.exe process.
name: Command Shell Spawned by IntelliJ Runner
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('runnerw.exe') or src.process.image.originalFileName in:anycase ('runnerw.exe')) and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe', 'cmd.exe') or tgt.process.displayName in ('Windows PowerShell', 'Windows Command Processor'))

description: Detects the presence compromised Glassworm VSIX extension packages. Adversaries can abuse these compromised extensions to deliver stealthy, persistent backdoors that evade code review by embedding invisible Unicode or Private Use Area characters to conceal malicious payloads. Once active, the malware can establish resilient command-and-control channels using decentralized or legitimate services to retrieve payloads or receive instructions, making takedown and conventional blocking more difficult. Glassworm's worm-like capabilities enable it to harvest repository and package manager credentials and misuse them to propagate by compromising additional packages and extensions, turning each victim into a new distribution point.
name: Compromised VSIX Glassworm Package Detected
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'File Rename' and tgt.file.path contains ('codejoy.codejoy-vscode-extension-1.8.3','codejoy.codejoy-vscode-extension-1.8.4','l-igh-t.vscode-theme-seti-folder-1.2.3','kleinesfilmroellchen.serenity-dsl-syntaxhighlight-0.3.2','JScearcy.rust-doc-viewer-4.2.1','SIRILMP.dark-theme-sm-3.11.4','CodeInKlingon.git-worktree-menu-1.0.91','ginfuru.better-nunjucks-0.3.2','ellacrity.recoil-0.7.4','grrrck.positron-plus-1-e-0.0.71','jeronimoekerdt.color-picker-universal-2.8.91','srcery-colors.srcery-colors-0.3.9','sissel.shopify-liquid-4.0.1','TretinV3.forts-api-extention-0.3.1','cline-ai-main.cline-ai-agent-3.1.3','spacetoow.vsc-python-indent-1.21.3','floktokbok.autoimport-smart-tool-1.5.8','flape-osx.align-code-format-tool-1.4.6','dev-tm.code-python-indent-helper-1.21.3','gorth-tm.your-project-manager-organizer-13.0.4','tool-studio.prettier-pro-code-format-11.0.3','specstudio.code-wakatime-activity-tracker-25.5.9','croct-studio.antigravity-model-usage-dashboard-2.1.35','calow-dex.prisma-database-schema-tools-7.0.4','sxatvo-tm.compile-runnner-build-1.0.89','codbro-dxp.explorer-xml-xquery-1.0.3','sweaty-tstudio.quarto-report-studio-1.127.0','codevunm-tm.cluster-kuberntes-manager-1.0.6','awwwadem.language-professional-tools-1.20.24','namop-dex.claude-code-assistant-2.1.46','marcus-tm.ruby-intelligence-toolkit-0.10.1','yamal-dext.wonder-workspace-icons-0.10.3','msw-tm.component-vetur-toolkit-0.37.3','silvia68.console-log-generator-1.0.1','shinypy.pycode-formatter-2025.2.0','anessaeah.php-intelephense-language-support-1.16.5','jupstudio.dotenv-tools-dev-1.0.1','pessa07tm.my-js-ts-auto-commands-1.0.4','oigotm.my-command-palette-extension-0.0.4','myexttool.my-command-palette-extension-0.0.4','projmanager.your-project-manager-extension-13.0.4','intellipro.extension-json-intelligence-1.0.5','turbobase.sql-turbo-tool-0.28.9','prismapp.prisma-vs-code-extension-7.0.3','twilkbilk.color-highlight-css-2.8.2','gvotcha.claude-code-extension-2.1.46','crotoapp.vscode-xml-extension-1.0.3','kharizma.vscode-extension-wakatime-25.5.7','tamokill12.foundry-pdf-extension-1.9.9','craz2team.vscode-todo-extension-1.0.8','sxatvo.jinja-extension-0.2.2','codbroks.compile-runnner-extension-1.0.88','otoboss.autoimport-extension-1.5.7','tokcodes.import-cost-extension-3.3.3','ssgwysc.volar-vscode-3.1.9','codevunmis.csv-sql-tsv-rainbow-3.3.3','awesomeco.wonder-for-vscode-icons-0.10.3','exss-studio.yaml-professional-extension-1.20.24','namopins.prettier-pro-vscode-extension-11.0.3','bhbpbarn.vsce-python-indent-extension-1.21.2','yamaprolas.revature-labs-extension-1.0.20','mswincx.antigravity-cockpit-extension-2.1.34','kotpot.modern-css-toolkit-0.14.28','clotomoto.code-way-editor-0.19.4','daeumer-web.align-format-tool-1.4.5','quickrunn.auto-run-command-quick-1.6.1','cod-vok.arko-dev-devsecops-1.0.3','catspace-studio.ng-angular-language-21.1.0','potstok.dotnet-runtime-extension-3.0.2','gvotcha.claude-code-extensions-2.1.45','flutxvs.vscode-kuberntes-extension-1.0.2','federicanc.dotenv-syntax-highlighting-1.0.3','pyflowpyr.py-flowpyright-extension-1.1.329','blockstoks.easily-gitignore-manage-0.10.2','thing-mn.your-flow-extension-for-icons-1.3.2','mswincx.antigravity-cockpit-2.1.31','federicanc.envglow-syntax-highlighting-1.0.1','kwinsolin.better-cpp-tool-1.27.1','dopbop-studio.vscode-tailwindcss-extension-toolkit-0.14.29','kwitch-studio.auto-run-command-extension-1.6.2','aligntool.extension-align-professional-tool-1.4.6','runnerpost.runner-your-code-0.13.2','rubyideext.ruby-ide-extension-0.10.0-alpha.2','aadarkcode.one-dark-material-3.20.1','pyscopexte.pyscope-extension-1.1.403','baksmink.vscode-quokka-extension-1.0.755','ovixcode.vscode-better-comments-3.0.2','pubruncode.ccoderunner-9.4.8','codwayexten.code-way-extension-0.19.5','toowespace.worksets-extension-0.2.41','treedotree.tree-do-todoextension-0.0.216','shinypy.shiny-extension-for-vscode-1.3.3','markvalid.vscode-mdvalidator-extension-0.61.2','errlenscre.error-lens-finder-ex-3.28.1','coneditorfig.config-editor-extension-0.17.5','tamokill12.pdf-extension-1.2.3','daeumer-web.es-linter-for-vs-code-3.0.21','trovizno.arko-extension-1.0.3','ll-service-vm-studio.vscode-clangd-cross-platform-0.3.21','devmidu-studio.svg-better-extension-0.4.41','redcapcollective.vscode-quarkus-elite-suite-1.21.2025112809','tima-web-wang.shell-check-utils-0.38.61','jeronimo-self-dev.smart-color-picker-2.8.92','crazy-ndkilddmn.vim-smart-tool-1.32.2','ilvvilab.php-composr-tool-extension-1.63.18165','juptool.jupyter-pro-tool-extension-2025.9.1','cliagenball.cline-agent-extension-3.38.1','kwinsolin.act-extension-0.5.16','daeumer-web.style-align-extension-1.3.9','icepower1997.glacier-cave-theme-1.0.0','brategmaqendaalar-studio.pro-prettyxml-formatter-6.4.2','mecreation-studio.pyrefly-pro-extension-0.48.1','reditorsupporter.r-vscode-2.8.8','dark-code-studio.flutter-extension-3.122.1','dep-labs-studio.dep-proffesinal-extension-0.7.22','inangalek.project-manager-extension-13.0.1','tettetrouse0t.geode-amethyst-theme-1.0.0','densy-little-studio.wonder-for-vscode-icons-0.10.1','lavender-studio.theme-lavender-dreams-1.0.3','cosmic-themes.sql-formatter-4.2.6','stackmason1.synesthesia-theme-1.0.0','randevtek-dev.extension-thunder-client-free-2.38.6','studio-jja-laire.quarto-advanced-suite-1.127.0','oorzc.scss-to-css-compile-1.3.4','oorzc.i18n-tools-plus-1.6.8','oorzc.ssh-tools-0.5.1','oorzc.mind-map-1.0.61','angular-studio.ng-angular-extension-21.1.1','vce-brendan-studio-eich.js-debuger-vscode-1.105.1','tucyzirille-studio.angular-pro-tools-extension-7.0.1','ko-zu-gun-studio.synchronization-settings-vscode-0.18.3','studio-jjalaire-team.professional-quarto-extension-1.126.1','dev-studio-sense.php-comp-tools-vscode-1.63.18156')

description: Detects the execution of conhost.exe in a headless configuration when it is used with wmic to install a product hosted on a remote server. An adversary can abuse this technique, commonly associated with ClickFix attacks, to achieve remote code execution, bypass security mechanisms, and deploy malicious payloads.
name: Conhost Remote Installation via WMIC

description: Detects connections to the AWS Elastic Container Service (ECS) metadata service. Adversaries may attempt to access this service to retrieve IAM role credentials assigned to ECS tasks, which could be used for privilege escalation or lateral movement within an AWS environment. Monitoring these connections can help identify potential unauthorized access attempts.
name: Connection to AWS ECS Metadata Service
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'IP Connect' and dst.ip.address = '169.254.170.2' and src.process.displayName = 'curl' and src.process.cmdline contains 'credentials'

description: Detects connections to cloud instance metadata services (IMDS) across AWS, GCP, and Azure cloud platforms. Instance metadata services provide local access to instance configuration data, credentials, and other sensitive information via a special IP address (169.254.169.254). While legitimate applications may access these endpoints for authorized cloud operations, adversaries can exploit this service to obtain cloud credentials, discover instance details, or escalate privileges within the cloud environment. This rule monitors for processes making connections to the metadata service IP address with specific paths that target credential information across all three major cloud providers. By identifying suspicious access patterns to these services on Linux, Windows, and macOS operating systems, this detection can help prevent unauthorized access to cloud credentials and potential privilege escalation attacks in cloud environments.
name: Connection to Instance Metadata Service
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'IP Connect' and dst.ip.address = '169.254.169.254' and dst.port.number in (80, 443) and src.process.cmdline contains ('/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials', '/computeMetadata/v1/instance/service-accounts/', '/metadata/identity/oauth2/token')

description: Detects the execution of known container exploitation tools within a container. Adversaries may use these tools to escalate privileges, escape the container runtime, or exploit misconfigurations in Kubernetes, Docker, or other containerized environments. This behavior could indicate an attempt to compromise the underlying host, access sensitive data, or move laterally within the infrastructure.
name: Container Exploitation Tool Execution Inside Container
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and k8sCluster.containerId matches '[a-zA-Z0-9]' and (tgt.process.name = 'peirates' or tgt.process.name contains 'botb')

description: Detects the installation or execution of container inspection tools on Linux endpoints, which may indicate attempts to enumerate or analyze container environments. Adversaries may leverage such tools to gather detailed information about container configurations, security settings, and runtime environments, potentially identifying weaknesses or misconfigurations that can be exploited for privilege escalation or lateral movement.
name: Container Inspection Tool Installation or Execution
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('curl', 'wget', 'git') and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'https://github.com/' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('quarkslab/kdigger/', 'genuinetools/amicontained/')) or (tgt.process.name in ('kdigger', 'amicontained')) or (tgt.process.name in ('bash', 'dash', 'ash', 'zsh', 'csh', 'ksh', 'sh', 'tcsh') and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('kdigger', 'amicontained'))

subQueries.subQuery: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Behavioral Indicators' and indicator.name = 'ProcessTrace' and k8sCluster.containerId = * and not (src.process.cmdline contains ('jvm/temurin', 'log4j-hotpatch'))

dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Behavioral Indicators' and indicator.name = 'ProcessInjection' and k8sCluster.containerId = * and not (src.process.cmdline contains ('jvm/temurin', 'log4j-hotpatch'))

description: Correlates events where a process inside a container first attaches to another process using ptrace and subsequently performs memory injection. This behavior is indicative of advanced techniques used for process manipulation, credential access, or evading detection. While legitimate in debugging scenarios, such activity is uncommon in production environments and may signal malicious behavior.
name: Container Process Tracing and Injection

description: Detects event patterns consistent with attempted exploitation of the CopyFail vulnerability tracked as CVE-2026-31431. This activity is security-relevant because successful exploitation can indicate a compromised host and enable follow-on actions that impact confidentiality, integrity, or availability. Adversaries commonly leverage vulnerability exploitation as an entry point to establish an initial foothold and expand control within an environment.
name: CopyFail Vulnerability Exploitation Attempt (CVE-2026-31431)
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and ((event.type = 'Behavioral Indicators' and (indicator.category = 'Post Exploitation' or indicator.name = 'FilelessScriptExecution') and indicator.metadata contains 'copy.fail') or (event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.cmdline contains 'python' or src.process.displayName contains 'python') and tgt.process.cmdline = 'sh -c su'))

description: Detects suspicious BitsAdmin connections to uncommon TLDs.
name: Corelight Bitsadmin Request To Uncommon TLD
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Corelight' AND metadata.log_name = 'http' AND http_request.url.url_string contains 'microsoft bits' AND device.hostname = * AND !(device.hostname matches ('\\.com$', '\\.net$', '\\.org$', 'oneclient.sfx.ms$', '\\.com:(\\d+)$', '\\.net:(\\d+)$', '\\.org:(\\d+)$'))

description: Detects common command and control (C2) patterns in HTTP traffic.
name: Corelight C2 Traffic Observed
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Corelight' and _path = 'notice' and (note contains 'C2_Traffic_Observed' or msg contains 'C2_Traffic_Observed')

description: Detects instances where the CertUtil utility is being used to make HTTP requests.
name: Corelight CertUtil Usage in HTTP Traffic
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Corelight' and metadata.log_name = 'http' and http_request.url.url_string contains 'CertUtil'

description: Detects instances where the default SSL/TLS certificate used by Cobalt Strike, a well-known post-exploitation tool used by attackers, is observed in the network traffic.
name: Corelight Cobalt Strike Default Certificate

description: Detects invocation of Windows command shell remotely over SMB.
name: Corelight Command Shell Activity Over SMB
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Corelight' and _path = 'suricata_corelight' and alert.signature contains 'Command Shell Activity Over SMB'

description: Detects HTTP requests attempting to access or download files with a .dmp extension. Such requests can indicate attempts to obtain sensitive memory dump data, potentially signaling malicious activity.
name: Corelight DMP File over HTTP
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Corelight' and metadata.log_name = 'http' and http_request.url.url_string contains '.dmp'

description: Detects instances where DNS queries are made for domains associated with Tor and Tor2Web services.
name: Corelight DNS Connection to Tor Proxy
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Corelight' and _path = 'dns' and query contains ('tor2web.org', 'tor2web.com', 'torlink.co', 'onion.to', 'onion.ink', 'onion.cab', 'onion.nu', 'onion.link', 'onion.it', 'onion.city', 'onion')

description: Detects activity likely associated with abusing DNS infrastructure to hide data or commands inside DNS queries or responses.
name: Corelight DNS Tunnel Observed
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Corelight' and _path = 'notice' and (note contains 'DNSTunnel' or msg contains 'DNSTunnel')

description: Detects the execution of DownloadFile PowerShell command. This behavior is common in PowerShell stagers.
name: Corelight File Download via PowerShell
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Corelight' and _path = 'suricata_corelight' and alert.signature contains 'PowerShell DownloadFile'

description: Detects instances where an HTTP GET request is made for a URL that contains a .lnk file.
name: Corelight HTTP GET Request for .lnk File
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Corelight' and metadata.log_name = 'http' and http_request.http_method = 'GET' and http_request.url.url_string contains '.lnk'

description: Detects potential ICMP ping tunneling activity. ICMP ping tunneling is a technique used by attackers to encapsulate malicious traffic within ICMP echo request and reply packets, bypassing traditional network security measures.
name: Corelight ICMP Tunnel Activity

description: Detects IRC (Internet Relay Chat) traffic by detecting any IRC-related activities in network traffic logs.
name: Corelight IRC Activity
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Corelight' and _path = 'irc'

description: Detects potential exploitation attempts of Oracle E-Business Suite ieshostedsurvey.jsp via path traversal and untrusted XSL stylesheet loading on port 7201. Attackers may leverage this to load remote XSL files and achieve remote code execution.
name: Corelight Oracle EBS CVE-2025-61882 ieshostedsurvey.jsp XSLT Exploitation Attempt
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Corelight' and metadata.log_name = 'http' and http_request.http_method = 'GET' and http_request.url.url_string contains ('OA_HTML/help/../', 'OA_HTML/help/..;/') and http_request.url.url_string contains 'ieshostedsurvey.jsp' and dst_endpoint.port = 7201 and not (src_endpoint.ip matches '^(10\\\.|192\\\.168\\\.|172\\\.(1[6-9]|2[0-9]|3[0-1])\\\.)')

description: Detects post-exploitation or lateral network behavior consistent with successful Oracle E-Business Suite CVE-2025-61882 exploitation. After initial SSRF and XSLT injection via ieshostedsurvey.jsp, attackers may cause Oracle servers to perform outbound HTTP requests to external servers to fetch malicious XSL or payloads, often leveraging encoded XML requests or XSLT extensions to execute code remotely.
name: Corelight Oracle EBS CVE-2025-61882 SSRF and XSLT Follow-Up Network Activity
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Corelight' and metadata.log_name = 'http' and http_request.http_method in ('GET', 'POST') and src_endpoint.port = 7201 and dst_endpoint.ip = * and http_request.url.url_string contains ('.xsl', '.xml', '.jsp', 'VBScript.Encode') and (http_request.url.url_string contains ('Oracle', 'Java', 'XML', 'HttpURLConnection') or http_request.url.url_string contains ('ieshostedsurvey.xsl', 'configurator/UiServlet', 'oacore/html')) and not (dst_endpoint.ip matches '^(10\\\.|192\\\.168\\\.|172\\\.(1[6-9]|2[0-9]|3[0-1])\\\.)')

description: Detects possible outbound SSRF requests initiated from Oracle E-Business Suite servers following exploitation of CVE-2025-61882. The attack chain may force Oracle’s backend to POST XML payloads to attacker-controlled URLs through UiServlet or CZURLConnection, enabling command execution or data exfiltration.
name: Corelight Oracle EBS CVE-2025-61882 SSRF Payload Delivery
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Corelight' and metadata.log_name = 'http' and http_request.http_method = 'POST' and http_request.url.url_string contains ('/configurator/UiServlet', 'getUiType', 'return_url', 'redirectFromJsp') and http_request.length > 0 and not (dst_endpoint.ip matches '^(10\\\.|192\\\.168\\\.|172\\\.(1[6-9]|2[0-9]|3[0-1])\\\.)')

description: Detects DNS responses where the TXT records contain unusually long strings. Extended strings in TXT records can be indicative of DNS tunneling or data exfiltration attempts.
name: Corelight Potential DNS Tunneling via TXT Records
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Corelight' and qtype_name = 'TXT' and _path = 'dns' and answers matches '.{110,}' and not (query contains ('dnscrypt-cert.opendns.com', 'debug.opendns.com', 'secpoll.powerdns.com'))

description: Detects SMB file actions in web directories (inetpub or wwwroot) involving files with potentially suspicious extensions or unusual file naming patterns. This can help identify potential web shells or malicious scripts on web servers.
name: Corelight Potential Web Shell via Shared Webroot

description: Detects instances where HTTP requests are made for URIs that include .ps1, indicating that PowerShell scripts are being requested or transferred.
name: Corelight PowerShell Script Downloads Over HTTP
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Corelight' and metadata.log_name = 'http' and http_request.url.url_string contains '.ps1'

description: Detects instances where rclone is being used to make HTTP requests. Rclone can be used to transfer large amounts of data to cloud storage providers.
name: Corelight Rclone Usage in HTTP Traffic
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Corelight' and metadata.log_name = 'http' and http_request.url.url_string contains 'rclone'

description: Detects instances where sensitive files are being accessed or transferred over SMB, specifically those located in hidden administrative shares.
name: Corelight Sensitive File Access Over SMB

description: Detects suspicious SMB file activity by identifying sensitive file types and configurations targeted by adversaries. Flags files with extensions or names indicative of cryptographic keys, credentials, dumps, and other sensitive data in SMB shares.
name: Corelight Sensitive File Access via SMB

description: Detects instances where .lnk files are being accessed or transferred over SMB.
name: Corelight SMB File Access for .lnk Files
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Corelight' and _path = 'smb_files' and name contains '.lnk'

description: Detects file open actions in the Sysvol directory over the SMB protocol, which could indicate potential unauthorized activity.
name: Corelight SMB File Open via Sysvol
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Corelight' and _path = 'smb_files' and path contains 'sysvol' action = 'SMB::FILE_OPEN' and not (name matches ('\\.ini$', '}$'))

description: Detects SMB file write actions to hidden or administrative shares and could suggest potential unauthorized activity.
name: Corelight SMB File Writes to Hidden Shares
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Corelight' and _path = 'smb_files' and path contains '$' and action = 'SMB::FILE_WRITE'

description: Detects file write actions to the Program Files directory over the SMB protocol, which could indicate potential unauthorized activity.
name: Corelight SMB File Writes to Program Files
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Corelight' and _path = 'smb_files' and name contains 'program files' and action = 'SMB::FILE_WRITE'

description: Detects file write actions to the System32 directory over the SMB protocol, which could indicate potential unauthorized activity.
name: Corelight SMB File Writes to System32
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Corelight' and _path = 'smb_files' and name contains 'system32' and action = 'SMB::FILE_WRITE'

description: Detects instances where WebDAV clients are making HTTP PUT requests.
name: Corelight WebDAV PUT Request
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Corelight' and metadata.log_name = 'http' and http_request.url.url_string contains 'webdav' and http_request.http_method = 'PUT'

description: Detects the execution of commands that modify system resource allocation on Linux endpoints, specifically those that adjust process priority or assign CPU affinity. Adversaries may abuse these capabilities to ensure their malicious processes, such as unauthorized cryptocurrency miners or other resource-intensive malware, receive preferential access to CPU resources. By manipulating system resource settings, attackers can maximize the performance and persistence of their malicious activities while potentially degrading the effectiveness of security tools and impacting overall system stability. This behavior is often associated with attempts to evade detection, maintain control over compromised systems, and achieve operational goals that require significant computing power. Monitoring for unusual or unauthorized resource manipulation is critical for identifying and mitigating threats that seek to exploit system resources for malicious purposes.
name: CPU Tuning Command
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and ((tgt.process.name = 'nice' and tgt.process.cmdline contains (' -n ', ' --adjustment ') and tgt.process.cmdline matches 'nice -n 1\\d') or (tgt.process.name = 'taskset' and tgt.process.cmdline contains (' -c ', ' --cpu-list '))) and not (src.process.name in ('kubelet', 'systemd'))

description: Detects attempts to access credentials by dumping Windows registry hives, which store sensitive information such as user passwords and security settings. Registry hives, particularly the SAM (Security Account Manager), SYSTEM, and SECURITY hives, contain password hashes and other authentication data that can be exploited by malicious actors to extract credentials and gain unauthorized access to systems. Such activity is often a precursor to privilege escalation, lateral movement, and further compromise of critical systems within a network.
name: Credential Access via Registry Hive Dumping
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type='Process Creation' and tgt.process.cmdline matches ('reg.*\\s+export\\s+', 'reg.*\\s+save\\s+') and tgt.process.cmdline matches ('\\\\sam$', '\\\\system$', '\\\\security$')

description: Detects command-line execution of the credential-dumping tool PyPyKatz to access LSASS memory or registry hives. Adversaries can use PyPyKatz to harvest plaintext credentials, NTLM hashes, Kerberos tickets, and other authentication material, enabling lateral movement, privilege escalation, persistence, and access to sensitive resources. Extracted credentials may be used for pass-the-hash, pass-the-ticket, or offline cracking to expand compromise, deploy ransomware, or exfiltrate data, making this behavior a high-risk indicator of post-exploitation activity.
name: Credential Dump Attempt via PyPyKatz

description: Detects attempts to dump credentials from a shadow copy, which involves extracting sensitive registry hives such as SAM, SYSTEM, or SECURITY for offline cracking. This activity is commonly used by attackers to exfiltrate hashed credentials and perform password cracking outside the compromised environment, enabling them to gain unauthorized access to user accounts, escalate privileges, or move laterally within the network.
name: Credential Dumping via Shadow Copy
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type='Process Creation' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'shadowcopy' and tgt.process.cmdline matches '\\\\windows\\\\system32\\\\config\\\\(sam|system|security)[\\s\\x27\\x22]'

description: Detects the execution of the unshadow utility, a component of the John the Ripper password-cracking tool commonly used on Linux systems. Adversaries can abuse this utility to retrieve the combined contents of the /etc/shadow and /etc/passwd files, which contain sensitive information such as password hashes and user account details. By combining these files, attackers can attempt to crack passwords offline, gaining unauthorized access to user accounts. This technique is often used during post-exploitation to escalate privileges or maintain access to compromised systems.
name: Credential Dumping via Unshadow Utility
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and tgt.process.name in:anycase ('john') and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'unshadow' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('/etc/passwd','/etc/shadow')

description: Detects the use of the wbadmin command to access or back up sensitive Windows credential store files such as NTDS.dit, SAM, SYSTEM, or SECURITY. Adversaries may abuse wbadmin to create backups of these files in order to extract password hashes or security data offline. This technique bypasses normal file permissions and is often used in post-exploitation scenarios for credential dumping, privilege escalation, or persistence. This activity is uncommon in routine backup operations and may indicate unauthorized access to credentials.
name: Credential Files Accessed via Wbadmin
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('wbadmin.exe') OR tgt.process.displayName contains 'BLB Backup') and tgt.process.cmdline contains ' -include:' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('\\windows\\NTDS\\ntds.dit', '\\windows\\system32\\config\\SYSTEM', '\\windows\\system32\\config\\SECURITY', '\\windows\\system32\\config\\SAM') and not (src.process.user contains ('SYSTEM', 'AUTORITE NT', 'SISTEMA'))

description: Detects the replacement of crontab entries using the 'crontab -' or 'crontab -f -' command on a Linux endpoint. This action overwrites existing cron jobs and may indicate unauthorized modification or the insertion of persistence mechanisms by an attacker. Monitoring for such changes is critical to detect potential privilege abuse or stealthy task scheduling often used in post-compromise scenarios.
name: Crontab Entries Replaced
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.name = 'crontab' and (tgt.process.cmdline matches "^crontab\\s+-$" or tgt.process.cmdline matches "^crontab\\s+-f\\s+-")

description: Detects the modification of a crontab file with the contents of a referenced file, which may indicate unauthorized manipulation of cron jobs. Adversaries can abuse this technique to schedule malicious tasks, execute arbitrary commands, or maintain persistence on a compromised system by altering cron jobs. By modifying crontab files to reference external or malicious files, attackers can ensure that their payloads or commands are executed at specified intervals, allowing them to bypass traditional defenses and automate malicious activities. This tactic can be used to maintain a foothold on the system, establish repeat access, or exfiltrate sensitive data.
name: Crontab Replacement via Referenced file
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.name = 'crontab' and ((tgt.process.cmdline matches "^crontab\\s+([\\/\\w\\.-]+)$" and not (tgt.process.cmdline matches "^crontab\\s+-")) or (tgt.process.cmdline matches "^crontab\\s+\\-u\\s+([\\/\\w\\.-]+)\\s+([\\/\\w\\.-]+)$" and not (tgt.process.cmdline matches "^crontab\\s+\\-u\\s+([\\/\\w\\.-]+)\\s+\\-")))

description: Detects event patterns consistent with cryptocurrency wallet stealer malware operating on an endpoint. This behavior is security-relevant because it can enable adversaries to access wallet-related secrets and facilitate unauthorized transfer of digital assets. Such activity aligns with information-stealing malware tradecraft where attackers harvest wallet data to monetize access through theft.
name: Cryptocurrency Wallet Stealer Malware Activity

description: Detects the execution of processes on Linux endpoints associated with potentially unauthorized activities, such as cryptocurrency mining or other resource-intensive operations. It identifies command-line arguments that are commonly used by mining software to adjust system resources or connect to mining pools. Additionally, the rule checks for attempts to load kernel modules that may be used to facilitate such activities.
name: Cryptominers Commands Executed
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (tgt.process.cmdline contains (' --cpu-priority=', ' --donate-level=', ' --nicehash', ' --algo=', 'stratum+tcp://', 'stratum+udp://') or (tgt.process.cmdline contains 'modprobe msr' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'allow_writes'))

description: Detects the creation of a scheduled task executing JavaScript initiated by cscript.exe. While cscript.exe is a legitimate Windows Script Host executable for running script files, adversaries may exploit it to schedule malicious JavaScript code for execution, thereby establishing persistence or automating unauthorized activities. Monitoring for such behavior is essential to identify and mitigate potential security threats.
name: Cscript Create Scheduled Task Suspicious Javascript Execution
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.parent.name in:anycase ('cscript.exe') or src.process.parent.displayName = 'Microsoft ® Console Based Script Host') and (src.process.name in:anycase ('schtasks.exe') or src.process.displayName = 'Task Scheduler Configuration Tool') and src.process.cmdline contains 'create' and src.process.cmdline contains '.js'

description: Detects cmd-launched curl writing a downloaded file as a DLL on Windows, a strong indicator of payload staging for later execution (e.g., rundll32/regsvr32 or DLL side-loading). This helps defenders quickly spot and block malicious DLL drops delivered via curl.
name: Curl DLL Download via Command Line
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'File Creation' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and src.process.parent.name contains:anycase 'cmd.exe' and src.process.parent.cmdline contains:anycase 'curl.exe' and (src.process.parent.cmdline contains:anycase ' -o ' or src.process.parent.cmdline contains:anycase '--output') and tgt.file.extension matches:anycase '^dll$'

description: Detects the execution of quickassist.exe followed by curl.exe to download a .zip file, a pattern exploited by threat actors to deploy ransomware.
name: Curl Execution via Quick Assist

description: Detects curl-initiated network upload activity that exfiltrates data from a temporary directory on macOS endpoints. An adversary can abuse this technique to stealthily remove sensitive information from a compromised environment after gathering and staging it in locations that are frequently overlooked.
name: Curl Exfiltration Activity via Temporary Directory
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'osx' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.image.path contains '/usr/bin/osascript' and tgt.process.name contains ('bash','sh','zsh','dash','ksh','csh','tcsh') and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'curl' and tgt.process.cmdline matches "\\/tmp\\/.*?\\.zip") and not (src.process.cmdline contains '/usr/bin/osascript /Library/Application Support/Quad/')

description: Detects ElevationRequest events where the process originates from a user-controlled or temporary directory such as AppData, Windows Temp, Public Users, or Unix temporary paths. Executables launched from these writable locations are commonly associated with staged malware payloads, red team tooling, or unauthorized software. Elevation evaluation of binaries in these directories increases the risk of privilege escalation or execution of malicious code with elevated rights. Legitimate enterprise applications typically run from trusted installation paths like Program Files or system directories, making this behavior higher risk. This detection helps surface potential privilege escalation attempts and suspicious execution activity early in the attack chain.
name: CyberArk EPM Elevation Request from Temp Directory
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'CyberArk EPM' and event.type = 'ElevationRequest' and (process.file.parent_folder contains ('\\Appdata\\', ':\\Windows\\Temp\\', ':\\Users\\Public\\', '/dev/shm/', '/private/tmp/', '/var/tmp') or process.file.parent_folder matches '^\\/tmp\\/')

description: Detects a high volume of Block events generated by a single endpoint under standard user context in a span of 10 minutes. An unusual concentration of blocked execution attempts may indicate automated tool deployment, malware execution loops, lateral movement staging, or red team activity. While isolated block events may represent benign policy enforcement, sustained or clustered blocked activity suggests abnormal behavior. This detection provides visibility into execution storms or repeated attempts to run unauthorized software. It helps surface endpoints exhibiting suspicious execution patterns that warrant further investigation.
name: CyberArk EPM Excessive Execution Blocks on an Endpoint

description: Detects repeated AttackAttempt or SuspiciousActivityAttempt events associated with a single user account in a 10 minute window. Recurrent privileged threat attempts tied to a user identity may indicate credential misuse, compromised credentials, privilege probing, or insider misuse. While individual events may not confirm malicious intent, repeated occurrences suggest persistent interaction with protected resources. This behavior can align with credential access attempts, privilege escalation testing, or automated tool execution under a user context. The detection provides user-centric visibility into abnormal privileged activity patterns.
name: CyberArk EPM Multiple Attack Attempt Events From Same User

description: Detects repeated triggering of the same privileged threat protection policy through AttackAttempt or SuspiciousActivityAttempt events in a 10 minute window. Persistent activation of a specific protection policy may indicate targeted probing of a protected resource, automated retry behavior, or deliberate attempts to bypass security controls. While individual policy triggers may reflect isolated behavior, repeated occurrences suggest sustained interaction with sensitive components. This pattern can align with credential access attempts, privilege abuse, or defense evasion activity. The detection provides insight into repeated targeting of specific security boundaries within the environment.
name: CyberArk EPM Multiple Attempts Against Same Threat Protection Policy

description: Detects 10 or more Block events involving the same file hash executed by a standard user in a span of 10 minutes. Multiple execution attempts of an identical binary indicate persistence, automated retry behavior, or scripted execution attempts following enforcement denial. Attack tools and malware commonly retry execution after being blocked, especially when attempting privilege escalation or persistence. Legitimate users typically do not repeatedly execute the same blocked binary within a short timeframe. This detection helps identify potential brute-force execution attempts, staging activity, or misuse of unauthorized tools.
name: CyberArk EPM Multiple Execution Attempts from Blocked File

description: Detects repeated AttackAttempt or SuspiciousActivityAttempt events associated with the same process file hash in a 10 minute window. When a single executable repeatedly triggers privileged threat protection mechanisms, it may indicate automated credential probing, scripted post-exploitation activity, or persistent interaction with protected resources. Legitimate software typically does not continuously generate privileged threat attempts under normal operation. Recurrent activity from the same binary suggests potential misuse, malicious tooling, or repeated attempts to access restricted assets. This detection provides visibility into binaries exhibiting sustained suspicious behavior across one or more endpoints.
name: CyberArk EPM Multiple Threat Protection Events From Same Process

description: Detects DetectAccess or RestrictAccess events where a process without a valid publisher attempts to access administrative network shares such as C$, ADMIN$, or IPC$. These shares are commonly used for remote administration but are also frequently abused during lateral movement and post-exploitation activity. Access to administrative shares by unsigned or untrusted processes is highly unusual and may indicate credential misuse, remote execution attempts, or network propagation behavior. While legitimate administrative tools may access these shares, they are typically signed and originate from trusted installation paths. This detection provides visibility into potential lateral movement activity within the environment.
name: CyberArk EPM Possible Network Share Enumeration
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'CyberArk EPM' and event.type in:anycase ('DetectAccess', 'RestrictAccess') unmapped.accessTargetType = 'NetworkShare' and unmapped.accessTargetName contains ('\\C$', '\\ADMIN$', 'IPC$') and not (actor.invoked_by = *)

description: Detects DetectAccess or RestrictAccess events where a non-administrative process lacking a valid publisher attempts to access sensitive operating system credential stores or browser data. An adversary can abuse these locations including the SAM, SECURITY, and SYSTEM hives, NTDS.dit, Windows Credential Manager files, Unix shadow/passwd files, and browser profile to harvest authentication material for credential dumping or offline password extraction. By accessing these files through an unsigned or untrusted process, an attacker can steal stored web credentials and administrative hashes to facilitate lateral movement, privilege escalation, or further post-exploitation activity within the environment.
name: CyberArk EPM Potential Credential Access Activity
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'CyberArk EPM' and event.type in:anycase ('DetectAccess', 'RestrictAccess') and unmapped.accessTargetType = 'File' and unmapped.accessTargetName contains ('\\System32\\config\\SAM', '\\System32\\config\\SECURITY', '\\System32\\config\\SYSTEM', 'NTDS.dit', '\\Microsoft\\Credentials\\', '/etc/shadow', '/etc/passwd', 'Login Data', '\\Mozilla\\Firefox\\Profiles\\', '\\Microsoft\\Edge\\User Data\\') and unmapped.userIsAdmin = false and not (actor.invoked_by = *)

description: Detects file access activity occurring in Detect mode involving potentially sensitive file types commonly associated with credential storage, memory dumps, backups, or database content. These file types may contain authentication material, application data, or other sensitive information that could be leveraged during post-exploitation or data exfiltration activity. Because the event is logged in Detect mode rather than being actively blocked, the behavior may represent a policy gap or monitoring-only control. While not inherently malicious on its own, access to these file types can be indicative of credential harvesting, data collection, or staging behavior. This detection provides visibility into potentially risky file access patterns that warrant review.
name: CyberArk EPM Potential Sensitive File Access
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'CyberArk EPM' and event.type = 'DetectAccess' and unmapped.accessTargetType = 'File' and unmapped.accessTargetName contains ('.dmp', '.bak', '.db', '.sqlite', '.kdbx', '.sql')

description: Detects Block events where a standard (non-admin) user attempted to execute an executable or script file from a user-controlled or temporary directory such as AppData, Windows Temp, Public Users, or Unix temporary paths. Executables launched from these writable locations are commonly associated with staged malware payloads, unauthorized software, or attack tooling. The default-deny enforcement prevented the process from running, indicating policy enforcement against potentially unsafe code. Legitimate enterprise applications rarely execute directly from these transient directories under normal user context. This detection helps identify early-stage malware execution attempts and potential initial access or privilege escalation activity that was successfully blocked.
name: CyberArk EPM Potential Staged Binary
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'CyberArk EPM' and event.type = 'Block' and disposition = 'Blocked' and  unmapped.userIsAdmin = false and process.file.name contains ('.exe', '.msi', '.sh') and (process.file.path contains ('\\Appdata\\', ':\\Windows\\Temp\\', ':\\Users\\Public\\', '/dev/shm/', '/private/tmp/', '/var/tmp') or process.file.parent_folder matches '^\\/tmp\\/')

description: Detects potential ransomware-related activity where a process triggers CyberArk EPM ransomware protection events in conjunction with ransomware-specific behaviors observed in SentinelOne endpoint telemetry. The combination of privilege-aware ransomware detection and additional file or process-based behaviors significantly increases confidence of active encryption activity. This pattern is consistent with real-world ransomware operations that rapidly modify files and interact with protected resources. Correlating multiple telemetry sources reduces false positives and strengthens detection fidelity.
name: CyberArk EPM Ransomware Protection Triggered with Correlated Endpoint Indicators

description: Detects internet access attempts to domains using top-level domains (TLDs) or network ports commonly associated with malicious infrastructure, such as reverse shells, command-and-control (C2) frameworks, and penetration testing tools. An adversary can abuse these communication channels to establish persistence and maintain remote control over a compromised host. Specifically, attackers often utilize suspicious TLDs—frequently observed in phishing campaigns and short-lived infrastructure—to mask their identity and evade standard reputation filters. Furthermore, by leveraging specific ports commonly used by adversary tooling like Metasploit and Cobalt Strike, an attacker can facilitate data exfiltration, stage secondary malware payloads, or execute unauthorized remote commands.
name: CyberArk EPM Suspicious Internet Access
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'CyberArk EPM' and event.type in:anycase ('DetectAccess', 'RestrictAccess') and unmapped.accessTargetType = 'Internet' and unmapped.accessTargetName matches ('http(s)?:\\/\\/[^\\/].*\\.(xyz|top|site|info|sh|ru|su|icu|pw|ga|club|work|click|rest|shop|fun)$', ':(4444|1337|31337|6666|6667|9001|5555|2222)$')

description: Detects DetectAccess or RestrictAccess events where a process originating from a user-controlled or temporary directory attempts to access the memory of security-related processes or lsass.exe. An adversary can abuse this behavior to perform credential dumping from the Local Security Authority Subsystem Service (LSASS) or to inject malicious code into trusted security tools to disable, tamper with, or evade defensive controls during post-exploitation. Legitimate interactions with these processes are typically performed by signed, trusted components running from standard installation directories. Memory access attempts from executables located in writable or temporary paths are highly abnormal and may indicate defense evasion activity.
name: CyberArk EPM Suspicious Memory Access to LSASS or Security Tools
s1ql: dataSource.name='CyberArk EPM' and event.type in:anycase ('RestrictAccess', 'DetectAccess') and actor.process.file.path contains ('\\Appdata\\', ':\\Windows\\Temp\\', ':\\Users\\Public\\', '/dev/shm/', '/private/tmp/', '/var/tmp') and unmapped.accessTargetName contains ('avastui', 'avgui', 'nswscsvc', 'sophoshealth', 'defender', 'msmpeng', 'windefend', 'avira', 'mcafee', 'mcshield', 'symantec', 'kaspersky', 'crowdstrike', 'falcon', 'sentinel', 'defender', 'trendmicro', 'carbonblack', 'cortex', 'cylance', 'antivirus', 'lsass.exe')

description: Detects anomalous deletion of AWS resources within an account. While resource deletion may occur during routine operations such as infrastructure cleanup, scaling activities, or testing, adversaries may delete critical resources to disrupt services, evade detection, or hinder forensic investigations. Unauthorized or unexpected deletions can result in loss of availability, weakened security posture, or destruction of evidence. Monitoring for anomalous AWS resource deletions helps detect malicious activity, safeguard cloud assets, and maintain operational resilience.
name: Darktrace Anomalous AWS Resources Deletion
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Darktrace' and event.type = 'Create' and class_name = 'Detection Finding' and unmapped.model.then.name contains 'Anomalous AWS Resources Deleted'

description: Detects unusually long incoming Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) sessions that may indicate potential malicious activity. While extended RDP sessions can occur during legitimate administrative tasks, adversaries often maintain prolonged connections to perform data exfiltration, privilege escalation, lateral movement, or persistent access. Monitoring for anomalous session durations helps identify potential unauthorized access and compromised systems.
name: Darktrace Anomalous Incoming Long Remote Desktop Session
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Darktrace' and event.type = 'Create' and class_name = 'Detection Finding' and unmapped.model.then.name contains 'Anomalous Connection::Unusual Incoming Long Remote Desktop Session'

description: Detects unusual network scanning activity from a device that may indicate potential reconnaissance behavior. While network scans can be legitimate during vulnerability assessments, troubleshooting, or inventory checks, adversaries often perform scans to identify live hosts, open ports, and vulnerable services. Monitoring for anomalous scanning patterns helps detect early stages of attacks, including lateral movement, privilege escalation, and preparation for exploitation.
name: Darktrace Anomalous Internal/External Scanning Activity
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Darktrace' and event.type = 'Create' and class_name = 'Detection Finding' and unmapped.model.then.name contains 'Suspicious Network Scan Activity'

description: Detects potential password spraying attacks targeting LDAP services. Password spraying occurs when an attacker attempts to authenticate against multiple accounts using a small set of commonly used passwords.
name: Darktrace Anomalous LDAP Login Spray Activity
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Darktrace' and event.type = 'Create' and class_name = 'Detection Finding' and unmapped.model.then.name contains 'LDAP Password Spray'

description: Detects the potential use of attack frameworks or reconnaissance tools executed from a device within the environment. Reconnaissance tools are commonly used by both security professionals and malicious actors to identify hosts, open ports, vulnerabilities, and misconfigurations. However, unauthorized usage may indicate adversarial behavior aimed at network enumeration, privilege escalation, lateral movement, or data exfiltration preparation.
name: Darktrace Anomalous Use of Attack and Recon Tools
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Darktrace' and event.type = 'Create' and category_name = 'Findings' and class_name= 'Detection Finding' and unmapped.model.then.name contains 'Device::Attack and Recon Tools'

description: Detects the use of an application-layer protocol (e.g., HTTP, DNS, SMB) operating over a non-standard or uncommon port. While some environments may intentionally remap services for testing or legacy reasons, adversaries frequently use uncommon ports to bypass security controls, evade detection, or establish covert command and control channels. Monitoring for application protocols running on non-standard ports helps identify potential tunneling, lateral movement, or exfiltration activity.
name: Darktrace Application Protocol on Uncommon Port
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Darktrace' and event.type = 'Create' and unmapped.model.then.name contains 'Application Protocol on Uncommon Port'

description: Detects when a network interface (ENI) is removed from an AWS environment. Network interfaces are essential components that connect EC2 instances and other AWS resources to virtual networks. While legitimate removals may occur during instance termination, scaling events, or reconfiguration, adversaries may detach or delete ENIs to disrupt monitoring, evade detection, break security controls, or interfere with forensic investigations. Monitoring unexpected ENI removals helps ensure visibility, maintain connectivity, and detect potential malicious activity.
name: Darktrace AWS Network Interface Removal
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Darktrace' and event.type = 'Create' and class_name = 'Detection Finding' and unmapped.model.then.name contains 'AWS Network Interface Removed'

description: Detects a device repeatedly connecting to external or internal hosts. This is commonly seen during botnet compromise activity. An adversary can abuse this behavior by establishing a Command and Control (C2) channel where the compromised endpoint periodically connects back to an attacker-controlled server to check for new commands, download additional stages, or exfiltrate data.
name: Darktrace Beacon Activity Detected
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Darktrace' and category_name = 'Findings' and event.type = 'Create' and unmapped.model.then.name contains 'Beacon'

description: Detects potential cryptocurrency mining activity. While legitimate mining may occur in research or controlled environments, unauthorized mining typically indicates resource hijacking or system compromise. Adversaries often deploy Monero miners on cloud infrastructure, servers, or endpoints to exploit compute resources for profit. These operations can lead to degraded performance, increased costs, and security risks. Monitoring for network traffic to known mining pools, mining software execution, or anomalous CPU usage helps identify cryptojacking and unauthorized compute abuse.
name: Darktrace Crypto Mining Activity Detected
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Darktrace' and event.type = 'Create' and unmapped.model.then.name contains ('Mining', 'Miner')

description: Detects repeated or unusual Kerberos authentication attempts (AS-REQ/AS-REP or TGS requests) that may indicate brute-force or credential-guessing activity. While normal Kerberos traffic is expected, attackers can abuse it to find valid accounts or steal service account credentials. Monitoring these patterns helps identify credential theft and early lateral movement.
name: Darktrace Kerberos Authentication Brute Force Attempt
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Darktrace' and event.type = 'Create' and class_name = 'Detection Finding' and unmapped.model.then.name contains 'Kerberos Password Brute Force'

description: Detects when a user logs in from an endpoint that is rare or previously unseen while the same user is already active on another device. While legitimate scenarios may involve users switching devices during travel or remote work, adversaries may attempt to log in from unusual endpoints to hijack active sessions, move laterally, or bypass detection. Monitoring for concurrent logins from rare endpoints helps identify potential account compromise, credential misuse, or unauthorized access.
name: Darktrace Login From Rare Endpoint While User Is Active
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Darktrace' and event.type = 'Create' and unmapped.model.then.name contains 'Login From Rare Endpoint While User Is Active'

description: Detects connections where a new or previously unseen user agent communicates directly with an IP address instead of a hostname. While direct-to-IP communication can occur in some automation or testing scenarios, adversaries often use it to evade DNS-based defenses, conceal infrastructure, or establish command and control channels. The combination of a new user agent string with an IP-only connection increases the likelihood of malicious activity such as malware beaconing, data exfiltration, or unauthorized remote access.
name: Darktrace New User Agent to IP Without Hostname
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Darktrace' and event.type = 'Create' and unmapped.model.then.name contains 'New User Agent to IP Without Hostname'

description: Detects suspicious Remote Procedure Call (RPC) activity that may indicate reconnaissance operations. RPC is commonly used in Windows environments for legitimate management tasks such as service enumeration, file sharing, or remote execution. However, adversaries frequently abuse RPC to gather information about users, groups, shares, services, and system configurations. Identifying anomalous or excessive RPC requests can help detect early stages of enumeration, lateral movement, or privilege escalation attempts.
name: Darktrace Possible RPC Recon Activity
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Darktrace' and event.type = 'Create' and class_name = 'Detection Finding' and unmapped.model.then.name contains 'Possible RPC Recon'

description: Detects when a file is downloaded from GitHub that may be associated with potentially malicious tools, exploit scripts, or reconnaissance utilities. While downloading files from GitHub is common for development and administrative purposes, adversaries often leverage public repositories to fetch malicious payloads, living-off-the-land binaries, or scripts for privilege escalation, lateral movement, and persistence. Unusual or unauthorized downloads should be investigated to determine intent and potential risk.
name: Darktrace Suspicious GitHub File Download
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Darktrace' and event.type = 'Create' and category_name = 'Findings' and class_name= 'Detection Finding' and unmapped.model.then.name contains 'Device::Anomalous Github Download'

description: Detects anomalous SMBv3 (Server Message Block version 3) connections to a domain controller (DC). While SMB traffic between endpoints and domain controllers is common for authentication, file access, and policy distribution, unusual or unexpected SMBv3 activity may indicate malicious behavior. Adversaries can abuse SMB to move laterally, deliver payloads, or exfiltrate sensitive data.
name: Darktrace Suspicious SMB3 Traffic to Domain Controller
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Darktrace' and event.type = 'Create' and class_name = 'Detection Finding' and unmapped.model.then.name contains 'Anomalous SMB3 to DC'

description: Detects unusual or suspicious SMB (Server Message Block) sessions established using administrative privileges. While administrative SMB sessions are common for legitimate tasks such as system management, patch deployment, or file sharing, adversaries often abuse SMB admin sessions to move laterally, access sensitive files, or execute commands remotely. Monitoring for anomalous SMB admin activity helps identify potential credential misuse, lateral movement, or unauthorized administrative access within the environment.
name: Darktrace Unusual Admin SMB Session
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Darktrace' and event.type = 'Create' and class_name = 'Detection Finding' and unmapped.model.then.name contains 'Unusual Admin SMB Session'

description: Detects when files are uploaded through a Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) session. While administrators may legitimately transfer patches, updates, or configuration files using RDP, adversaries often abuse this capability to deliver malware, stage tools for lateral movement, or stealthily exfiltrate data. Monitoring RDP file upload activity helps identify potential misuse, unauthorized access, and early stages of compromise.
name: Darktrace Upload via Remote Desktop
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Darktrace' and event.type = 'Create' and unmapped.model.then.name contains 'Upload via Remote Desktop'

description: Detects the use of the 'shred' command on Linux systems, which is commonly used to securely delete files by overwriting their contents. This activity may indicate an attempt to destroy data, potentially as part of a malicious operation such as ransomware or data sabotage. Monitoring for this command helps identify potential data destruction activities that could impact system integrity and availability.
name: Data Destruction via Shred Utility
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type in ('File Modification', 'File Deletion') and src.process.name = 'shred' and src.process.cmdline contains (' -s ', ' --size', ' -u ', ' -z ', ' --zero', ' -n ', ' --iterations') and not (src.process.parent.name in ('logrotate')) and not (tgt.file.extension in ('gz', 'log', 'zip', 'tar'))

subQueries.subQuery: event.type = 'Behavioral Indicators' and indicator.name = 'InfoStealerTelegramExfiltration' and src.process.parent.name contains 'bash'
event.type = 'Behavioral Indicators' and indicator.name = 'ExfiltrationOverNonEncryptedNonC2Channel' and indicator.metadata contains 'api.telegram.org/bot'

description: Detects host or network activity consistent with sending data to Telegram through bot-based communications. Adversaries leverage messaging platforms to move sensitive information off-network, blending exfiltration traffic with legitimate web services to evade traditional egress controls. This behavior is security-relevant because it indicates use of an external command-and-control or data drop channel outside sanctioned file transfer mechanisms, potentially impacting data confidentiality.
name: Data Exfiltration via Telegram Bot API Communications

description: Detects the creation or modification of D-Bus services on Linux systems. Adversaries may abuse the D-Bus interprocess communication system to achieve persistence by registering malicious services or hijacking legitimate ones. Monitoring changes to D-Bus configurations can help identify unauthorized service registrations indicative of compromise.
name: D-Bus Service Created or Updated
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type in ('File Creation', 'File Modification') and tgt.file.path contains ('/dbus-1/system-services/', '/dbus-1/system.d/') and not (src.process.name in ('crio', 'dockerd', 'containerd', 'podman', 'dpkg', 'apt-get', 'yum', 'rpm', 'packagekitd', 'microdnf', 'executor')) and not (tgt.file.path matches '^/(var|tmp|run)/') and src.process.name = * and not (src.process.cmdline contains ('yum update', 'dnf update', 'dnf install', 'AnsiballZ_dnf.py'))

description: Detects the execution of the Linux DebugFS utility within a container. DebugFS is a low-level debugging tool that provides direct access to disk devices and filesystems. Its use inside a container—especially a privileged one—may indicate an attempt to access host system data or escape the container boundary. This behavior is uncommon in legitimate containerized workloads and may represent malicious activity such as privilege escalation or host compromise.
name: Debugfs Launched in Container
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and k8sCluster.containerId = * and tgt.process.name = 'debugfs' and not (tgt.process.cmdline contains ' -r ')

description: Detects attempts to delete Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) connection history files or related registry entries via command-line utilities such as 'powershell.exe' or 'cmd.exe' on Windows endpoints. This rule identifies process or script executions that invoke deletion commands targeting artifacts like 'Default.rdp', 'Microsoft/Terminal Server Client/Servers', or RDP jump list files. Attackers often use these techniques to erase evidence of RDP usage and evade forensic analysis. Monitoring for these actions helps identify potential lateral movement, anti-forensics activity, or post-exploitation cleanup on compromised systems.
name: Delete RDP History via Command Line Execution
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' AND endpoint.os = 'windows' AND event.type in ('Process Creation', 'Command Script') AND (src.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe', 'cmd.exe') OR src.process.displayName in:anycase ('Windows PowerShell', 'Windows Command Processor')) AND (src.process.cmdline contains ('Remove-Item ', 'Remove-ItemProperty ', 'del ', 'delete ', 'erase ', ' rd ', ' ri ', ' rm ', 'rmdir ') OR cmdScript.content contains ('Remove-Item ', 'Remove-ItemProperty ', 'del ', 'delete ', 'erase ', ' rd ', ' ri ', ' rm ', 'rmdir ')) AND (src.process.cmdline contains ('Default.rdp', '\\Microsoft\\Terminal Server Client\\Servers', '\\Microsoft\\Terminal Server Client\\Default', '%AppData%\\Microsoft\\Windows\\Recent\\AutomaticDestinations') OR cmdScript.content contains ('Default.rdp', '\\Microsoft\\Terminal Server Client\\Servers', '\\Microsoft\\Terminal Server Client\\Default', '%AppData%\\Microsoft\\Windows\\Recent\\AutomaticDestinations'))

description: Detects the use of fsutil.exe to delete the USN journal volume. Adversaries can abuse this action to obscure file system activity and evade forensic analysis. By deleting the USN journal, attackers remove a key source of evidence that tracks file creation, modification, and deletion events. This makes it more difficult to detect and investigate malicious actions such as malware installation, data exfiltration, or other unauthorized activity on the system.
name: Delete Volume Journal via Fsutil
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'fsutil' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'deletejournal' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'usn'

description: Detects the deletion of shell history files such as .bash_history, .zsh_history, or .sh_history on Unix-like systems. Unauthorized deletion of these files can indicate attempts to conceal command-line activity, evade detection, or cover up malicious actions. Monitoring this behavior helps identify potential security incidents and supports forensic investigations.
name: Deletion of Shell History Files
s1ql: dataSource.name  = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'File Deletion' and src.process.name in:anycase ('rm', 'unlink', 'unlinkat', 'rmdir', 'shred','srm', 'secure-delete', 'safe-rm', 'wipe', 'wipefile', 'python', 'python3', 'perl', 'ruby', 'node', 'java', 'osascript', 'powershell') AND tgt.file.path matches '^\\/(home|Users)\\/[^\\/]+\\/\\.(bash|zsh|sh)_history$|^\\/root\\/\\.(bash|zsh|sh)_history$'

description: Detects the scripted use of macOS directory services to systematically validate account credentials through repeated authentication attempts. An adversary can abuse these native authentication pathways to conduct stealthy brute-force attacks, password spraying, or credential stuffing operations against local or network accounts. Such activity commonly precedes account takeover, privilege escalation, or lateral movement once a working credential is discovered.
name: Directory Services Scripted Credential Validation
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'osx' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.parent.name in:anycase ('bash', 'dash', 'ash', 'zsh', 'csh', 'ksh', 'sh', 'tcsh', 'osascript') or src.process.parent.displayName in:anycase ('bash', 'dash', 'ash', 'zsh', 'csh', 'ksh', 'sh', 'tcsh', 'osascript')) and (src.process.name in:anycase ('osascript') or src.process.displayName in:anycase ('osascript')) and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('dscl') or tgt.process.displayName in:anycase ('dscl')) and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'authonly'

description: Detects the disabling of systemd-journald through the use of systemctl commands. Adversaries can exploit this capability to disrupt logging and monitoring processes, effectively preventing the generation of system logs that could record malicious activities. By disabling systemd-journald, attackers can conceal evidence of actions such as privilege escalation, lateral movement, or data exfiltration. This tactic may be used to evade detection by security teams or automated defenses that rely on log data for threat identification.
name: Disabling systemd-journald via systemctl
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and tgt.process.name in:anycase ('systemctl') and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'stop' and tgt.process.cmdline matches 'systemd-journald$' and not (src.process.image.path contains '/opt/chef/')

description: Detects the disabling of User Account Control (UAC) using reg.exe. An adversary can abuse this by turning off UAC, which serves as a critical security feature designed to prevent unauthorized changes to system settings and application installations. Disabling UAC reduces user oversight and allows the attacker to escalate privileges without triggering security warnings or prompts.
name: Disabling User Account Control via Reg
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Registry Value Modified' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('reg.exe') or src.process.displayName = 'Registry Console Tool') and registry.keyPath = 'MACHINE\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\Policies\\System\\EnableLUA' and registry.value = '0'

description: Detects attempts to disable Windows event logging using wevtutil.exe, a command-line utility that adversaries can abuse to halt the collection of critical system and security logs, hindering detection efforts. Disabling event logging can disrupt incident response, obstruct forensic analysis, and obscure traces of malicious activity. This tactic is commonly employed alongside other evasion techniques to reduce visibility and avoid detection during or after an attack.
name: Disabling Windows Event Logging via Wevtutil
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' AND endpoint.os = 'windows' AND (tgt.process.name contains 'wevtutil' OR tgt.process.displayName = 'Eventing Command Line Utility') AND tgt.process.cmdline contains ('system', 'application', 'security') AND tgt.process.cmdline contains ' sl ' AND tgt.process.cmdline contains '/e:false' AND (src.process.parent.name in ('powershell.exe', 'pwsh.exe', 'cmd.exe') OR src.process.parent.displayName in ('Windows Command Processor', 'Windows PowerShell', 'pwsh'))

description: Detects the use of the dev drive or developer volume in Windows 11 with the /disallowAv flag. This flag disables the attachment of antivirus filters to the dev drive. Adversaries can exploit this to bypass AV solutions by placing and executing malware directly within the dev drive. Since the AV filters are disabled, traditional real-time scanning and detection mechanisms will not function, allowing the malware to operate undetected. This could lead to the execution of malicious code, data exfiltration, or further compromise of the system.
name: Disallow Antivirus Filter via DevDrv
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'devdrv' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'enable' and tgt.process.cmdline contains '/disallowAv'

description: Detects the use of the dd utility to overwrite or erase disk data. Adversaries may leverage dd to destroy evidence, remove sensitive information, or wipe partitions as part of a destructive attack or anti-forensic technique. Monitoring for dd executions with parameters targeting entire disks or partitions can help identify potential data destruction attempts, unauthorized disk modifications, or system tampering.
name: Disk Data Wipe Attempt via Dd Utility
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os in ('osx', 'linux') and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.image.path matches 'bin/dd$' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'of=/dev/disk'

description: Detects the use of the diskutil utility on macOS to mount a disk with the noowners option enabled. Disabling file ownership enforcement allows all users to read and write files on the mounted volume, which can be abused by adversaries to bypass permission controls, access sensitive data, or stage malicious payloads from external or removable media. This behavior may indicate defense evasion or preparation for further malicious activity on the system.
name: Disk Mount with Ownership Disabled
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'osx' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.name = 'diskutil' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'mount' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'noowners'

description: Detects instances where a Scheduled Task is registered or started to execute rundll32.exe and load a Dynamic-Link Library (.dll) from the Public Music folder. It is highly uncommon for DLL files to be stored in this directory and invoked via scheduled tasks. Adversaries may abuse this technique to establish persistence, evade detection, or escalate privileges. Monitoring for such activity is essential to detect unauthorized modifications and potential security threats.
name: DLL Execution from Public Music Directory via Scheduled Task

description: Detects instances where a process signed by 'HAIHAISOFT LIMITED' loads a DLL from an unexpected or untrusted location, potentially indicating a DLL hijacking attempt. Adversaries may exploit legitimate applications that load DLLs without specifying full paths, allowing malicious DLLs placed in higher-priority directories to be executed. Monitoring for such behavior is crucial to identify and respond to potential security threats.
name: DLL Hijack via HAIHAISOFT LIMITED Publisher
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Behavioral Indicator' and indicator.name = 'DllHijack' and src.process.publisher = 'HAIHAISOFT LIMITED'

description: Detects DNS requests from a Linux host to domains associated with known cryptocurrency mining pools. Such connections often indicate unauthorized cryptocurrency mining activity, where system resources are hijacked to mine cryptocurrency for an attacker. Monitoring for known mining pool domains can help identify and stop illicit mining operations early.
name: DNS Queries to Known Cryptocurrency Mining Pools
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.category = 'dns' and event.dns.request contains ('pool.supportxmr.com', 'mine.c3pool.com', 'xmr-eu1.nanopool.org', 'xmr-eu2.nanopool.org', 'xmr-us-east1.nanopool.org', 'xmr-us-west1.nanopool.org', 'xmr-asia1.nanopool.org', 'xmr-jp1.nanopool.org', 'xmr-au1.nanopool.org', 'xmr.2miners.com', 'xmr.hashcity.org', 'xmrpool.eu', 'pool.hashvault.pro', 'moneroocean.stream')

description: Detects attempts to download the Docker client utility from within a running container. This behavior may indicate adversary efforts to escalate privileges, perform container breakout, or gain control over the container host. Threat actors often leverage the installation of management utilities inside containers to facilitate lateral movement or further compromise. Such activity is atypical in standard container operations and may signal malicious intent or misconfiguration.
name: Docker Client Download Attempt Within Containerized Environment
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and k8sCluster.containerId matches '[a-zA-Z0-9]' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('curl', 'wget') and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('https://', 'http://') and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('download.docker.com', 'get.docker.com')

description: Detects suspicious Docker run command execution with device mount on Linux endpoints. Adversaries can abuse Docker to run containers with device mounts such as /dev/sda, which can provide access to sensitive devices and potentially bypass traditional security controls. This technique allows attackers to leverage Docker's capabilities to perform actions that may evade detection.
name: Docker Container Sharing Host Devices

description: Detects the creation or modification of Domain Backup private keys. Adversaries can abuse this by attempting to retrieve the Data Protection API (DPAPI) domain backup key from a Domain Controller (DC). This key can be used to decrypt domain user master key files, granting the adversary access to sensitive information such as user credentials, Kerberos tickets, and protected data. This allows for lateral movement, privilege escalation, and long-term persistence within the compromised domain.
name: Domain Backup DPAPI Key Creation or Alteration
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' AND endpoint.os = 'windows' AND event.category = 'file' AND event.type != 'File Deletion' AND tgt.file.path contains 'ntds_capi' AND tgt.file.extension in ('pfx', 'pvk')

description: Detects attempts to conduct domain enumeration using Beacon Object Files (BOF), which involve executing in-memory shellcode. Adversaries may leverage BOF for stealthy enumeration of domain information, gathering details about users, groups, and domain configurations without writing data to disk. This technique is used to evade traditional detection mechanisms and gain insights into the Active Directory environment for further exploitation. Please enable the 'AccountDiscovery' and 'BreakpointHookBypassWithoutDebugger' Behavioral Indicators to make this rule work.
name: Domain Enumeration via Beacon Object File

description: Detects the execution of net.exe or net1.exe with command line arguments designed to retrieve the domain password policy. Adversaries may abuse this by querying the domain's password policy to gather information about password complexity requirements, expiration settings, and lockout thresholds. This information can be used to tailor password-guessing or brute-force attacks, helping attackers identify weak spots in the organization's security posture. Understanding these policies allows adversaries to craft more effective attacks, such as targeting accounts with weak passwords or attempting to bypass lockout mechanisms.
name: Domain Password Policy Discovery via Net
