s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('net.exe', 'net1.exe') or src.process.displayName = 'Net Command') and src.process.cmdline contains 'account' and src.process.cmdline contains 'domain'

description: Detects the use of the dsconfigad utility to unbind a macOS system from an Active Directory (AD) domain. Attackers or malicious insiders may use this command to disrupt authentication, evade monitoring, or isolate a system from domain policies and controls. Legitimate use of this command is typically performed by administrators during decommissioning or reconfiguration, so unexpected executions should be investigated.
name: Domain Unenrollment via Dsconfigad Utility
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'osx' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.image.path matches 'bin/dsconfigad$' and tgt.process.cmdline contains (' -r ', ' -remove ') and (not(src.process.parent.cmdline contains '/Application Support/JAMF/'))

description: Detects presence of the Doppelganger tool used for dumping credentials from the LSASS process. This activity is indicative of credential access attempts, a common objective in post-exploitation stages by adversaries seeking lateral movement or privilege escalation. The use of specialized LSASS dumping tools aligns with advanced threat actor tactics to extract sensitive authentication material from memory. Such behavior poses a significant risk to enterprise security by enabling unauthorized access to privileged accounts.
name: Doppelganger LSASS Credential Dumper Tool
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type in ('Process Creation', 'Command Script', 'File Creation', 'File Modification') and (src.process.image.originalFileName in:anycase ('Doppelganger.exe') or src.process.name in:anycase ('Doppelganger.exe') or tgt.process.name in:anycase ('Doppelganger.exe') or src.process.cmdline contains 'Doppelganger.exe' or tgt.process.cmdline contains 'Doppelganger.exe' or cmdScript.content contains 'Doppelganger.exe' or tgt.file.OriginalFileName in:anycase ('Doppelganger.exe') or tgt.file.name in:anycase ('Doppelganger.exe') or tgt.file.path contains 'Doppelganger.exe')

subQueries.subQuery: event.type = 'File Creation' and tgt.file.extension = 'py' and src.process.name in ('curl', 'wget')
indicator.name = 'MemoryDump' and (src.process.name contains 'python' or src.process.displayName contains 'python')

description: Detects the use of curl or wget to download a Python script and its subsequent execution, where the script performs memory dumping operations. Such scripts may be designed to extract sensitive information, including secrets and credentials, from the memory of CI/CD pipeline runners. This behavior is indicative of potential supply chain compromises or unauthorized analysis attempts. Monitoring such activities is crucial for detecting security threats and preventing unauthorized access to sensitive data.
name: Download and Execute Memory Dump Python Script

description: Detects a driver being loaded from a suspicious directory. Threat actors may deploy malicious kernel drivers from non-standard locations to gain elevated privileges, disable security tools, or establish persistent access. This activity may indicate BYOVD (Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver) attacks or rootkit deployment.
name: Driver Load From Suspicious Directory

description: Detects kernel driver load events from Alternate Data Streams (ADS). Adversaries may abuse NTFS ADS to hide malicious driver files and load them to gain kernel-level privileges.
name: Driver Load via Alternate Data Stream

description: Detects attempts to dump account hashes on macOS using built-in utilities. These commands can be abused to extract sensitive account information, including password hashes, from the system. Such behavior is often indicative of credential theft or privilege escalation attempts. Monitoring for the misuse of these commands helps identify potential unauthorized access or compromise of account credentials.
name: Dumping Account Hashes via Built-In Commands
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'osx' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and ((tgt.process.image.path matches 'bin/mkpassdb$' or tgt.process.image.path matches 'bin/defaults$') and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('ShadowHashData', '-dump'))

description: Detects when an eBPF (extended Berkeley Packet Filter) program is attached to a network interface on a Linux system. eBPF is a powerful technology that allows programs to run in the kernel space and can be used for network filtering, monitoring, and packet manipulation. While eBPF has legitimate uses for performance monitoring and security tools, attackers may abuse this capability to intercept network traffic, capture credentials, or establish covert communication channels. By attaching eBPF filters to network interfaces, adversaries can potentially sniff sensitive data including authentication credentials as they traverse the network. Monitoring for unexpected eBPF program attachments can help identify potential credential access attempts or network monitoring by unauthorized actors.
name: eBPF Filter Attached to Network Interface
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Behavioral Indicators' and indicator.name in ('eBPFXdpProgramAttach', 'EbpfProgramAttached') and not (src.process.image.path contains ('/opt/microsoft/mdatp/sbin/wdavdaemon', '/opt/datadog-agent/embedded/', '/opt/dynatrace/oneagent/', '/opt/ds_agent/netagent/', ' /opt/aws/amazon-guardduty-agent/', '/opt/carbonblack/', '/Tanium/TaniumClient/'))

description: Detects the loading of eBPF (extended Berkeley Packet Filter) programs into the Linux kernel. eBPF allows for dynamic code execution within the kernel space, providing powerful capabilities for monitoring and manipulating system behavior. While eBPF has legitimate uses for performance monitoring and networking, adversaries may abuse this functionality to establish persistence, bypass security controls, or monitor sensitive system activities. By loading malicious eBPF programs, attackers can intercept system calls, hide processes, redirect network traffic, or exfiltrate sensitive data while operating with kernel-level privileges. This detection focuses on identifying potentially unauthorized or suspicious eBPF module loading, which could indicate an attempt to gain persistent access to the system through kernel-level modifications.
name: eBPF Program Loaded into Kernel
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Behavioral Indicators' and indicator.name in ('SuspiciousEbpfProgramLoad', 'eBPFProgramLoad') and not (src.process.name in ('systemd', 'init', 'cilium-agent', 'runc', 'crun', 'snap-confine', 'system-probe', 'wdavdaemon', 'amazon-guardduty-agent', 'snap', 'oneagentebpfdiscovery', 'wiz-sensor', 'bpftool')) and src.process.parent.cmdline = *

description: Detects the creation or modification of ClusterRoleBindings in Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) that grant anonymous access to the cluster. Anonymous access permissions allow unauthenticated users to interact with the Kubernetes API server, potentially leading to unauthorized access and privilege escalation within the cluster. Adversaries may exploit this configuration to gain initial access to the cluster, perform reconnaissance activities, or execute further attacks against cluster resources. Monitoring these permission changes is critical to maintaining proper authentication controls and preventing unauthorized access to your Kubernetes environment.
name: EKS Anonymous Access Granted to Cluster
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and http_request.http_method in ('create', 'patch', 'update') and resources.type = 'clusterrolebindings' and unmapped.requestObject.subjects contains '"User","name":"system:anonymous"'

description: Detects the creation of ClusterRoleBinding resources within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. ClusterRoleBindings are Kubernetes resources that associate users, groups, or service accounts with ClusterRoles, granting cluster-wide permissions that span across all namespaces. While creating ClusterRoleBindings is necessary for legitimate administrative purposes, unauthorized or suspicious bindings could indicate privilege escalation attempts. Adversaries may create ClusterRoleBindings to grant themselves or compromised service accounts elevated permissions, enabling unauthorized access to sensitive resources, data exfiltration, or further lateral movement within the cluster. Monitoring the creation of ClusterRoleBindings is essential for identifying potential privilege escalation paths and ensuring proper implementation of role-based access control (RBAC) within your Kubernetes environment.
name: EKS ClusterRoleBinding Created
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method = 'create' and resources.type = 'clusterrolebindings' and unmapped.annotations.authorization_k8s_io_decision = 'allow' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):')

description: Detects the deletion or modification of ClusterRoleBinding resources within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. ClusterRoleBindings are critical Kubernetes resources that link users, groups, or service accounts to cluster-wide permission sets defined in ClusterRoles. Unauthorized changes to these bindings could indicate privilege escalation attempts or efforts to disrupt security controls. Adversaries may modify existing ClusterRoleBindings to grant themselves elevated permissions, remove legitimate administrative access, or create backdoor access paths within the cluster. Similarly, deletion of ClusterRoleBindings could be used to deny service to legitimate administrators or remove evidence of previous modifications. Monitoring these events helps maintain the integrity of your cluster's role-based access control (RBAC) system and detect potential security breaches in your Kubernetes environment.
name: EKS ClusterRoleBinding Deleted or Modified
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('delete', 'patch', 'update') and resources.type = 'clusterrolebindings' and unmapped.annotations.authorization_k8s_io_decision = 'allow' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):')

description: Detects the creation of ClusterRole resources within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. ClusterRoles are Kubernetes resources that define permissions across the entire cluster, not limited to a specific namespace. While creating ClusterRoles is a standard administrative operation, unauthorized or overly permissive ClusterRoles could lead to privilege escalation or unauthorized access to sensitive cluster resources. Adversaries may create ClusterRoles to establish persistent elevated access, bypass namespace restrictions, or gain the ability to access or modify critical cluster components. Monitoring ClusterRole creation helps security teams identify potential privilege escalation paths and ensure that role-based access control (RBAC) is properly implemented within the Kubernetes environment.
name: EKS ClusterRole Created
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method = 'create' and resources.type = 'clusterroles' and unmapped.annotations.authorization_k8s_io_decision = 'allow' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):')

description: Detects the deletion or modification of ClusterRole resources within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. ClusterRoles define sets of permissions that apply across the entire cluster and are critical components of Kubernetes' role-based access control (RBAC) system. Unauthorized changes to ClusterRoles could indicate privilege escalation attempts or efforts to manipulate security controls. Adversaries may modify existing ClusterRoles to expand permissions, enabling unauthorized access to sensitive resources or operations within the cluster. Similarly, deletion of ClusterRoles could disrupt legitimate administrative functions or be used to remove evidence of previous modifications. Monitoring these events is essential for maintaining the integrity of your cluster's permission structure and detecting potential security breaches that could lead to unauthorized access or control of your Kubernetes environment.
name: EKS ClusterRole Deleted or Modified
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('delete', 'patch', 'update') and resources.type = 'clusterroles' and unmapped.annotations.authorization_k8s_io_decision = 'allow' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):')

description: Detects creation of ConfigMaps within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. ConfigMaps are used to store non-confidential data in key-value pairs, making them an essential part of application configuration in Kubernetes. Monitoring the creation of ConfigMaps helps security teams ensure that only authorized changes are made to application configurations and that sensitive information is not inadvertently exposed. An attacker who can create a ConfigMap could potentially inject malicious configuration into an application, leading to a range of security consequences including code execution, lateral movement, and data exfiltration.
name: EKS ConfigMap Created
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and http_request.http_method = 'create' and resources.type = 'configmaps' and not (unmapped.responseStatus.status = 'Failure')

description: Detects modification or deletion of ConfigMaps within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. ConfigMaps are used to store non-confidential data in key-value pairs, making them an essential part of application configuration in Kubernetes. Monitoring changes to ConfigMaps helps security teams ensure that only authorized modifications are made to application configurations. An attacker who can modify or delete ConfigMaps could potentially disrupt application functionality, inject malicious configuration values, or cause denial of service conditions. By altering existing ConfigMaps, adversaries may redirect application connections to attacker-controlled endpoints, modify security settings, or manipulate application behavior to facilitate further compromise. Deletion of critical ConfigMaps could render applications non-functional or cause them to fall back to potentially insecure default configurations. Tracking these activities is crucial for maintaining the integrity and security of Kubernetes-based applications.
name: EKS ConfigMap Modification or Deletion
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('delete', 'patch', 'update') and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and resources.type = 'configmaps'

description: Detects the creation or modification of Kubernetes resources with host namespace access within Amazon EKS clusters. This detection specifically monitors for pods, deployments, daemonsets, jobs, replicasets, statefulsets, cronjobs, and replication controllers where hostIPC=true, hostPID=true, or hostNetwork=true has been configured. These privileged settings effectively break container isolation by allowing containers to: (1) access the host's inter-process communication mechanisms (hostIPC), (2) view and interact with all processes running on the host system (hostPID), or (3) directly access the host's network interfaces and bind to privileged ports (hostNetwork). Adversaries leverage these configurations to escape container boundaries, perform lateral movement, access sensitive information on the host, intercept network traffic, or manipulate host processes. This technique aligns with MITRE ATT&CK's Privilege Escalation and Container Escape tactics and represents a critical security risk that could lead to complete host compromise. This detection excludes system-initiated changes to focus on potentially unauthorized or malicious activity.
name: EKS Container Created with Host Namespace Access
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('create', 'update', 'patch') and ((unmapped.responseObject.spec.template.spec.hostIPC = true or unmapped.requestObject.spec.template.spec.hostIPC = true or unmapped.responseObject.spec.hostIPC = true or unmapped.responseObject.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.hostIPC = true) or (unmapped.responseObject.spec.template.spec.hostPID = true or unmapped.requestObject.spec.template.spec.hostPID = true or unmapped.responseObject.spec.hostPID = true or unmapped.responseObject.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.hostPID = true) or (unmapped.responseObject.spec.template.spec.hostNetwork = true or unmapped.requestObject.spec.template.spec.hostNetwork = true or unmapped.responseObject.spec.hostNetwork = true or unmapped.responseObject.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.hostNetwork = true)) and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):')

description: Detects the creation or modification of pods with privileged security context within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. When a container is configured with privileged: true, it receives nearly all the same capabilities as processes running on the host, effectively disabling most container security mechanisms. Privileged containers can access host devices, modify kernel parameters, and perform other sensitive operations that break the isolation guarantees of containerization. Adversaries may exploit privileged containers to escape to the host system, escalate privileges, modify host configurations, or gain persistent access to the underlying infrastructure. While some system utilities and administrative tools legitimately require privileged access, the excessive permissions granted pose a significant security risk if compromised. Monitoring for privileged container deployments helps security teams identify potential container escape vectors and maintain proper isolation boundaries within Kubernetes environments.
name: EKS Container Launched as Root
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('create', 'update', 'patch') and (unmapped.responseObject.spec.template.spec.containers contains '"runAsUser":0' or unmapped.responseObject.spec.containers contains '"runAsUser":0' or unmapped.responseObject.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.containers contains '"runAsUser":0') and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):')

description: Detects the mounting of a ServiceAccount token into a container within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. ServiceAccounts are used to provide an identity for processes that run in a Pod, allowing containers to authenticate to the Kubernetes API server. While mounting ServiceAccount tokens is a standard practice for applications that need to interact with the Kubernetes API, it can also be abused by attackers for privilege escalation. Adversaries may deploy containers with overly permissive ServiceAccounts to gain unauthorized access to cluster resources, perform reconnaissance activities, or make unauthorized API calls. By monitoring ServiceAccount token mounting, security teams can identify potential privilege escalation paths, ensure proper RBAC controls are in place, and detect when containers are granted excessive permissions that could be exploited in an attack. This detection is particularly important as compromised containers with mounted ServiceAccount tokens could allow lateral movement throughout the Kubernetes cluster.
name: EKS Container with ServiceAccount Mounted
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and http_request.http_method = 'create' and (unmapped.responseObject.spec.template.spec.serviceAccountName = *  or unmapped.responseObject.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.serviceAccountName = * or unmapped.responseObject.spec.serviceAccountName = *) and not (unmapped.responseStatus.status = 'Failure')

description: Detects when containers in an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster mount critical host paths. These sensitive paths include container runtime sockets, system directories, configuration files, and device files that could provide excessive privileges to containers. Mounting these paths can enable container escape techniques, allowing attackers to break out of container isolation and gain access to the underlying host system. This activity may indicate a privilege escalation attempt, where an attacker is trying to leverage container access to compromise the host or other workloads in the cluster. By monitoring for these mounts, security teams can identify potential security threats and take action to enforce proper container isolation and least privilege principles in their Kubernetes environments.
name: EKS Critical Paths Mounted in Container
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' http_request.http_method='create' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and (unmapped.responseObject.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.volumes matches '"hostPath":{"path":"(\\\\\\/var\\\\/run\\\\/docker\\.sock|\\\\/run\\\\/containerd\\\\/containerd\\.sock|\\\\/run\\\\/crio\\\\/crio\\.sock|\\\\/var\\/lib\\/|\\\\/etc\\/|\\\\/root\\\\/|\\\\/var\\\\/log\\\\/|\\\\/proc\\\\/|\\\\/sys\\\\/|\\\\/boot\\\\/|\\\\/var\\\\/run\\\\/secrets\\\\/kubernetes\\.io\\\\/)' or unmapped.requestObject.spec.template.spec.volumes matches '"hostPath":{"path":"(\\\\\\/var\\\\/run\\\\/docker\\.sock|\\\\/run\\\\/containerd\\\\/containerd\\.sock|\\\\/run\\\\/crio\\\\/crio\\.sock|\\\\/var\\/lib\\/|\\\\/etc\\/|\\\\/root\\\\/|\\\\/var\\\\/log\\\\/|\\\\/proc\\\\/|\\\\/sys\\\\/|\\\\/boot\\\\/|\\\\/var\\\\/run\\\\/secrets\\\\/kubernetes\\.io\\\\/)' or unmapped.responseObject.spec.volumes matches '"hostPath":{"path":"(\\\\\\/var\\\\/run\\\\/docker\\.sock|\\\\/run\\\\/containerd\\\\/containerd\\.sock|\\\\/run\\\\/crio\\\\/crio\\.sock|\\\\/var\\/lib\\/|\\\\/etc\\/|\\\\/root\\\\/|\\\\/var\\\\/log\\\\/|\\\\/proc\\\\/|\\\\/sys\\\\/|\\\\/boot\\\\/|\\\\/var\\\\/run\\\\/secrets\\\\/kubernetes\\.io\\\\/)' or unmapped.requestObject.spec.template.spec.containers matches '"hostPath":{"path":"(\\\\\\/var\\\\/run\\\\/docker\\.sock|\\\\/run\\\\/containerd\\\\/containerd\\.sock|\\\\/run\\\\/crio\\\\/crio\\.sock|\\\\/var\\/lib\\/|\\\\/etc\\/|\\\\/root\\\\/|\\\\/var\\\\/log\\\\/|\\\\/proc\\\\/|\\\\/sys\\\\/|\\\\/boot\\\\/|\\\\/var\\\\/run\\\\/secrets\\\\/kubernetes\\.io\\\\/)')

description: Detects the creation of CronJob resources within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. CronJobs are Kubernetes resources that manage time-based scheduled tasks within the cluster. While CronJob creation is a routine operation, unauthorized creation of these resources could lead to establishment of persistent access or execution of malicious code. Adversaries may create CronJobs to establish persistence, schedule data exfiltration tasks, or execute malicious workloads at regular intervals. By creating new CronJobs, attackers can maintain access to the cluster, execute privileged commands, or schedule recurring malicious activities. Monitoring these events can help identify potential security threats and ensure the integrity of your Kubernetes environment.
name: EKS CronJob Created
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method = 'create' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and unmapped.requestObject.kind = 'CronJob'

description: Detects the deletion or modification of CronJob resources within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. CronJobs are Kubernetes resources that manage time-based scheduled tasks within the cluster. While CronJob modifications are routine operations, unauthorized changes to these resources could lead to disruption of critical scheduled operations or introduction of malicious code execution. Adversaries may target CronJobs to disable security scanning routines, establish persistence through scheduled malicious tasks, or interfere with operational maintenance jobs. By modifying existing CronJobs or deleting critical scheduling resources, attackers can impact system reliability, introduce backdoors, or conceal their malicious activities. Monitoring these events can help identify potential security threats and ensure the integrity of your Kubernetes environment.
name: EKS CronJob Deleted or Modified
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('delete', 'patch', 'update') and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and resources.type = 'cronjobs'

description: Detects the creation of DaemonSets within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. DaemonSets ensure that specific pods run on all or selected nodes within the cluster, making them powerful but potentially risky when misused. While DaemonSet creation is a legitimate administrative operation, unauthorized deployments could indicate security threats such as cryptomining, privilege escalation, or persistent access establishment. Adversaries may leverage DaemonSets to deploy malicious workloads across all nodes simultaneously, gaining widespread access to host resources. This detection helps security teams monitor DaemonSet creation activities to ensure they align with authorized administrative operations and security policies.
name: EKS Daemonset Created
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method = 'create' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and resources.type = 'daemonsets'

description: Detects modification or deletion of DaemonSets within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. DaemonSets ensure that specific pods run on all or selected nodes within the cluster, making changes to them potentially impactful across the entire environment. While modifying or removing DaemonSets can be legitimate administrative operations, unauthorized changes could indicate security threats such as attempts to disable security controls, disrupt critical infrastructure services, or cover tracks after malicious activities. Adversaries may target DaemonSets to remove security monitoring agents or modify existing workloads to establish persistence. This detection helps security teams monitor DaemonSet modifications to ensure they align with authorized administrative operations and change management processes.
name: EKS Daemonset Deleted or Modified
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('delete', 'patch', 'update') and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and resources.type = 'daemonsets'

description: Detects the creation of deployments within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. While deployment creation is a standard operation in Kubernetes environments, monitoring this activity is important for maintaining visibility into workload changes and cluster modifications. Unauthorized or suspicious deployments could indicate potential security risks, including the introduction of malicious containers, privilege escalation attempts, or infrastructure manipulation. This detection provides security teams with awareness of deployment activities to ensure they align with expected administrative operations and change management processes.
name: EKS Deployment Created
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method = 'create' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and resources.type = 'deployments'

description: Detects modification or deletion of deployments within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. While updating and removing deployments are standard operations in Kubernetes environments, monitoring these activities is crucial for maintaining visibility into workload changes and cluster modifications. Unauthorized or suspicious deployment modifications/deletions could indicate potential security risks, including tampering with running workloads, service disruption attempts, or malicious infrastructure manipulation. This detection provides security teams with awareness of deployment changes to ensure they align with expected administrative operations and change management processes.
name: EKS Deployment Deleted or Modified
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('delete', 'patch', 'update') and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and resources.type = 'deployments'

description: Detects the use of exec commands into pods within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. The 'exec' subresource allows users to execute commands directly inside running containers, which is useful for debugging and administration. However, this powerful capability can be abused by adversaries who have gained access to the Kubernetes API. Attackers may use exec commands to establish interactive sessions within containers, execute malicious code, access sensitive data, or pivot to other resources within the cluster. By monitoring exec operations, especially those containing specific command parameters, security teams can identify potential unauthorized access attempts and maintain the security posture of containerized workloads in the Kubernetes environment.
name: EKS Exec into Pod
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and resources.type='pods' and unmapped.objectRef.subresource='exec' and http_request.url contains 'command=' and unmapped.annotations.authorization_k8s_io_decision = 'allow'

description: Detects the deletion or modification of HorizontalPodAutoscaler resources within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. HorizontalPodAutoscalers are Kubernetes resources that automatically scale the number of pod replicas based on observed CPU utilization or other select metrics. While configuration changes to HorizontalPodAutoscalers are part of normal operations, unauthorized modifications could lead to resource availability issues or performance degradation. Adversaries may target these resources to disrupt autoscaling functionality, potentially causing service outages during high traffic periods or enabling resource hijacking by manipulating scaling thresholds. By modifying or deleting HorizontalPodAutoscaler configurations, attackers can impact application performance, cause denial of service, or increase cloud resource consumption resulting in financial damage. Monitoring these events helps identify potential security threats and maintain the stability and efficiency of your Kubernetes environment.
name: EKS HorizontalPodAutoscaler Configuration Modified or Deleted
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('delete', 'patch', 'update') and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and resources.type = 'horizontalpodautoscalers'

description: Detects the creation of Job resources within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. Jobs are Kubernetes resources that run containers to perform one-time tasks to completion. While Job creation is a routine operation, unauthorized creation of these resources could lead to execution of malicious code or unauthorized processing tasks. Adversaries may create Jobs to execute short-lived malicious workloads, perform data exfiltration tasks, or establish initial access to the cluster. By creating new Jobs, attackers can execute commands with potentially elevated permissions, process sensitive data, or perform malicious activities that complete and terminate to avoid detection. Jobs are particularly attractive for attackers as they're designed to run to completion and then terminate, potentially leaving minimal evidence of compromise. Monitoring these events can help identify potential security threats and ensure the integrity of your Kubernetes environment.
name: EKS Job Created
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method = 'create' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and unmapped.requestObject.kind = 'Job'

description: Detects the deletion or modification of Job resources within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. Jobs are Kubernetes resources that run containers to perform one-time tasks to completion. While Job modifications are routine operations, unauthorized changes to these resources could lead to disruption of critical batch processing or introduction of malicious code execution. Adversaries may target Jobs to disable security tasks, execute malicious workloads, or interfere with operational processes. By modifying existing Jobs or deleting critical processing resources, attackers can impact system reliability, introduce backdoors, or conceal their malicious activities. Monitoring these events can help identify potential security threats and ensure the integrity of your Kubernetes environment.
name: EKS Job Deleted or Modified
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('delete', 'patch', 'update') and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and resources.type = 'jobs'

description: Detects when LimitRange resources are modified or deleted within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. LimitRanges are Kubernetes resources that constrain resource usage (CPU, memory) for containers and pods within a namespace, preventing resource exhaustion and ensuring fair allocation. When these controls are modified or removed, it could allow for unconstrained resource consumption that impacts cluster stability and availability. Adversaries may deliberately alter LimitRanges as part of a resource consumption attack to enable denial of service conditions or to circumvent restrictions that would otherwise prevent their malicious workloads from consuming excessive resources. By removing or weakening these constraints, attackers can potentially starve legitimate workloads of necessary resources, causing application performance degradation or complete service disruption. This detection is particularly important for maintaining operational stability in multi-tenant Kubernetes environments where resource isolation between workloads is critical for preventing noisy neighbor problems and ensuring consistent application performance.
name: EKS LimitRanges Applied or Updated
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('create', 'patch', 'update', 'delete') and unmapped.annotations.authorization_k8s_io_decision = 'allow' and resources.type='limitranges' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):')

description: Detects multiple forbidden (403) responses from the Kubernetes API server to the same external IP address within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. This pattern indicates potential reconnaissance or discovery attempts where an external actor is probing the cluster for accessible resources or permissions. When external IPs receive multiple authorization failures in a short timeframe, it often signifies systematic attempts to discover accessible Kubernetes resources or identify privilege boundaries. Adversaries may use this technique during the discovery phase to map out the cluster's structure, identify potential vulnerabilities, or find misconfigured access controls. By filtering out internal traffic and focusing on external sources, this detection helps identify potential unauthorized access attempts from outside the cluster network. Monitoring these patterns enables security teams to detect early stages of reconnaissance before more damaging actions can occur.
name: EKS Multiple Forbidden Responses from the Same IP

description: Detects the creation of MutatingWebhookConfiguration resources within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. MutatingWebhooks are powerful admission controllers that can intercept and modify Kubernetes API requests before object persistence. Unlike ValidatingWebhooks, MutatingWebhooks can actually alter the objects being created or updated, making them particularly powerful and potentially dangerous if misused. While these webhooks serve legitimate purposes for policy enforcement and automation, unauthorized or malicious webhooks could be used to inject sidecars, modify container specifications, alter security contexts, or implement other covert modifications to workloads. An adversary who can create MutatingWebhooks could potentially modify any matching API request to inject malicious containers, credentials, or configurations, leading to widespread compromise. Monitoring the creation of these resources helps ensure that only authorized mutation mechanisms are implemented in the cluster.
name: EKS MutatingWebhook Created
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method = 'create' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and unmapped.responseObject.kind = 'MutatingWebhookConfiguration'

description: Detects the deletion or modification of MutatingWebhookConfiguration resources within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. MutatingWebhooks are powerful admission controllers that can intercept and modify Kubernetes API requests before object persistence. Modifications to these webhooks could indicate an attempt to alter their behavior, change their scope, or disable them entirely. Adversaries may target MutatingWebhooks to modify existing security controls, remove mutation logic that enforces security policies, or alter webhook configurations to exclude certain namespaces or resources from inspection. Given that MutatingWebhooks can modify objects being created or updated in the cluster, tampering with these resources could have significant security implications, potentially allowing deployment of non-compliant workloads or bypassing of security controls. Monitoring these events helps maintain the integrity of your Kubernetes admission control system and detect potential security policy bypasses.
name: EKS MutatingWebhook Deleted or Modified
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('delete', 'patch', 'update') and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and unmapped.responseObject.kind = 'MutatingWebhookConfiguration'

description: Detects the creation of new namespaces within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. Namespaces provide isolation and organization for Kubernetes resources, allowing teams to work in separate virtual clusters within the same physical cluster. While namespace creation is a standard administrative operation, unauthorized creation could indicate an attempt to establish a foothold within the cluster or evade detection by operating in an unmonitored area. Adversaries may create new namespaces to deploy malicious workloads, establish persistence, or isolate their activities from security controls focused on default or known namespaces. Monitoring namespace creation helps security teams maintain visibility across the entire cluster and identify potentially unauthorized expansion of the Kubernetes environment.
name: EKS Namespace Created
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('create', 'patch', 'update') and unmapped.annotations.authorization_k8s_io_decision = 'allow' and resources.type = 'namespaces' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):')

description: Detects the deletion of namespaces within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. Namespaces provide isolation and organization for Kubernetes resources, and their deletion results in the removal of all resources contained within them. While namespace deletion can be part of normal cluster management, unauthorized or unexpected deletions could indicate destructive activity aimed at disrupting services or removing evidence. Adversaries may delete namespaces to cause widespread application outages, destroy critical workloads, or eliminate traces of their activities within the cluster. Monitoring namespace deletion helps security teams identify potential service disruption attempts and maintain operational integrity across the Kubernetes environment.
name: EKS Namespace Deleted
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method = 'delete' and unmapped.annotations.authorization_k8s_io_decision = 'allow' and resources.type = 'namespaces' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):')

description: Detects the creation or modification of ClusterRole resources with overly permissive permissions within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. These widely-scoped ClusterRoles contain wildcards in their resource definitions or verb specifications, granting broad access across the cluster. While administrative roles sometimes require extensive permissions, overly permissive ClusterRoles can pose significant security risks if assigned inappropriately. Adversaries may create these roles as part of privilege escalation attempts, enabling them to access sensitive resources, modify critical configurations, or perform unauthorized operations throughout the cluster. Such broad permissions violate the principle of least privilege and can significantly expand the attack surface of your Kubernetes environment. Monitoring the creation of widely-scoped ClusterRoles helps security teams identify potential privilege escalation paths and ensure proper implementation of role-based access control (RBAC) within your EKS cluster.
name: EKS Overly Permissive ClusterRole
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('create', 'patch', 'update') and resources.type = 'clusterroles' and unmapped.annotations.authorization_k8s_io_decision = 'allow' and (unmapped.responseObject.rules matches 'resources":\\\["\\\*' or unmapped.responseObject.rules matches 'verbs":\\\["\\\*') and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):')

description: Detects the deletion or modification of PersistentVolume (PV) or PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) resources within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. PVs and PVCs are Kubernetes resources that provide persistent storage for containerized applications. While PV/PVC modifications are routine operations, unauthorized changes to these resources could lead to data loss, service disruption, or compromise of sensitive information. Adversaries may target storage resources to disrupt application availability, destroy persistent data, or gain access to sensitive information stored in volumes. By modifying existing PVs/PVCs or deleting critical storage resources, attackers can cause application failures, data corruption, or permanent data loss. This detection specifically excludes system and EKS-managed service actions to reduce noise. Monitoring these events can help identify potential security threats targeting your cluster's data persistence layer and ensure the integrity of your Kubernetes storage environment.
name: EKS PersistentVolume or PersistentVolumeClaim Modified or Deleted
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('delete', 'patch', 'update') and resources.type in ('persistentvolumeclaims', 'persistentvolumes') and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and unmapped.annotations.authorization_k8s_io_decision = 'allow'

description: Detects the creation or modification of pods with AppArmor profiles set to 'Unconfined' within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. AppArmor is a Linux security module that restricts program capabilities through mandatory access control profiles, providing an additional layer of container isolation. When AppArmor is set to 'Unconfined', containers run without these security constraints, allowing them unrestricted access to system resources within their permission scope. Adversaries may intentionally disable AppArmor to remove security barriers that would otherwise prevent exploitation of vulnerabilities, container escapes, or access to sensitive host resources. Without AppArmor restrictions, malicious containers have greater freedom to execute unauthorized system calls, access file systems, or perform other potentially harmful operations. Monitoring for disabled AppArmor profiles helps security teams identify containers running with reduced security controls and potential privilege escalation vectors within Kubernetes environments.
name: EKS Pod with AppArmor Disabled
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('create', 'update', 'patch') and (unmapped.responseObject.spec.template.spec.containers contains '"appArmorProfile":{"type":"Unconfined"}}' or unmapped.responseObject.spec.containers contains '"appArmorProfile":{"type":"Unconfined"}}' or unmapped.responseObject.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.containers contains '"appArmorProfile":{"type":"Unconfined"}}') and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):')

description: Detects the creation or modification of Kubernetes resources in Amazon EKS clusters that include sensitive credentials or secrets passed directly as environment variables. This insecure practice exposes credentials within container configurations, making them accessible through API calls, logs, or to anyone with access to the cluster's configuration. Credentials commonly exposed include AWS access keys, database passwords, API tokens, and private keys. Adversaries may deliberately configure containers with plaintext credentials to establish persistence, facilitate lateral movement, or enable data exfiltration. These exposed credentials can be extracted and leveraged to escalate privileges both within the Kubernetes cluster and in connected systems or cloud environments. Monitoring for this pattern helps identify credential exposure risks and enforce proper secret management practices within your Kubernetes environment.
name: EKS Possible Secret Passed via Environment Variable in Container
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('create', 'patch', 'update') and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and (unmapped.responseObject.spec.containers matches '"env":\\[.*{"name":"(AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID|AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY|AWS_ACCESS_KEY|ECS_ENGINE_AUTH_DATA|AWS_SESSION_TOKEN|AWS_SECURITY_TOKEN|DB_PASSWORD|MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD|POSTGRES_PASSWORD|MONGO_INITDB_ROOT_PASSWORD|REDIS_PASSWORD|PRIVATE_KEY|GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS|AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET|SLACK_TOKEN|SLACK_BOT_TOKEN)' or unmapped.responseObject.spec.template.spec.containers matches '"env":\\[.*{"name":"(AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID|AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY|AWS_ACCESS_KEY|ECS_ENGINE_AUTH_DATA|AWS_SESSION_TOKEN|AWS_SECURITY_TOKEN|DB_PASSWORD|MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD|POSTGRES_PASSWORD|MONGO_INITDB_ROOT_PASSWORD|REDIS_PASSWORD|PRIVATE_KEY|GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS|AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET|SLACK_TOKEN|SLACK_BOT_TOKEN)' or unmapped.responseObject.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.containers matches '"env":\\[.*{"name":"(AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID|AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY|AWS_ACCESS_KEY|ECS_ENGINE_AUTH_DATA|AWS_SESSION_TOKEN|AWS_SECURITY_TOKEN|DB_PASSWORD|MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD|POSTGRES_PASSWORD|MONGO_INITDB_ROOT_PASSWORD|REDIS_PASSWORD|PRIVATE_KEY|GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS|AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET|SLACK_TOKEN|SLACK_BOT_TOKEN)') and not (unmapped.responseStatus.status = 'Failure')

description: Detects modifications to PriorityClasses within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. PriorityClasses are Kubernetes resources that determine the scheduling priority of pods, influencing which pods get preferential treatment during resource allocation and eviction decisions. While PriorityClass modifications are sometimes necessary for workload management, unauthorized changes could lead to service disruption or resource starvation. Adversaries may target PriorityClasses to conduct denial of service attacks by manipulating scheduling priorities, causing critical workloads to be preempted or preventing them from being scheduled. By modifying existing PriorityClasses or creating malicious ones with extremely high values, attackers can impact cluster stability, disrupt business operations, or create resource contention that affects application availability. Monitoring these events can help identify potential impact-oriented attacks and ensure the integrity of your Kubernetes scheduling system.
name: EKS PriorityClasses Updated
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('delete', 'patch', 'update', 'create') and resources.type = 'priorityclasses' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and unmapped.annotations.authorization_k8s_io_decision = 'allow'

description: Detects the creation of privileged containers within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. Privileged containers have elevated access to the host system, including access to all devices and the ability to perform operations that would otherwise be restricted by the container runtime. While privileged containers may be necessary for specific administrative tasks, they represent a significant security risk as they can potentially escape container isolation and access the underlying host. Adversaries may exploit privileged containers to escalate privileges, access sensitive host resources, modify system configurations, or establish persistence on the host system. Monitoring for the creation of privileged containers helps security teams identify potential security policy violations and container escape risks within Kubernetes environments.
name: EKS Privileged Container Spawned
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('create', 'patch', 'update') and unmapped.annotations.authorization_k8s_io_decision = 'allow' and (unmapped.responseObject.spec.containers contains '"privileged":true' or unmapped.responseObject.spec.template.spec.containers contains '"privileged":true' or unmapped.responseObject.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.containers contains '"privileged":true' ) and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):')

description: Detects the creation of ReplicaSet resources within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. ReplicaSets are Kubernetes resources that maintain a stable set of replica Pods running at any given time. While ReplicaSet creation is a routine operation, unauthorized creation of these resources could lead to deployment of malicious workloads or resource abuse. Adversaries may create ReplicaSets to deploy unauthorized containers, establish persistence, or execute malicious code within the cluster. By creating new ReplicaSets, attackers can introduce containers that perform lateral movement, data exfiltration, or resource abuse, potentially evading detection by blending in with legitimate workloads. Monitoring these events can help identify potential security threats and ensure the integrity of your Kubernetes environment.
name: EKS ReplicaSet Created
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method = 'create' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and unmapped.requestObject.kind = 'ReplicaSet'

description: Detects the deletion or modification of ReplicaSet resources within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. ReplicaSets are Kubernetes resources that maintain a stable set of replica Pods running at any given time. While ReplicaSet modifications are routine operations, unauthorized changes to these resources could lead to disruption of application availability or introduction of malicious workloads. Adversaries may target ReplicaSets to disable running services, deploy compromised containers, or manipulate the desired state of applications. By modifying existing ReplicaSets or deleting critical resources, attackers can cause denial of service, deploy backdoored containers, or evade detection. Monitoring these events can help identify potential security threats and ensure the integrity of your Kubernetes environment.
name: EKS ReplicaSet Deleted or Modified
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('delete', 'patch', 'update') and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and resources.type = 'replicasets'

description: Detects the creation of ReplicationController resources within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. ReplicationControllers are Kubernetes resources that ensure a specified number of pod replicas are running at any given time. While ReplicationController creation is a routine operation, unauthorized creation of these resources could lead to deployment of malicious workloads or resource abuse. Adversaries may create ReplicationControllers to deploy unauthorized containers, establish persistence, or execute malicious code at scale. By creating new ReplicationControllers, attackers can ensure continuous execution of their malicious workloads, maintain presence across node failures, or blend in with legitimate application deployments. Monitoring these events can help identify potential security threats and ensure the integrity of your Kubernetes environment.
name: EKS ReplicationController Created
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method = 'create' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and unmapped.requestObject.kind = 'ReplicationController'

description: Detects the deletion or modification of ReplicationController resources within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. ReplicationControllers are Kubernetes resources that ensure a specified number of pod replicas are running at any given time. While ReplicationController modifications are routine operations, unauthorized changes to these resources could lead to disruption of application availability or introduction of malicious workloads. Adversaries may target ReplicationControllers to disable running services, deploy compromised containers, or manipulate the desired state of applications. By modifying existing ReplicationControllers or deleting critical resources, attackers can cause denial of service, deploy backdoored containers, or evade detection. Monitoring these events can help identify potential security threats and ensure the integrity of your Kubernetes environment.
name: EKS ReplicationController Deleted or Modified
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('delete', 'patch', 'update') and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and resources.type = 'replicationcontrollers'

description: Detects when an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster allows API requests from anonymous users. This rule fires once per day to highlight persistent security misconfigurations across the clusters. Anonymous access to Kubernetes API servers enables unauthenticated users to interact with the cluster without credentials, creating a significant initial access risk. Attackers can potentially query cluster information, discover resources, or attempt to exploit vulnerabilities without first needing to compromise valid credentials. While this configuration may exist in some development environments, it represents a critical security risk in production clusters. The daily alert cadence ensures continued visibility into this misconfiguration until it's remediated, helping security teams maintain proper authentication controls for Kubernetes environments.
name: EKS Request Allowed from Anonymous User
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and unmapped.annotations.authorization_k8s_io_decision = 'allow' and actor.user.name contains 'system:anonymous'

description: Detects the creation of Kubernetes resources in reserved namespaces within Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) clusters. Reserved namespaces such as 'kube-system' and 'kube-public' are designed for core Kubernetes components and system-level resources that are critical for cluster operations. Creating custom resources in these namespaces can interfere with cluster functionality and represents a common defense evasion technique. Adversaries may target these reserved namespaces to hide malicious workloads, as they often receive less scrutiny than application namespaces and may have elevated privileges within the cluster. By deploying resources in system namespaces, attackers can potentially evade detection tools configured to monitor only user namespaces, establish persistence, or gain access to sensitive cluster components. This detection specifically excludes system-initiated changes and legitimate event resources to focus on potentially unauthorized activities. Monitoring for unauthorized resource creation in reserved namespaces helps security teams identify potential defense evasion techniques and maintain the integrity of Kubernetes system components.
name: EKS Resource Creation in Reserved Namespace
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and http_request.http_method = 'create' and unmapped.responseObject.metadata.namespace in ('kube-system', 'kube-public') and unmapped.requestObject.kind != 'Event'

description: Detects the creation of ResourceQuota objects within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. ResourceQuotas are Kubernetes objects that provide constraints on resource consumption per namespace, limiting the quantity of objects that can be created and the amount of compute resources that may be consumed. While ResourceQuotas are legitimate tools for resource management, unexpected or unauthorized creation of these objects could indicate an attempt to implement resource constraints that might lead to denial of service conditions. An adversary might create overly restrictive ResourceQuotas to limit critical namespace functionality, preventing applications from scaling during high demand or blocking the creation of essential resources. Monitoring ResourceQuota creation helps ensure that only authorized resource constraints are implemented and prevents potential availability impacts to production workloads.
name: EKS ResourceQuota Created
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method = 'create' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and resources.type = 'resourcequotas'

description: Detects the deletion or modification of ResourceQuota objects within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. ResourceQuotas enforce constraints on resource consumption within namespaces, limiting the quantity of objects and compute resources that can be used. Modifications to ResourceQuotas could indicate an attempt to alter resource constraints, potentially allowing for resource exhaustion attacks or bypassing established resource governance policies. Adversaries may target ResourceQuotas to remove limitations that prevent resource abuse, enable denial of service conditions through excessive resource consumption, or prepare for deploying resource-intensive malicious workloads. By removing or weakening these controls, attackers could potentially consume excessive cluster resources, causing performance degradation or service disruption to legitimate workloads. Monitoring these events helps maintain proper resource governance and prevent potential resource-based attacks.
name: EKS ResourceQuota Deleted or Modified
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('delete', 'patch', 'update') and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and resources.type = 'resourcequotas'

description: Detects the creation of RoleBinding resources within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. RoleBindings are Kubernetes resources that associate users, groups, or service accounts with Roles, granting permissions within specific namespaces. While creating RoleBindings is necessary for legitimate administrative purposes, unauthorized or suspicious bindings could indicate privilege escalation attempts. Adversaries may create RoleBindings to grant themselves or compromised service accounts elevated permissions within targeted namespaces, enabling unauthorized access to sensitive resources, data exfiltration, or further lateral movement within the cluster. Unlike ClusterRoleBindings, RoleBindings are namespace-scoped, making them more targeted but still potentially dangerous if misused. Monitoring the creation of RoleBindings is essential for identifying potential privilege escalation paths and ensuring proper implementation of role-based access control (RBAC) within your Kubernetes environment.
name: EKS RoleBinding Created
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method = 'create' and resources.type = 'rolebindings' and unmapped.annotations.authorization_k8s_io_decision = 'allow' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):')

description: Detects the deletion or modification of RoleBinding resources within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. RoleBindings are Kubernetes resources that link users, groups, or service accounts to permission sets defined in Roles within specific namespaces. Unauthorized changes to these bindings could indicate privilege escalation attempts or efforts to disrupt security controls. Adversaries may modify existing RoleBindings to grant themselves elevated permissions, remove legitimate administrative access, or create backdoor access paths within targeted namespaces. Similarly, deletion of RoleBindings could be used to deny service to legitimate administrators or remove evidence of previous modifications. Unlike changes to ClusterRoleBindings, these modifications are namespace-scoped, making them more targeted but still potentially dangerous if misused. Monitoring these events helps maintain the integrity of your cluster's role-based access control (RBAC) system and detect potential security breaches in your Kubernetes environment.
name: EKS RoleBinding Deleted or Modified
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('delete', 'patch', 'update') and resources.type = 'rolebindings' and unmapped.annotations.authorization_k8s_io_decision = 'allow' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):')

description: Detects the creation of a pod in an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster with seccomp disabled. Disabling seccomp removes kernel syscall filtering, which increases the attack surface and exposes the cluster to risks such as container escape attempts and privilege escalation. Adversaries may deliberately configure pods this way to bypass runtime restrictions and execute malicious system calls directly on the host kernel. This behavior is uncommon in legitimate workloads and should be treated as highly suspicious, as it may indicate an attempt to weaken container isolation and security boundaries.
name: EKS Seccomp Disabled in Container
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('create', 'update', 'patch') and ( unmapped.responseObject.spec.securityContext.seccompProfile.type = 'Unconfined' or unmapped.responseObject.spec.template.spec.securityContext.seccompProfile.type='Unconfined' or unmapped.responseObject.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.securityContext.seccompProfile.type='Unconfined') and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):')

description: Detects the creation or modification of Kubernetes Secrets within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. Secrets are Kubernetes resources designed to store and manage sensitive information such as passwords, tokens, or keys. While Secret creation and modification are routine operations, unauthorized access to these resources could lead to credential theft, privilege escalation, or service compromise. Adversaries may target Secrets to obtain authentication material, API tokens, or service credentials that can be leveraged to further their access within the cluster or connected services. Monitoring Secret operations helps security teams maintain visibility into sensitive resource changes and identify potentially unauthorized access to credential material within Kubernetes environments.
name: EKS Secret Created or Modified
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and resources.type = 'secrets' and unmapped.annotations.authorization_k8s_io_decision = 'allow' and http_request.http_method in ('create', 'update', 'patch') and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):')

description: Detects the deletion of Kubernetes Secrets within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. Secrets store sensitive information such as credentials, tokens, and certificates that are critical for application functionality. While Secret deletion can be part of normal administrative operations, unauthorized removal of these resources may indicate malicious activity aimed at disrupting services or causing application downtime. Adversaries might delete Secrets to impact availability of dependent applications, force fallback authentication mechanisms, or as part of covering tracks after credential theft. Monitoring Secret deletion activities helps security teams identify potential service disruption attempts and maintain operational continuity within Kubernetes environments.
name: EKS Secret Deleted
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and resources.type = 'secrets' and unmapped.annotations.authorization_k8s_io_decision = 'allow' and http_request.http_method = 'delete' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):')

description: Detects service account activity originating from public IP addresses within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. This rule specifically monitors for system or EKS service accounts performing sensitive operations (create, delete, update, patch) from non-private IP ranges. Service accounts in Kubernetes are designed to provide identities for processes running in pods and should typically operate from within the cluster network. Activity from public IP addresses may indicate compromised credentials, token theft, or unauthorized access to the Kubernetes API server. Adversaries who obtain service account tokens can leverage these privileged identities to perform malicious actions while appearing legitimate to the cluster's authorization systems. By filtering out expected private IP ranges, this detection focuses on potentially suspicious external access that could represent defense evasion techniques using valid but compromised service account credentials.
name: EKS Service Account Activity from Public IP
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('delete', 'patch', 'update', 'create') and actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):' and unmapped.sourceIPs != '[::1]' and unmapped.sourceIPs != '[0.0.0.0]' and not (unmapped.sourceIPs matches '\\[10\\.') and not (unmapped.sourceIPs matches '\\[127\\.0\\.') and not (unmapped.sourceIPs matches '\\[100\\.\\d{2}\\.') and not (unmapped.sourceIPs matches '\\[100\\.127\\.') and not (unmapped.sourceIPs matches '\\[192\\.168\\.') and not (unmapped.sourceIPs matches '\\[172\\.(1[6-9]|2[0-9]|3[01])\\.')

description: Detects when a service account is bound to the cluster-admin role within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. The cluster-admin role provides unrestricted access to all resources across the entire cluster, granting virtually unlimited permissions. While administrative access is sometimes necessary, binding service accounts to the cluster-admin role represents a significant security risk. Adversaries may exploit over-privileged service accounts to escalate privileges, access sensitive resources, modify critical configurations, or execute unauthorized operations throughout the cluster. This technique is commonly used during post-exploitation phases to gain full control over the Kubernetes environment. Monitoring the creation or modification of ClusterRoleBindings associated with the cluster-admin role helps security teams identify potential privilege escalation paths and enforce the principle of least privilege within your EKS cluster.
name: EKS Service Account Bound to Cluster-Admin Role
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('create', 'patch', 'update') and resources.type = 'clusterrolebindings' and unmapped.responseObject.roleRef.name = 'cluster-admin' and unmapped.annotations.authorization_k8s_io_decision = 'allow' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):')

description: Detects the creation of ServiceAccount resources within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. ServiceAccounts provide identities for processes running in pods, allowing containers to authenticate to the Kubernetes API server with specific permissions. While ServiceAccount creation is a routine operation, unauthorized creation of these resources could lead to persistence and privilege escalation within the cluster. Adversaries may create ServiceAccounts to establish persistence, bypass authentication controls, or gain unauthorized access to cluster resources. By creating new ServiceAccounts and binding them to permissive roles, attackers can maintain access to the cluster even if their initial entry point is discovered. These accounts can then be used to deploy additional malicious workloads, access sensitive information, or perform actions that circumvent security controls. The detection specifically excludes system and EKS-managed ServiceAccounts to reduce noise and focus on potentially suspicious activity. Monitoring ServiceAccount creation helps security teams identify potential security threats and ensure proper identity management within the Kubernetes environment.
name: EKS ServiceAccount Created
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and http_request.http_method = 'create' and unmapped.responseObject.kind = 'ServiceAccount' and not (unmapped.responseStatus.status = 'Failure')

description: Detects the deletion or modification of ServiceAccount resources within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. ServiceAccounts provide identities for processes running in pods, allowing containers to authenticate to the Kubernetes API server with specific permissions. While ServiceAccount modifications are routine operations, unauthorized changes to these resources could lead to disruption of workloads or security control bypasses. Adversaries may target ServiceAccounts to disable security monitoring, remove authentication constraints, or modify permissions to facilitate further attacks. By modifying existing ServiceAccounts or deleting critical ones, attackers can cause application failures, bypass access controls, or remove audit trails that would detect their activities. The detection specifically excludes system and EKS-managed ServiceAccount changes to reduce noise. Monitoring these events can help identify potential security threats and ensure the integrity of your Kubernetes environment's identity and access management configuration.
name: EKS ServiceAccount Modification or Deletion
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and http_request.http_method in ('delete', 'patch', 'update') and unmapped.responseObject.kind = 'ServiceAccount' and not (unmapped.responseStatus.status = 'Failure')

description: Detects the creation of Service resources within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. Services are Kubernetes resources that define a logical set of Pods and a policy by which to access them. While Service creation is a routine operation, unauthorized creation of these resources could lead to exposure of malicious workloads or unauthorized network access. Adversaries may create Services to expose backdoor access points, establish command and control channels, or facilitate lateral movement within the cluster. By creating new Services, attackers can route traffic to malicious pods, bypass network segmentation controls, or create persistent access points to compromised resources. Monitoring these events can help identify potential security threats and ensure the integrity of your Kubernetes environment.
name: EKS Service Created
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method = 'create' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and unmapped.requestObject.kind = 'Service'

description: Detects the deletion or modification of Service resources within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. Services are Kubernetes resources that define a logical set of Pods and a policy by which to access them. While Service modifications are routine operations, unauthorized changes to these resources could lead to service disruption or network traffic redirection. Adversaries may target Services to disrupt application availability, redirect traffic to malicious endpoints, or prevent legitimate access to resources. By modifying existing Services or deleting critical service definitions, attackers can cause denial of service conditions, intercept network traffic, or isolate components from communication. Monitoring these events can help identify potential security threats and ensure the integrity of your Kubernetes environment.
name: EKS Service Deleted or Modified
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('delete', 'patch', 'update') and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and resources.type = 'services'

description: Detects the creation of StatefulSet resources within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. StatefulSets are Kubernetes resources that manage stateful applications, ensuring persistent identity and storage for each Pod instance. While StatefulSet creation is a routine operation, unauthorized creation of these resources could lead to deployment of persistent malicious workloads or unauthorized access to storage resources. Adversaries may create StatefulSets to establish persistent backdoors, deploy malicious applications with stable network identities, or gain access to persistent storage volumes. By creating new StatefulSets, attackers can maintain presence across pod restarts, establish long-lived command and control infrastructure, or exfiltrate data from persistent volumes. The stable network identities of StatefulSets can also enable more sophisticated attacks that rely on predictable addressing. Monitoring these events can help identify potential security threats and ensure the integrity of your Kubernetes environment.
name: EKS StatefulSet Created
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method = 'create' and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and unmapped.requestObject.kind = 'StatefulSet'

description: Detects the deletion or modification of StatefulSet resources within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. StatefulSets are Kubernetes resources that manage stateful applications with unique network identities and persistent storage. While StatefulSet modifications are routine operations, unauthorized changes to these resources could lead to disruption of stateful applications or introduction of malicious code execution. Adversaries may target StatefulSets to compromise data persistence, deploy backdoored containers, or disrupt critical stateful services. By modifying existing StatefulSets or deleting these resources, attackers can cause data loss, service disruption, or gain unauthorized access to persistent volumes. Monitoring these events can help identify potential security threats and ensure the integrity of your Kubernetes environment.
name: EKS StatefulSet Deleted or Modified
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('delete', 'patch', 'update') and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and resources.type = 'statefulsets'

description: Detects when containers are granted high-risk Linux capabilities within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. This rule specifically monitors for containers receiving powerful capabilities such as SYS_ADMIN, SYS_BPF, SYS_MODULE, SYS_PTRACE, or combinations of DAC_OVERRIDE with DAC_READ_SEARCH or NET_ADMIN with NET_RAW. These elevated capabilities can allow containers to break out of their isolation, access host resources, load kernel modules, trace processes, or bypass access controls. While some applications legitimately require specific capabilities, granting these high-risk permissions significantly increases the attack surface and could enable container escape techniques. Adversaries may exploit these capabilities to gain access to the host system, escalate privileges, or compromise the entire cluster. Monitoring for suspicious capability assignments helps security teams identify potential security policy violations and container escape vectors.
name: EKS Suspicious Capabilities Granted to Container
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and unmapped.annotations.authorization_k8s_io_decision = 'allow' and ((unmapped.responseObject.spec.containers matches '"capabilities":{"add":\\[.*("SYS_ADMIN"|"SYS_BPF"|"SYS_MODULE"|"SYS_PTRACE")' or unmapped.responseObject.spec.template.spec.containers matches '"capabilities":{"add":\\[.*("SYS_ADMIN"|"SYS_BPF"|"SYS_MODULE"|"SYS_PTRACE")' or unmapped.responseObject.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.containers matches '"capabilities":{"add":\\[.*("SYS_ADMIN"|"SYS_BPF"|"SYS_MODULE"|"SYS_PTRACE")') or (unmapped.responseObject.spec.containers matches '"capabilities":{"add":\\[.*"DAC_OVERRIDE"' and unmapped.responseObject.spec.containers matches '"capabilities":{"add":\\[.*"DAC_READ_SEARCH"') or (unmapped.responseObject.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.containers matches '"capabilities":{"add":\\[.*"DAC_OVERRIDE"' and unmapped.responseObject.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.containers matches '"capabilities":{"add":\\[.*"DAC_READ_SEARCH"') or (unmapped.responseObject.spec.template.spec.containers matches '"capabilities":{"add":\\[.*"DAC_OVERRIDE"' and unmapped.responseObject.spec.template.spec.containers matches '"capabilities":{"add":\\[.*"DAC_READ_SEARCH"') or (unmapped.responseObject.spec.containers matches '"capabilities":{"add":\\[.*"NET_ADMIN"' and unmapped.responseObject.spec.containers matches '"capabilities":{"add":\\[.*"NET_RAW"') or (unmapped.responseObject.spec.template.spec.containers matches '"capabilities":{"add":\\[.*"NET_ADMIN"' and unmapped.responseObject.spec.template.spec.containers matches '"capabilities":{"add":\\[.*"NET_RAW"') or (unmapped.responseObject.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.containers matches '"capabilities":{"add":\\[.*"NET_ADMIN"' and unmapped.responseObject.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.containers matches '"capabilities":{"add":\\[.*"NET_RAW"')) and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):')

description: Detects when taints are applied to nodes in an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. Node taints are Kubernetes mechanisms that enable operators to repel pods from specific nodes, effectively controlling pod scheduling behavior. While taints serve legitimate purposes in workload management and resource allocation, they can also be manipulated by adversaries as part of sophisticated attack chains. When an attacker applies taints to nodes, they can effectively create 'reserved' nodes that only accept pods with matching tolerations. This technique can be part of a persistence or defense evasion strategy where adversaries first apply taints to target nodes, then deploy specially crafted pods with matching tolerations that can bypass normal scheduling restrictions. These pods may contain privileged containers (T1610) or other malicious workloads designed to execute privileged code on the tainted nodes. By controlling which pods can be scheduled on specific nodes through taint manipulation, attackers can establish persistent access to cluster resources while potentially evading detection by security monitoring focused on standard deployment patterns. This detection is particularly important for maintaining the integrity of node scheduling in Kubernetes environments and preventing adversaries from establishing persistence through taint and toleration mechanisms.
name: EKS Taint Applied to Node
