s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Google Workspace' and metadata.product.name = 'admin' and metadata.log_name = 'DELEGATED_ADMIN_SETTINGS' and event.type = 'Revoke Privileges' and enrichments contains 'ROLE_NAME' and enrichments contains 'PRIVILEGE_NAME'

description: Detects modifications to SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) service provider configurations within Google Workspace. SAML enables single sign-on (SSO) authentication for users across different applications and services. Unauthorized changes to these configurations can lead to security risks, including unauthorized access and potential data breaches. Monitoring for such configuration changes is essential to detect security threats and prevent unauthorized access to sensitive information.
name: Google Workspace SAML Service Provider Configuration Modification
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Google Workspace' and metadata.product.name='admin' and event.type = 'SAML2_SERVICE_PROVIDER_CONFIG_SETTINGS'

description: Detects the assignment of the 'Super Administrator' role in Google Workspace. This role grants full access to all features and settings in the Admin console, enabling the management of users, devices, and configurations across the organization. Adversaries may attempt to assign this role to themselves or others to gain unrestricted control over the Google Workspace environment. Monitoring for unauthorized or unexpected assignments of the Super Administrator role is crucial to detect potential security threats and prevent unauthorized access to sensitive data and resources.
name: Google Workspace Super Administrator Role Assigned
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Google Workspace' and metadata.product.name ='admin' and event.type = 'ASSIGN_ROLE' and metadata.log_name='DELEGATED_ADMIN_SETTINGS' and enrichments contains '_SEED_ADMIN_ROLE'

description: Detects the addition of third-party applications within the Google Workspace environment. While such applications can enhance productivity, unauthorized installations may pose security risks, including potential data exfiltration or unauthorized access. Monitoring for unauthorized or unexpected additions of applications is essential to detect potential security threats and prevent unauthorized access to sensitive data and resources.
name: Google Workspace Third-Party Application Addition
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Google Workspace' and metadata.product.name = 'admin' and event.type = 'ADD_APPLICATION'

description: Detects OAuth consent events in Google Workspace where a user authorizes the ToogleBox application. This application has been observed enabling modification or removal of mailbox content, including deletion of security notification emails. Once granted, the application can maintain delegated API access to the user’s mailbox, creating an opportunity to suppress alerts, remove evidence of suspicious activity, or otherwise interfere with security visibility. As this detection targets a specific tool linked to mailbox tampering behavior, it provides high-confidence insight into potential email manipulation.
name: Google Workspace Token Authorization for ToogleBox
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Google Workspace' metadata.product.name = 'token' and metadata.log_name = 'authorize' and unmapped.events matches 'app_name","value":"([^"]+)?(tooglebox|recall)'

description: Detects unauthorized additions of trusted domains within Google Workspace. Trusted domains establish trust relationships between different domains, enabling secure communication and collaboration. Unauthorized additions can lead to security risks, including unauthorized access, data exfiltration, and potential data breaches. Monitoring for such unauthorized or unexpected additions is essential to detect potential security threats and prevent unauthorized access to sensitive information.
name: Google Workspace Trusted Domain Addition
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Google Workspace' and metadata.product.name = 'admin' and event.type = 'ADD_TRUSTED_DOMAINS'

description: Detects instances where Two-Step Verification (2SV) is disabled for a user account within Google Workspace. 2SV adds an additional layer of security by requiring users to provide a second form of authentication in addition to their password. Disabling this feature can expose accounts to security risks, including unauthorized access and potential data breaches. Monitoring for such changes is essential to detect potential security threats and prevent unauthorized access to sensitive information.
name: Google Workspace Two-Step Verification Disabled
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Google Workspace' and metadata.product.name = 'login' and metadata.log_name = '2sv_change' and event.type = '2sv_disable'

description: Detects instances where the Google Workspace Marketplace app access setting is modified to permit all applications. This setting governs whether users can install and utilize applications from the Google Workspace Marketplace. Allowing unrestricted access to all applications can introduce security risks, including unauthorized access to sensitive information and potential data breaches. Monitoring for such changes is essential to detect potential security threats and prevent unauthorized access to sensitive information.
name: Google Workspace Unrestricted Marketplace App Access Enabled
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Google Workspace' and metadata.product.name = 'admin' and metadata.log_name = 'APPLICATION_SETTINGS' and event.type = 'CHANGE_APPLICATION_SETTING' and enrichments contains 'Google Workspace Marketplace' and enrichments contains 'Apps Access Setting Allowlist access' and enrichments contains '"NEW_VALUE","value":"ALLOW_ALL"'

description: Detects the use of Windows short names for JavaScript files as seen with Gootloader malware, which can be abused by attackers to obfuscate malicious scripts and evade detection. By leveraging short names, attackers can evade traditional signature-based detection, as the shortened or alternate names appear legitimate and can bypass security filters that might flag suspicious file names. This technique is often used in phishing campaigns or malware distribution, where malicious payloads are hidden in seemingly benign files to exploit vulnerable systems or users.
name: Gootloader via Suspicious JavaScript File

description: Detects the use of the findstr command to search for stored passwords in Group Policy files located on the SYSVOL share. Adversaries may exploit this technique to extract plaintext or weakly encrypted credentials from Group Policy Preferences (GPP), potentially enabling privilege escalation or lateral movement within the network.
name: Group Policy Password Discovery via Findstr
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and src.process.cmdline contains 'findstr' and src.process.cmdline contains '/s' and src.process.cmdline contains 'sysvol' and src.process.cmdline contains 'cpassword'

description: Detects modifications to the system bootloader that may indicate an attempt to establish persistence or evade defenses during the early stages of system startup. Adversaries may leverage this technique to execute unauthorized code before the operating system fully loads, potentially bypassing security controls and gaining privileged access. Monitoring for changes in the bootloader configuration can help identify suspicious activity that operates outside the visibility of traditional endpoint protections.
name: Grub Files Manipulation
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type in ('File Creation', 'File Modification') and (tgt.file.path contains ('/boot/grub/grub.cfg', '/etc/default/grub.d/') or tgt.file.path matches '/boot/efi/EFI/*/grub.cfg' or tgt.file.path matches '/etc/default/grub$')

description: Detects the creation of a guest account on a system. Adversaries may create a guest account to gain unauthorized access while maintaining a low profile. Guest accounts typically have limited privileges, but they can be leveraged for persistence, lateral movement, or privilege escalation depending on system misconfigurations. Monitoring for unexpected guest account creation can help identify potential unauthorized access attempts.
name: Guest Account Creation via Sysadminctl Utility
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'osx' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.image.path matches 'bin/sysadminctl$' and tgt.process.cmdline contains '-guestAccount' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'on'

description: Detects the execution of the osascript utility being used to create a hidden login item via AppleScript. Adversaries often leverage AppleScript to persist on macOS systems by silently adding applications or scripts to launch at startup without user awareness. Monitoring for osascript usage with login item modifications can help detect stealthy persistence attempts.
name: Hidden Login Item via Apple Script
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'osx' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.image.path matches 'bin/osascript$' and (tgt.process.cmdline contains 'login item' and tgt.process.cmdline matches 'hidden(:\\s|:)true')

subQueries.subQuery: event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.cmdline contains 'findstr.exe' or tgt.process.cmdline contains 'findstr.exe') and (src.process.cmdline contains ('OS Name', 'Host Name', 'System Manufacturer', 'System Model', 'Time Zone', 'Domain', 'Nombre del sistema operativo', 'Nombre de host', 'Fabricante del sistema', 'Modelo el sistema', 'Zona horaria', 'Dominio') or tgt.process.cmdline contains ('OS Name', 'Host Name', 'System Manufacturer', 'System Model', 'Time Zone', 'Domain', 'Nombre del sistema operativo', 'Nombre de host', 'Fabricante del sistema', 'Modelo el sistema', 'Zona horaria', 'Dominio'))

event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.cmdline contains 'net.exe' and src.process.cmdline contains 'group' and src.process.cmdline contains ('domain admins', 'enterprise admins', 'Domain computers')) or (tgt.process.cmdline contains 'net.exe' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'group' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('domain admins', 'enterprise admins', 'Domain computers'))

event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.cmdline contains 'nltest.exe' and src.process.cmdline contains ('dclist', 'domain_trusts')) or (tgt.process.cmdline contains 'nltest.exe' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('dclist', 'domain_trusts'))

description: Detects a rapid sequence of enumeration activity occurring within a short time window, indicative of automated reconnaissance. This pattern often precedes lateral movement as threat actors inventory hosts, services, and accounts to map the environment. The rule correlates high-volume discovery events temporally to surface anomalous reconnaissance behavior, with potential false positives from aggressive vulnerability scans or authorized inventory jobs.
name: High-Frequency System and Account Enumeration Burst

description: Detects the creation of a scheduled task configured to run with SYSTEM privileges from a temporary directory. Adversaries can abuse scheduled tasks for privilege escalation, persistence, or execution of malicious payloads. Using the temp directory as the source path is uncommon for legitimate activity and may indicate attempts to run arbitrary code with the highest level of system privileges, enabling further compromise of the host.
name: High-Privilege Task from Temp Directory
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (tgt.process.name = 'schtasks.exe' or tgt.process.displayName = 'Task Scheduler Configuration Tool') and tgt.process.cmdline contains '/Create' and tgt.process.cmdline contains '/ru SYSTEM' and tgt.process.cmdline contains '\\temp\\' and not (tgt.process.user matches '\\\\(SYSTEM|SISTEMA|Système|NETWORK SERVICE)$') and not (src.process.parent.displayName contains ('TeamViewer Remote Control Application Installer', 'snaptool.exe', 'VMware Tools Core Service', 'DATEV-Software', 'CleanWipe')) and not (src.process.displayName contains ('TeamViewer Remote Control Application Installer', 'snaptool.exe', 'VMware Tools Core Service', 'DATEV-Software', 'CleanWipe'))

subQueries.subQuery: event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('net.exe', 'net1.exe') or src.process.displayName = 'Net Command') and src.process.cmdline contains 'user' and src.process.cmdline contains (' administrator', 'domain admins', 'enterprise admins', 'remote desktop users')

description: Detects multiple account reconnaissance commands being executed in a short time span using net.exe. Adversaries can abuse this tool to enumerate privileged accounts or groups within a domain. This information can be leveraged to identify potential targets for further attacks, such as privilege escalation or lateral movement
name: High Volume Net User and Group Enumeration

description: Detects the use of Hping, a network tool commonly utilized for diagnostics, testing, and firewall rule auditing on Linux systems. While Hping is a legitimate tool, adversaries can abuse it for various malicious activities, including conducting Denial of Service (DoS) attacks, port scanning, and network reconnaissance. By sending crafted packets, attackers can gather information about a target system's open ports, firewall configuration, and network vulnerabilities. Furthermore, Hping can be used to spoof packets, evade detection, or launch smokescreen attacks to divert attention while conducting other malicious activities.
name: Hping Network Utility Activity
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.name in ('hping', 'hping2', 'hping3') and not (src.process.parent.name in:anycase ('nbagent'))

description: Detects the enabling of Hyper-V virtualization features on Windows systems. While Hyper-V is a legitimate virtualization technology, unauthorized or unexpected activation could indicate attempts to establish persistence, evade detection, or create isolated environments for malicious activities. Adversaries may leverage virtualization to run malware, test exploits, or hide malicious processes from security tools. Monitoring these configuration changes helps identify potential security risks and ensure virtualization features align with organizational policies.
name: Hyper-V Role Enabled
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type in ('Process Creation', 'Command Script') and ((src.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe', 'pwsh.exe', 'powershell_ise.exe') or src.process.displayName in:anycase ('Windows PowerShell', 'pwsh', 'PowerShell ISE', 'Registry Console Tool')) and (src.process.cmdline contains ('Enable-WindowsOptionalFeature', 'DISM') or cmdScript.content contains ('Enable-WindowsOptionalFeature', 'DISM')) and ((src.process.cmdline contains ('online', 'FeatureName') or cmdScript.content contains ('online', 'FeatureName')) and (src.process.cmdline contains:matchcase 'Microsoft-Hyper-V' or cmdScript.content contains:matchcase 'Microsoft-Hyper-V')) and (src.process.cmdline matches '\\s(\\-|\\/)All' or cmdScript.content matches '\\s(\\-|\\/)All')) or (tgt.process.name in ('dism.exe', 'cmd.exe') and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'dism.exe' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ' /enable-feature ' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ' /all' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('VirtualMachinePlatform', 'Microsoft-Hyper-V-All'))

description: Detects instances of ie4uinit.exe being executed from paths outside standard system directories. This behavior can be exploited by adversaries to bypass security controls or evade detection by running a legitimate binary from an unexpected location. By executing ie4uinit.exe from a non-standard path, attackers may achieve unauthorized execution of malicious payloads while appearing to utilize a legitimate system binary, making it crucial to monitor this activity.
name: Ie4uinit Execution from Non-System Path
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('ie4uinit.exe') or src.process.displayName = 'IE Per-User Initialization Utility') and not (src.process.image.path matches ('\\\\windows\\\\system32', '\\\\windows\\\\syswow64'))

description: Detects instances where HTTP logging in Internet Information Services (IIS) has been disabled. This action may signify an adversary employing anti-forensics tactics to evade detection after gaining unauthorized access to the server, possibly via a web shell or other exploit. Disabling HTTP logging obscures malicious activities, complicating efforts to trace attack vectors, monitor unauthorized access, or assess the scope of a compromise.
name: IIS HTTP Logging Disabled
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('appcmd.exe') or src.process.displayName in:anycase ('appcmd.exe')) and (src.process.cmdline contains '/dontLog:' and src.process.cmdline contains 'true') and not (src.process.parent.name in:anycase ('iissetup.exe') or src.process.displayName in:anycase ('iissetup'))

description: Detects PHP execution within IIS environments indicative of potential web shell file creation or malicious file writing operations. The command line activity is commonly used by attackers to write web shell code directly to the file system through web application vulnerabilities. This technique allows adversaries to bypass traditional file upload restrictions and establish persistent backdoor access to compromised web servers.
name: IIS PHP Execution Indicative of Web Shell File Writing
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type in ('Process Creation', 'Command Script') and (src.process.parent.name in:anycase ('w3wp.exe') or src.process.parent.displayName = 'IIS Worker Process') and (tgt.process.cmdline contains 'php' or cmdScript.content contains 'php') and (tgt.process.cmdline matches '\\s*-r\\s*' or cmdScript.content matches '\\s*-r\\s*') and (tgt.process.cmdline contains 'file_put_contents' or cmdScript.content contains 'file_put_contents')

subQueries.subQuery: ((src.process.parent.name = 'w3wp.exe' or src.process.parent.displayName = 'IIS Worker Process') or (src.process.name = 'w3wp.exe' or src.process.displayName = 'IIS Worker Process')) and (tgt.process.cmdline matches 'cmd.exe (\\/.*?)? \\/c' and tgt.process.cmdline matches 'net user (\\w+) (\\w+) \\/add')
tgt.process.cmdline matches 'net localgroup administrators (\\w+) \\/add'

description: Detects an IIS worker process, which may be associated with a webshell or other malicious activity, attempting to create a new local user account and elevate that account to administrative privileges. Adversaries can abuse this technique to establish persistence, gain privileged access, and maintain long-term control of a compromised system. By leveraging the IIS worker process, attackers may blend malicious activity with legitimate web server operations, making the abuse more difficult to detect. This method of privilege escalation allows adversaries to perform further system compromise, execute commands with elevated rights, and expand their control across the environment.
name: IIS Worker Process Adding User and Granting Admin Privileges

description: Detects instances where an IIS worker process initiates a file download via command-line execution. This behavior is atypical for IIS processes and may indicate exploitation or post-exploitation activity, such as an attacker attempting to retrieve malicious payloads or tools. Adversaries may leverage IIS processes to blend malicious actions with legitimate web server operations, increasing the likelihood of evasion. Monitoring for command-line downloads by IIS processes helps identify potential web server compromise and lateral movement attempts.
name: IIS Worker Process Initiating Command-Line File Download
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.parent.name in:anycase ('w3wp.exe') or src.process.parent.displayName = 'IIS Worker Process') and src.process.cmdline matches '\\b(Net\\.WebClient|curl|invoke-webrequest|iwr|DownloadString|DownloadFile|wget)\\b' and not (src.process.cmdline matches:matchcase '(-X\\s+POST|--request\\s+POST|Method\\s*=\\s*.?POST)')

description: Detects when the Program Compatibility Assistant (pcalua.exe) runs programs and commands. Adversaries can abuse pcalua.exe to bypass User Account Control (UAC) and execute commands with elevated privileges. This is possible because pcalua.exe is a signed Microsoft binary that is often allowed to run even when other command execution methods are restricted. By leveraging pcalua.exe, an adversary can launch malicious payloads, execute scripts, or perform other actions that would normally require administrative rights, effectively bypassing security restrictions designed to prevent command-line interpreter use. This can lead to system compromise, data exfiltration, or other malicious activities.
name: Indirect Command Execution via Pcalua
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and ((src.process.name in:anycase ('cmd.exe') or src.process.displayName = 'Windows Command Processor') or (src.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe') or src.process.displayName = 'Windows PowerShell') or (src.process.name in:anycase ('Picus.Simulator.exe') or src.process.displayName = 'Picus Simulator')) and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('pcalua.exe') or tgt.process.displayName = 'Program Compatibility Assistant') and tgt.process.cmdline matches:anycase '(/|-)a'

description: Detects PowerShell commands that perform process creation through indirect WMI object construction. This technique can be used to evade traditional detection mechanisms by avoiding direct invocation of process creation methods. Instead, attackers use generic object instantiation to trigger process execution through WMI in a stealthier manner. This behavior is commonly observed during execution, lateral movement, or defense evasion, where adversaries aim to mask their activity within legitimate system management operations.
name: Indirect Process Creation via PowerShell and WMI
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type in ('Process Creation', 'Command Script') and (cmdScript.content matches 'New-Object Management\\.ManagementPath\\(\\"Win32_Process' or tgt.process.cmdline contains 'New-Object Management\\.ManagementPath\\(\\"Win32_Process')

description: Detects the execution of python through the Program Compatibility Assistant (pcalua.exe). Adversaries can abuse pcalua.exe, a signed Microsoft binary, to bypass User Account Control (UAC) and run commands with elevated privileges. This technique may be used in Living off the Land (LOTL) scenarios, such as Redcurl's tactic of setting up a reverse proxy with Python and RPivot. Monitoring this behavior helps identify unauthorized privilege escalation and potential post-exploitation activities.
name: Indirect Python Execution via Pcalua

description: Detects user-driven attempts to harvest Active Directory credentials by abusing Volume Shadow Copy Service (VSS). This activity typically involves creating or accessing shadow copies to extract the NTDS.dit database, enabling offline credential dumping. The presence of user interaction indicates hands-on activity rather than automated system operations, increasing the likelihood of malicious intent such as privilege escalation, lateral movement, or domain compromise.
name: Interactive NTDS Harvesting via VSS
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Behavioral Indicators' and indicator.name contains:anycase 'VssNtdsHarvesting' and indicator.metadata contains 'Had User Interaction: True'

description: Detects the use of the Perl utility to spawn an interactive terminal session, which may indicate the initiation of a reverse shell or unauthorized remote access. Adversaries often leverage Perl’s built-in networking and system capabilities to craft lightweight but effective reverse shells or command and control (C2) channels. This behavior is highly suspicious on Linux systems, particularly if it originates from unexpected users, cron jobs, or web-facing services. Monitoring for such activity helps uncover stealthy persistence mechanisms, privilege escalation, or lateral movement attempts.
name: Interactive Terminal Launched via Perl Utility
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os in ('linux', 'osx') and tgt.process.displayName in:anycase ('perl') and tgt.process.cmdline contains ' exec ' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ("bin/sh", "bin/bash", "bin/zsh", "bin/dash", "bin/ksh", "bash", "ksh", "zsh", "dash")

description: Detects the execution of the Invoke-WMIExec utility. An adversary can abuse this tool to achieve lateral movement by using WMI commands with NTLMv2 pass-the-hash authentication, enabling them to execute commands remotely on target systems and spread throughout the network.
name: Invoke-WMIExec Utility Executed
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Command Script' and (cmdScript.content contains 'Invoke-WMIExec' or (cmdScript.content contains '4D454F570100000018AD09F36AD8D011A07500C04FB68820' and cmdScript.content contains  '4E544C4D535350'))

description: Detects suspicious file creation activity associated with Ivanti vulnerability CVE-2024-29847, where the AgentPortal.exe process creates files within the Management Suite Core Webservices directory on Windows endpoints. This behavior may indicate an exploitation attempt targeting the Ivanti vulnerability, potentially allowing attackers to gain unauthorized access, execute arbitrary code, or deploy malicious web shells.
name: Ivanti Exploitation Attempt (CVE-2024-29847)

description: Detects Java applications initiating DNS queries to domains associated with the Tor network. Such activity may indicate attempts to anonymize communications or bypass network security controls using Tor infrastructure. Adversaries often leverage Tor-related domains to facilitate covert command and control, data exfiltration, or evade monitoring. This behavior is atypical for legitimate Java applications and may signal malicious intent or unauthorized network usage.
name: Java-Based DNS Requests to Tor-Associated Domains
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os in ('windows') and event.type = 'DNS Resolved' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('java.exe', 'javaw.exe') OR src.process.displayName in ('OpenJDK Platform binary', 'Java(TM) Platform SE binary')) and event.dns.request matches ('\\.tor$', 'check\\.torproject\\.org')

description: Detects modification of Windows Registry Run keys associated with Java applications to establish persistence. Adversaries may alter these registry keys to ensure malicious Java programs execute automatically upon system startup. This technique enables threat actors to maintain long-term access and control over compromised hosts. Monitoring such changes is critical, as unauthorized modifications often indicate post-compromise persistence activities aligned with common adversary behavior patterns.
name: Java-Based Persistence via Windows Registry Run Key Modification
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type in ('Process Creation') and (src.process.parent.name in:anycase ('java.exe', 'javaw.exe') OR src.process.parent.displayName in ('OpenJDK Platform binary', 'Java(TM) Platform SE binary')) and (src.process.name in:anycase ('reg.exe') or src.process.displayName = 'Registry Console Tool') and src.process.cmdline contains 'Software\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\Run' and src.process.cmdline contains 'reg' and src.process.cmdline contains 'add'

description: Detects instances where a Java process spawns PowerShell to invoke scheduled task commands. This behavior is indicative of potential abuse of Java applications to execute system-level tasks, which may be leveraged by adversaries for persistence or lateral movement. Such activity aligns with common attacker techniques seeking to blend malicious actions within legitimate processes. Monitoring for this pattern helps identify suspicious process chains that could signal the presence of malware or unauthorized automation.
name: Java-Initiated PowerShell Scheduled Task Invocation
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type in ('Process Creation', 'Command Script') and (src.process.parent.name in:anycase ('java.exe', 'javaw.exe') OR src.process.parent.displayName in ('OpenJDK Platform binary', 'Java(TM) Platform SE binary')) and (src.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe') or src.process.displayName = 'Windows PowerShell') and ((src.process.cmdline contains 'schtasks' and src.process.cmdline contains 'create') or (cmdScript.content contains 'schtasks' and cmdScript.content contains 'create'))

description: Detects instances where wscript.exe is used to execute JavaScript contents extracted from a ZIP file. By embedding malicious JavaScript in a ZIP archive, attackers can evade detection by traditional file scanning methods, relying on the user to extract and execute the payload. This tactic is commonly used in phishing campaigns or lateral movement attacks, where the JavaScript file contains instructions to download additional payloads, establish persistence, or communicate with command-and-control servers. The presence of network connections following the execution of such scripts may further indicate data exfiltration or the download of additional malicious payloads.
name: JavaScript Executing from ZIP File
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.category in ('dns','url','ip') and (src.process.name in:anycase ('wscript.exe') OR src.process.displayName contains 'Windows Based Script Host') and src.process.cmdline contains 'users' and src.process.cmdline contains 'temp' and src.process.cmdline contains '.zip' and src.process.cmdline contains '.js'

description: Detects the creation of scheduled jobs using the at command on Linux endpoints. The at utility allows users to schedule commands for one-time execution at a specific time in the future. Adversaries may abuse this utility to achieve persistence, execute payloads during periods of low activity, or evade real-time detection. Monitoring the use of the at command can help identify suspicious or unauthorized task scheduling behavior indicative of post-exploitation activity.
name: Job Scheduled via At Utility
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.name = 'at' and not (tgt.process.cmdline matches "^at\\s+-\\w") and not (tgt.process.cmdline = 'atq')

description: Detects the execution of JScript engine via WScript or CScript, a technique often exploited by attackers to execute malicious scripts stealthily. By leveraging legitimate Windows script hosts, adversaries can bypass security controls and execute payloads with minimal detection. This behavior is typically associated with initial access or lateral movement phases of an attack, where scripts are used to download additional malware or gather sensitive information. The rule is crucial for identifying potential script-based attacks that might otherwise blend in with normal administrative tasks, highlighting the importance of monitoring script execution in Windows environments. The technique has been associated with the Latrodectus downloader.
name: JScript Payload Execution via WScript or CScript
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.parent.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe', 'cmd.exe') or src.process.parent.displayName in:anycase ('Windows PowerShell', 'Windows Command Processor')) and (src.process.name in:anycase ('cscript.exe', 'wscript.exe') or src.process.displayName in:anycase ('Microsoft ® Console Based Script Host', 'Windows Based Script Host')) and src.process.cmdline contains '/e:jscript' and not (src.process.cmdline matches '\\.js|\\.bat|\\.cmd|\\.vbs')

description: Detects exploitation of the Kubernetes nodes/proxy GET permission, which can enable remote command execution on cluster nodes via direct connections to the Kubelet API. Service accounts with nodes/proxy GET access—often required by monitoring tools for reading Pod metrics and logs—may inadvertently gain the ability to execute arbitrary commands in any Pod, including privileged system Pods, if they reach the Kubelet on port 10250 using connection protocols like WebSockets. This stems from the Kubelet authorizing WebSocket connections based solely on the initial handshake, without enforcing additional CREATE permissions required by the /exec endpoint. Attackers leveraging this misconfiguration can achieve full cluster compromise while evading detection, as Kubernetes AuditPolicy does not log such command executions. The risk is not vendor-specific and is prevalent due to the widespread use of nodes/proxy GET permissions in public Helm charts and vendor solutions. Organizations are advised to review service account permissions, consult vendors, and monitor for suspicious direct Kubelet API interactions.
name: K8s RCE via Nodes/Proxy GET Request
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.cmdline contains '10250/exec/' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'error=1' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'output=1' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'command=' and k8sCluster.containerName = *

description: Detects instances where the KACE Konea process acts as a parent to PowerShell executions utilizing encoded command parameters. This behavior is indicative of potential adversary use of KACE Konea as a launchpad for obfuscated PowerShell activity, which may be employed to bypass security controls and evade detection. Such activity aligns with common attacker tradecraft for executing malicious payloads or performing post-exploitation tasks. Monitoring for this pattern helps identify suspicious lateral movement or privilege escalation attempts within the environment.
name: KACE Konea Initiated PowerShell Encoded Command Execution
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and (src.process.parent.name in:anycase ('konea.exe') or src.process.parent.displayName in ('konea')) and (src.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe') or src.process.displayName = 'Windows PowerShell') and src.process.cmdline contains  ('-e ', '-ec ', '-enc')

description: Detects the execution of Kali Linux through Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL) on Windows endpoints. Kali Linux is a security-focused Linux distribution that contains numerous penetration testing and security assessment tools. While it has legitimate uses for security professionals, adversaries may leverage Kali Linux via WSL to access powerful security tools and utilities while evading traditional Windows-based detections. This technique can be used for defense evasion, as it allows attackers to run Linux-based attack tools directly from a Windows environment without requiring a separate machine or virtual machine. Monitoring for Kali Linux execution through WSL helps identify potential security testing activities or malicious exploitation attempts that utilize Linux-based attack vectors from Windows hosts.
name: Kali Execution via WSL
s1ql:
dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and ((tgt.process.image.path contains ':\\Users\\' and tgt.process.image.path contains ('\\AppData\\Local\\packages\\KaliLinux', '\\AppData\\Local\\Microsoft\\WindowsApps\\kali.exe')) or (tgt.process.image.path contains (':\\Program Files\\WindowsApps\\KaliLinux.', '\\kali.exe')) or (src.process.image.path matches '\\\\wsl(host)*\\.exe$' and (tgt.process.image.path contains ('\\kali.exe', '\\KaliLinux') or tgt.process.cmdline contains ('Kali.exe', 'Kali-linux', 'kalilinux')) and not (tgt.process.cmdline contains (' -i ', ' --install ', ' --unregister ')))
)

description: Detects the execution of the Bifrost tool on macOS to perform Kerberos attacks. Bifrost is often used for ticket manipulation, credential extraction, or forging Kerberos tickets to gain unauthorized access to resources. Monitoring for the use of Bifrost can help identify potential credential abuse or lateral movement attempts in Active Directory environments.
name: Kerberos Attack via Bifrost

description: Detects the execution of the KCC (Kerberos Credentials Cache) utility on macOS to access Kerberos tickets. The KCC utility is used to view or manipulate Kerberos tickets stored on the system. Attackers may use this utility to enumerate, extract, or manipulate Kerberos tickets for lateral movement or privilege escalation. Monitoring for kcc usage can help identify potential credential abuse or unauthorized access attempts targeting Kerberos authentication.
name: Kerberos Tickets Accessed via KCC Utility
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'osx' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.image.path matches 'bin/kcc$' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'copy_cred_cache'

description: Detects attempts to create kernel driver services with binPath pointing to Alternate Data Streams (ADS). Adversaries may abuse NTFS ADS to hide malicious driver files and register them as kernel services to gain kernel-level privileges.
name: Kernel Driver Creation via Alternate Data Stream
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('sc.exe') or tgt.process.displayName contains 'Service Control Manager Configuration Tool') and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'create' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'kernel' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ':' and tgt.process.cmdline matches 'binPath\\=\\s+.{0,64}\\.[A-Za-z1]{2,3}:[A-Za-z0-9_\\-]{1,64}'

description: Detects the creation of a kernel-mode driver service pointing to a suspicious file location. Threat actors may create kernel driver services referencing binaries in non-standard directories to load malicious drivers for privilege escalation, security tool evasion (BYOVD), or rootkit installation. The creation of kernel-type services from these locations is highly unusual in legitimate operations.
name: Kernel Driver Service Creation From Suspicious Directory

description: Detects execution of Kernel Driver Utility, KDU, a tool designed to bypass Windows Driver Signature Enforcement and load unsigned kernel drivers. KDU enables attackers to load malicious drivers with kernel-level privileges, effectively bypassing security controls.
name: Kernel Driver Utility Execution
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.image.path matches ('\\\\kdu\\.exe$') or src.process.image.originalFileName in:anycase ('hamakaze.exe')) and src.process.cmdline contains:anycase ('-map ', '-prv ', '-dse ', '-ps ')

description: Detects the loading of kernel-mode drivers using names associated with known malicious or unauthorized drivers. This activity may indicate an attempt by adversaries to gain privileged access, execute code at the kernel level, or bypass security controls. Threat actors often exploit driver loading mechanisms to establish persistence, conceal malicious operations, or disable endpoint protections. Monitoring for such driver loads is critical for identifying early stages of kernel-level compromise.
name: Kernel-Mode Driver Load via Known Malicious Driver Name
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Driver Load' and event.type = 'Module Load' and module.path matches ('\\\\2\\.sys$', '\\\\daxin_blank2\\.sys$', '\\\\gftkyj64\\.sys$', '\\\\driver_146b8f4f\\.sys$', '\\\\driver_5d61e4ea\\.sys$', '\\\\driver_a6deeea6\\.sys$', '\\\\6771b13a53b9c7449d4891e427735ea2\\.sys$', '\\\\driver_4fc254af\\.sys$', '\\\\4748696211bd56c2d93c21cab91e82a5\\.sys$', '\\\\wantd_6\\.sys$', '\\\\7\\.sys$', '\\\\f\\.sys$', '\\\\be6318413160e589080df02bb3ca6e6a\\.sys$', '\\\\gmer64\\.sys$', '\\\\daxin_blank4\\.sys$', '\\\\driver_5c308aed\\.sys$', '\\\\driver_668c5bea\\.sys$', '\\\\ktes\\.sys$', '\\\\sense5ext\\.sys$', '\\\\driver_85ca0dcd\\.sys$', '\\\\driver_d9f15d91\\.sys$', '\\\\driver_1afc1d06\\.sys$', '\\\\mimikatz\\.sys$', '\\\\driver_89036534\\.sys$', '\\\\driver_4d8bc539\\.sys$', '\\\\daxin_blank5\\.sys$', '\\\\driver_930da474\\.sys$', '\\\\driver_312c83a9\\.sys$', '\\\\poortry1\\.sys$')

description: Detects execution of processes accessing macOS Keychain files, such as 'login.keychain' or 'login.keychain-db', by unsigned or untrusted processes. This rule identifies attempts to interact with sensitive Keychain data via command lines referencing user or system Keychain paths, where the initiating process lacks a valid or trusted code signature. It also covers cases where common scripting or utility binaries like 'bash', 'sh', 'zsh', 'osascript', 'cat', 'echo', or 'cp' are used and their parent processes are unsigned or untrusted. Such activity may indicate credential theft, unauthorized access, or post-exploitation techniques. Analysts should investigate the source process, its parent, and the context of Keychain file access for potential malicious behavior.
name: Keychain Access via Unsigned or Untrusted Process
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' AND endpoint.os = 'osx' AND event.type = 'Process Creation' AND ((src.process.parent.signedStatus = 'unsigned' and src.process.name in ('bash', 'dash', 'ash', 'zsh', 'csh', 'ksh', 'sh', 'tcsh', 'osascript', 'cat', 'cp')) or src.process.signedStatus = 'unsigned') AND tgt.process.cmdline contains '/Users/' AND tgt.process.cmdline contains ('login.keychain', 'System.keychain', '/library/keychains/')

description: Detects execution of the macOS security utility with the dump-keychain and -d argument, which are used to extract sensitive contents from the system keychain. This activity often indicates attempts to access stored credentials or secrets, a common tactic in credential dumping attacks. The rule triggers when SentinelOne reports a process start event on macOS endpoints where the command line includes dump-keychain or -d. Monitoring for this behavior helps identify potential unauthorized access to keychain data. Analysts should investigate the process context and user to determine if the activity is legitimate or malicious.
name: Keychain Dump via Security Utility
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'osx' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'dump-keychain' and tgt.process.cmdline matches '.*\\s+-d\\b.*' and not (src.process.parent.name in:anycase ('claude', 'codex', 'Cursor Helper (Plugin)', 'Windsurf Helper', 'Antigravity Helper', 'opencode', 'copilot') or src.process.parent.displayName in:anycase ('claude', 'codex', 'Cursor Helper (Plugin)', 'Windsurf Helper', 'Antigravity Helper', 'opencode', 'copilot')) and not (src.process.cmdline contains ('claude', 'cursor', 'anthropic', 'openai', 'windsurf'))

description: Detects the use of the macOS security utility to retrieve browser-related passwords from the Keychain via process creation events. This rule triggers when the security binary is executed with command-line arguments such as -wa or -ga, in combination with find-generic-password or find-internet-password, and targets browser application credentials including Chrome, Chromium, Opera, Safari, Brave, Microsoft Edge, or Firefox. The detection excludes legitimate activity from the Keeper Password Manager Helper. Adversaries may abuse the security utility to extract stored browser passwords for credential access or lateral movement. Monitoring this activity helps identify potential unauthorized access to sensitive credentials on macOS endpoints.
name: Keychain Password Retrieval via Security Utility
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' AND endpoint.os = 'osx' AND event.type = 'Process Creation' AND tgt.process.name = 'security' AND tgt.process.cmdline matches:matchcase '\\-wa\\s|\\-ga\\s' AND tgt.process.cmdline contains ('find-generic-password', 'find-internet-password') AND tgt.process.cmdline contains ('Chrome', 'Chromium', 'Opera', 'Safari', 'Brave', 'Microsoft Edge', 'Firefox') AND NOT (src.process.parent.image.path contains ('/Contents/Frameworks/Keeper Password Manager Helper', '/Contents/MacOS/Keeper Password Manager Helper'))

description: Detects the execution of the curl or wget command on macOS endpoints used to upload data containing the term keychain to a remote HTTP URL. Attackers may use this technique to exfiltrate sensitive keychain data from compromised systems. Monitoring for such behavior helps identify potential credential theft or unauthorized data exfiltration attempts.
name: Keychain Upload to Remote Host Command Line Execution
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'osx' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.cmdline matches:matchcase '(curl\\s.+\\-F\\s+[^ ]*=@|curl\\s.+\\-T\\s+[^ ]+|wget\\s.+\\--(post-file|body-file)\\s+[^ ]*=|scp\\s+[^ ]+\\s+[\\w.-]+@[^ :]+:[^ ]+|sftp\\s+[\\w.-]+@[^ :]+:[^ ]+|rsync\\s+[^ ]+\\s+[\\w.-]+@[^ :]+:[^ ]+)' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('keychain-', '.keychain', '/library/keychains/')

description: Detects the use of various Win32 API functions within Windows PowerShell that can be leveraged to capture keystrokes. Adversaries can exploit this capability to monitor user input, including sensitive information such as passwords, authentication tokens, or confidential communications. By abusing these API functions, attackers may execute stealthy keylogging activities to gather credentials or other valuable data without triggering traditional antivirus or endpoint security defenses.
name: Keylogging Script via PowerShell
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Command Script' and (cmdScript.content contains ('GetAsyncKeyState', 'NtUserGetAsyncKeyState', 'GetKeyboardState', 'Get-Keystrokes') or cmdScript.content contains ('SetWindowsHookA', 'SetWindowsHookW', 'SetWindowsHookExA', 'NtUserSetWindowsHookEx')) and cmdScript.content contains ('GetForegroundWindow', 'GetWindowTextA', 'GetWindowTextW', 'WM_KEYBOARD_LL') and src.process.name = *

description: Detects the execution of commands that attempt to kill known malicious processes on Linux systems. Adversaries often deploy cryptominers and other malware on compromised systems, but may later need to terminate competing malicious software to maximize resource utilization or establish exclusive control. This detection identifies when kill commands are used to terminate commonly observed malicious binaries including cryptocurrency miners, backdoors, and other known malware processes. Such activity may indicate an ongoing infection where attackers are removing competing malware or attempting to clean up traces of their own previous infections to deploy updated variants.
name: Kill Known Malicious Binary
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.name in:anycase ('kill', 'pkill', 'killall') and tgt.process.cmdline contains (' minerd', ' cpuminer', ' cpuminer-multi', ' xmrig-daemon', ' xmrig-notls', ' xmrigCC', ' cryptonight', ' stratum', ' mrxminer', ' conns', ' mrsho', ' kinsing', ' kdevtmpfsi', ' java-miner', ' sysguard', ' sysupdate', ' libprocesshider', ' proscan', ' watchdogd', ' ksoftirqd', ' kswapd', ' netns', ' ksapd')

description: Detects the execution of the Proof of Concept (PoC) binary 'Krueger,' a .NET post-exploitation tool designed to remotely disable Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) systems by exploiting Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC) policies. Krueger enables adversaries with administrative access to deploy malicious WDAC policies on target systems, effectively preventing EDR services from launching upon reboot and facilitating evasion of security measures. Monitoring for the execution of Krueger is essential to identify and prevent unauthorized attempts to compromise system security controls.
name: Krueger Binary Execution
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and src.process.name in:anycase ('krueger.exe')

description: Detects the execution of kubectl commands used to delete Kubernetes cluster events. Adversaries may attempt to clear event logs to cover their tracks, evade detection, or obscure malicious activity within the cluster. This tactic is often employed to disrupt security monitoring, hinder forensic investigations, and remove evidence of unauthorized actions such as privilege escalation, lateral movement, or resource abuse. Monitoring for unexpected event deletions helps detect potential attacks and unauthorized administrative actions within a Kubernetes environment.
name: Kubectl Execution to Delete Kubernetes Cluster Events
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and k8sCluster.containerId matches '[a-zA-Z0-9]' and tgt.process.name = 'kubectl' and (tgt.process.cmdline contains 'delete events')

description: Detects the use of the kubectl command to enumerate the current user's permissions within a Kubernetes cluster. Adversaries or overly curious users may run commands such as kubectl auth can-i or access reviews to discover what operations they can perform. This behavior could be associated with privilege escalation, lateral movement, or preparation for further malicious activity within the cluster.
name: Kubectl Permissions Reconnaissance Activity
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.name = 'kubectl' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ' can-i ' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ' auth ' and not (tgt.process.cmdline contains ' can-i -h')

description: Detects the execution of commands that include flags commonly used to assume the identity of another user or group within a container orchestration environment. Adversaries may leverage these options to impersonate legitimate accounts, thereby bypassing access controls and gaining unauthorized privileges within the cluster. This technique can be used to access sensitive resources, perform lateral movement, or escalate privileges by operating under the guise of trusted users or groups.
name: Kubectl Potential Impersonation Attempt
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.name in ('kubectl', 'kubectl.exe') and tgt.process.cmdline contains (' --as ', ' --as-group', ' --as-uid', '--impersonate-user', '--impersonate-group')

description: Detects the creation, modification, or deletion of static pod manifest files within a Kubernetes cluster. Static pods are managed directly by the kubelet on a specific node and are stored in the '/etc/kubernetes/manifests/' directory as YAML files. While managing static pods is a legitimate administrative activity, unauthorized changes to these manifests could indicate an attempt to establish persistence, deploy malicious containers, or disrupt critical cluster components. Adversaries may target static pods because they bypass the Kubernetes API server's admission controllers and authorization mechanisms, making them valuable for persistence and privilege escalation. Monitoring changes to these manifest files helps identify potential security threats and ensures the integrity of your Kubernetes environment.
name: Kubernetes Static Pods Created or Updated
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type in ('File Creation', 'File Modification', 'File Deletion') and tgt.file.path contains '/etc/kubernetes/manifests/' and tgt.file.path matches '\\.(yaml|yml)$' and not (src.process.name in ('kubeadm', 'cluster-kube-apiserver-operator', 'cluster-kube-controller-manager-operator', 'tar'))

description: Detects registry-based autorun persistence consistent with Latrodectus masquerading as Intel Graphics Media Accelerator components. This behavior enables durable startup execution while blending with legitimate graphics driver artifacts, aiding defense evasion and facilitating follow-on actions after reboot. Please enable the 'RegistryAutorun' Behavioral indicator to make this rule work.
name: Latrodectus Spoofed Intel Graphics Registry Entry
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Behavioral Indicators' and indicator.name = 'RegistryAutorun' and indicator.metadata matches 'Key: "\\\\registry\\\\machine\\\\Software\\\\Microsoft\\\\Windows\\\\CurrentVersion\\\\(Run|RunOnce)"' and indicator.metadata matches 'ValueData: .*\\\\igfxSDK\\.exe"'

description: Detects potential click-fraud attacks on Windows endpoints that exploit the finger command to establish communication with external domains and execute subsequent commands. The detection focuses on process creation events where cmd.exe or powershell.exe are invoked with command lines indicating a network operation. This behavior is commonly associated with an attempt to misuse the finger protocol for malicious purposes, such as downloading and executing scripts from a controlled external domain. The rule triggers when such activity is initiated by either the Windows Command Processor or Windows PowerShell, capturing attempts to use native system tools for command-and-control (C2) communication or other unauthorized operations.
name: Legacy Finger Client Execution via ClickFix Component
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('cmd.exe', 'powershell.exe') or src.process.displayName in:anycase ('Windows Command Processor', 'Windows PowerShell')) and src.process.cmdline matches '(?:\\s|\\\\|^)[\\\"\\\']?finger(?:\\.exe)?[\\\"\\\']?(?:\\s|$)' and src.process.cmdline contains '@' and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('cmd.exe', 'powershell.exe', 'cscript.exe', 'mshta.exe', 'rundll32.exe', 'wscript.exe', 'java.exe', 'javaw.exe', 'python', 'python3') or tgt.process.displayName in:anycase ('Windows Command Processor', 'Windows PowerShell', 'Microsoft ® Console Based Script Host', 'Microsoft (R) HTML Application host', 'Windows Based Script Host', 'Windows host process (Rundll32)', 'python'))

description: Detects the execution of a process from the /dev/shm directory, a shared memory filesystem often abused by adversaries to evade detection and avoid leaving traces on disk. Attackers commonly use this technique to execute payloads directly from memory, facilitating fileless malware execution, privilege escalation, or persistence. Monitoring for processes launched from /dev/shm can help identify potential exploitation attempts, unauthorized code execution, or stealthy persistence mechanisms on Linux systems.
name: Linux Process Execution via /dev/shm
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.image.path matches '^/dev/shm' and not (tgt.process.image.path matches '^/dev/shm/tmp/overlay')

description: Detects the execution of proxy tools on a Linux system. Adversaries may leverage tools such as proxychains, tor, or socat to anonymize network traffic, bypass security controls, or establish covert communication channels. This behavior can indicate attempts to evade detection, exfiltrate data, or communicate with external command-and-control (C2) servers. Monitoring the execution of these tools helps detect unauthorized network tunneling, persistence mechanisms, or stealthy attacker activity within the environment.
name: Linux Proxy Tool Execution
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type='Process Creation' and ((tgt.process.name in:anycase ('proxychains', 'tsocks', 'proxify', 'socksify')) or (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('systemctl', 'service') and tgt.process.cmdline contains (' start ', ' restart ') and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('redsocks', ' tor', 'privoxy')))

description: Detects malicious Python code execution from the LiteLLM supply chain attack. Identifies Python processes executing base64-decoded inline code containing the malicious payload markers used to exfiltrate credentials, cloud keys, and cryptocurrency wallets.
name: LiteLLM Supply Chain Payload Execution
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (tgt.process.name contains 'python' or src.process.name contains 'python') and tgt.process.cmdline contains '-c' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'import base64' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'exec(base64.b64decode' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'kI2NF9TQ1JJUFQgPSAi' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'bW9kZWxzLmxpdGVsbG0uY2xvdWQ'

description: Detects a pattern of repeated login failures followed by a successful login from the same user. This pattern, once baselined, may indicate a brute force attack.
name: Login Failures Followed by Success

description: Detects the use of the defaults utility to modify login or logout hooks on macOS systems. Attackers may abuse these hooks to execute arbitrary scripts or programs during user login or logout, enabling persistence or execution of malicious payloads. Monitoring for changes to these hooks can help identify attempts to establish persistence or execute unauthorized actions during system logins or logouts.
name: LoginHook/LogoutHook Change via Defaults Utility
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'osx' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.image.path matches 'bin/defaults$' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'write' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('LoginHook', 'LogoutHook') and not (src.process.parent.image.path contains '/jamf/bin/jamf' or src.process.image.path contains '/jamf/bin/jamf')

description: Detects activity associated with the Loki command and control (C2) framework, a stage-1 C2 framework written in Node.js. An adversary can abuse Loki to script-jack vulnerable Electron applications, establishing unauthorized remote control over compromised systems, enabling them to execute commands, steal sensitive data, deploy further malicious payloads, or achieve persistence.
name: Loki C2 Framework Activity Detected

description: Detects Windows LOLBins initiating outbound WebSocket or raw TCP connections to external domains. Such network activity from these signed utilities is a common technique for living-off-the-land command-and-control, payload retrieval, or data exfiltration, and may indicate malware operating under trusted binaries to evade defenses.
name: LOLBin-Initiated WebSocket or Raw TCP Connection
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('dotnet.exe', 'rundll32.exe', 'regsvr32.exe', 'mshta.exe', 'cscript.exe', 'wscript.exe', 'wmic.exe', 'msbuild.exe') or tgt.process.displayName in ('.NET Host',  'Windows host process (Rundll32)', 'Microsoft(C) Register Server', 'Microsoft (R) HTML Application host', 'Microsoft ® Console Based Script Host', 'Microsoft ® Windows Based Script Host', 'WMI Commandline Utility', 'MSBuild.exe')) and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('\\appdata\\', '\\perflogs\\', ':\\Perflogs', ':\\Users\\All Users\\', ':\\Users\\Default\\', ':\\Users\\Public', ':\\Windows\\Temp', '\\AppData\\Local\\', '\\AppData\\Roaming\\', '%AppData%', '%Public%', '\\programdata\\') and tgt.process.cmdline matches '.*(?:ws|wss|tcp):\\/\\/.*' and not (tgt.process.cmdline contains ('localhost', '127.0.0.1'))

description: Detects registry-based autorun entries that launch script content through trusted system binaries from user-writable locations. This pattern is security-relevant because it can provide durable persistence by ensuring attacker-controlled code executes at logon or startup while blending into normal OS activity. Adversaries commonly leverage this approach to maintain access and evade application controls by abusing legitimate binaries and autorun mechanisms.
name: LOLBin Script Persistence via User-Writable Registry Autorun Entries
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Registry Value Create' and registry.keyPath matches 'Software\\\\Microsoft\\\\Windows\\\\CurrentVersion\\\\(?!RunOnce\\b)Run\\b' and (registry.value contains ('wscript', 'cscript', 'mshta') or registry.value matches ('\\.bat$', '\\.ps1$')) and registry.value contains ('\\appdata\\', '\\perflogs\\', ':\\Perflogs', ':\\Users\\All Users\\', ':\\Users\\Default\\', ':\\Users\\Public', ':\\Windows\\Temp', '\\AppData\\Local\\', '\\AppData\\Roaming\\', '%AppData%', '%Public%', '\\programdata\\') and src.process.integrityLevel != 'SYSTEM'

description: Detects modification of registry keys to disable Local Security Authority (LSA) protection. Disabling LSA protection can allow adversaries to extract credentials or escalate privileges by weakening security controls around sensitive authentication processes. This behavior is associated with credential access techniques and may indicate an attempt to facilitate credential theft or persistence. Such modifications are uncommon in standard administrative workflows and warrant further investigation.
name: LSA Protection Disabled via Registry Key Modification
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type in ('Registry Key Create', 'Registry Value Modified') and registry.keyPath matches ('\\\\Control\\\\Lsa\\\\RunAsPPL$') and not (registry.value in ('1', '0x00000001', '2', '0x00000002')) and not (src.process.user matches '\\\\SYSTEM$|\\\\SISTEMA$|\\\\Système$|\\\\NETWORK SERVICE$')

description: Detects the use of Sysinternals Procdump to extract credentials from lsass.exe, indicating potential credential dumping. Adversaries frequently use Procdump to create a memory dump of the lsass.exe process. This dump can then be processed offline using tools like Mimikatz to extract sensitive information such as NTLM hashes, Kerberos tickets, and plaintext passwords. By obtaining these credentials, adversaries can escalate privileges, move laterally within the network, and gain access to sensitive data and systems. This technique is often used as part of a larger attack chain to achieve complete domain compromise.
name: Lsass Dump via ProcDump

description: Detects attempts to dump the LSASS process memory using the Ghost in the PPL attack technique. This method leverages weaknesses in the Protected Process Light (PPL) mechanism to bypass security protections. Adversaries exploit this technique to extract sensitive credential information, including NTLM hashes, Kerberos tickets, and potentially plaintext passwords. This access enables lateral movement, privilege escalation, and unauthorized access to additional systems within the environment.
name: LSASS Memory Dump via Ghost in the PPL Attack

description: Detects modification of the LSASS Silent Process Exit registry key on Windows systems, indicating potential attempts to tamper with system security settings or evade detection. Adversaries can abuse this by configuring the system to automatically create a memory dump of the LSASS process when it exits. This dump file can then be exfiltrated and analyzed offline to extract sensitive information such as credentials, Kerberos tickets, and other secrets. This technique allows attackers to bypass traditional credential access controls and potentially gain elevated privileges or move laterally within the network.
name: LSASS Silent Process Exit Registry Modification
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' AND endpoint.os = 'windows' AND event.category = 'registry' AND event.type = 'Registry Value Modified' AND registry.keyPath contains '\\SilentProcessExit\\lsass'

description: Detects generic PowerShell stager behavior associated with the Lumma Stealer campaign. Adversaries use PowerShell stagers to download and execute payloads, enabling the delivery of Lumma Stealer for credential theft, data exfiltration, and further system compromise. Monitoring this behavior helps identify early-stage malware execution and prevent broader attacks. Please enable the 'PowershellStager' Behavioral Indicator to make this rule work.
name: Lumma Stealer PowerShell Stager

description: Detects the execution of the lwp-download utility, a command-line tool used to download files via HTTP or HTTPS. Adversaries have been leveraging lwp-download to retrieve malicious payloads from remote servers as part of their attack chains. Unexpected use of this utility, especially in automated scripts or from unusual locations, may indicate suspicious activity and should be investigated.
name: Lwp-download Utility Executed
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'osx' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (tgt.process.image.path matches 'bin/perl$' and tgt.process.cmdline contains '/bin/lwp-download') or (tgt.process.image.path matches 'bin/lwp-download$')

description: Detects the execution of payloads initiated through user copy-and-paste behavior from deceptive websites, where curl is used to retrieve remote content. These events often stem from social engineering techniques such as fake CAPTCHA challenges or misleading terminal commands, frequently associated with tools like ClickFix. This method enables adversaries to deliver malicious scripts directly into the terminal, potentially resulting in malware installation, RAT deployment, or unauthorized access to credentials and sensitive data.
name: Malicious Copy and Paste Payload Execution via Curl

description: Detects execution of a widely used SSH/SCP client under a non-standard or renamed filename, indicating potential masquerading to evade controls and baseline detections. This behavior is often associated with lateral movement, credential use, or covert file transfer under the guise of legitimate administration. In some environments, administrators may run portable, renamed copies for troubleshooting, which can produce benign matches.
name: Masqueraded PuTTY SSH Client Execution
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and ((src.process.image.originalFileName contains 'putty' or src.process.displayName in ('PuTTY.exe', 'SSH, Telnet, Rlogin, and SUPDUP client')) and not (src.process.name contains ('putty', 'kitty', 'ssh', '.rbf')) or (tgt.process.displayName in ('PuTTY.exe', 'SSH, Telnet, Rlogin, and SUPDUP client') and not (tgt.process.name contains ('putty', 'kitty', 'ssh', '.rbf'))))

description: Detects masqueraded Rclone execution. Threat actors may rename Rclone to evade detection while exfiltrating data to external cloud storage services. This technique is commonly observed during the late stages of an intrusion to transfer stolen data out of the target environment.
name: Masqueraded Rclone Execution

description: Detects potential bypass of security controls by renaming highly relevant binaries. This technique is often employed to disguise malicious activities under the guise of legitimate processes, making it challenging for traditional security measures to identify threats. By monitoring masqueraded process creation events this rule helps uncover attempts to execute renamed binaries that deviate from expected paths. Such evasion tactics are commonly used to maintain stealth and persistence, or perform living off the land application bypass.
name: Masquerading via Renamed System Binaries
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'Behavioral Indicators' and indicator.name = 'DifferentOriginalFilename' and src.process.displayName in ('Microsoft (R) HTML Application host', 'Microsoft ® Console Based Script Host', 'Microsoft ® Windows Based Script Host', 'CertUtil.exe', 'Microsoft Connection Manager Profile Installer', 'IE Per-User Initialization Utility', 'msxsl.exe', 'Execute processes remotely', 'Registry Console Tool', 'Microsoft(C) Register Server', 'Windows host process (Rundll32)', 'Windows Problem Reporting', 'WMI Commandline Utility', 'TCPIP Finger Command') and not (src.process.name in:anycase ('mshta.exe', 'cscript.exe', 'wscript.exe', 'certutil.exe', 'cmstp.exe', 'IE4UINIT.EXE', 'msxsl.exe', 'psexec.exe', 'psexec.c', 'psexec64.exe', 'reg.exe', 'regsvr32.exe', 'upwreg.exe', 'rundll32.exe', 'wermgr.exe', 'WerFault.exe', 'wmic.exe', 'UCPD-fix.exe', 'bssafe.exe', 'finger.exe')) and not (src.process.name matches '^\\$\\$DeleteMe.*$|^cscript-|^jmsagent-|^MSI[A-Za-z0-9]+\\.tmp')

subQueries.subQuery: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and ((tgt.process.name = 'shred' and tgt.process.cmdline contains (' -u ', ' --remove', ' -z ', ' --zero')) or (tgt.process.name in ('mkfs', 'mke2fs', 'wipe'))) and not (src.process.name in ('kubelet', 'logrotate', 'mkuserimg_mke2fs'))
dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'File Deletion'

description: Detects the execution of data erasure tools such as shred, mkfs, mke2fs, or wipe followed by a high volume of file deletions on a Linux endpoint. This pattern may indicate an adversary attempting to destroy evidence, disrupt operations, or cause irreversible damage by securely wiping or formatting data. Such behavior is commonly observed during ransomware attacks, insider threats, or post-exploitation cleanup activities. Monitoring for this sequence helps identify destructive actions before critical data loss occurs.
name: Massive Data Deletion via Data Erasure Tools

description: Detects the execution of commands on Linux systems that are used to access the memory space of running processes, specifically by reading from memory-related files within the operating system. Adversaries may abuse this behavior to extract sensitive information, such as authentication credentials, encryption keys, or other confidential data that reside in process memory. This technique is commonly leveraged during the post-exploitation phase of an attack, where gaining access to in-memory secrets can facilitate privilege escalation, lateral movement, or persistent access within a compromised environment. By monitoring for attempts to read process memory, organizations can identify potential credential dumping activities, which pose significant risks to the confidentiality and integrity of systems and data.
name: Memory Dumping for Credentials
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.name in ("cat", "grep", "tail", "less", "more", "egrep", "fgrep") and (tgt.process.cmdline matches '\\/proc\\/\\d+\\/mem' or tgt.process.cmdline matches '\\/proc\\/\\d+\\/maps') and not (src.process.displayName in ('TaniumClient')) and not (src.process.parent.cmdline contains ('/var/arcticwolfnetworks/agent/scans/'))

description: Detects the execution of MemProcFS in conjunction with the WinPmem driver (e.g., winpmem_x64.sys). MemProcFS is a memory forensics tool that creates a filesystem representation of physical memory, and WinPmem is a driver commonly used to facilitate memory acquisition. This combination may be used by attackers to collect forensic artifacts, analyze credentials, or bypass endpoint detection.
name: MemProcFS Execution With WinPmem Driver Load
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Driver Load' and src.process.name = 'memprocfs.exe' and tgt.file.path matches '\\\\winpmem_x64\\.sys$'

description: Detects the potential execution of MeshAgent, a remote access tool commonly used for legitimate IT administration. However, threat actors have been observed abusing MeshAgent by renaming its binary in an attempt to bypass security controls and avoid detection. While MeshAgent itself is not inherently malicious, its ability to run silently in the background, maintain persistent connections to remote servers, and provide full remote control over a system makes it a powerful tool for adversaries. If deployed by an attacker, it can enable long-term unauthorized access, facilitate data exfiltration, and assist in lateral movement across the network—all while masquerading as a legitimate service.
name: MeshAgent Execution

description: Detects instances where the MeshCentral agent is observed executing commands with encoded arguments, and the agent's executable is located in a directory outside its typical installation path. While MeshCentral is a legitimate remote access tool, adversaries may deploy it in an unexpected location, to execute obfuscated commands for persistent, unauthorized remote access, potentially leading to data exfiltration, malware deployment, or further malicious activities.
name: MeshCentral Agent Executing Encoded Commands from Non-Standard Directory
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type='Process Creation' and src.process.displayName contains 'MeshCentral' and src.process.cmdline contains 'b64exec ' and not (src.process.image.path contains ('C:\\Program Files\\Mesh Agent\\', 'C:\\Program Files\\Bacon Unlimited\\'))

description: Detects the use of the sqlite3 utility to query message databases on macOS endpoints, such as those used by messaging apps (e.g., iMessage). Attackers may access these databases to read or extract sensitive messages for reconnaissance or data exfiltration. Monitoring for suspicious access to messaging-related SQLite databases can help identify potential privacy violations or unauthorized data access attempts.
name: Messages Enumeration via Sqlite3
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'osx' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.image.path contains "bin/sqlite3" and tgt.process.cmdline contains "Messages/chat.db" and not (src.process.image.path contains '/Applications/Raycast.app/')

description: Detects activity associated with the installation or execution of the Metasploit Framework on Unix or Linux systems. Metasploit is a widely used offensive security tool, often abused by adversaries for exploitation, privilege escalation, persistence, and command and control (C2). This rule monitors for behaviors such as cloning the official Metasploit GitHub repository, executing the installer script, invoking key Metasploit components like msfconsole or msfvenom, or initiating Meterpreter sessions. Such activity may indicate unauthorized red team operations or the early stages of a real-world attack. Detection of this behavior is critical for identifying and containing post-exploitation tool use in your environment.
name: Metasploit Activity on Unix based Systems

description: Detects mpextms.exe, the Microsoft Defender Browser Protection Native Messaging Host, executing from outside its standard installation directory. This behavior may indicate a DLL sideloading attempt where an adversary places the trusted Microsoft-signed binary in a user-writable directory alongside a malicious DLL, causing the binary to load the attacker's payload. This technique has been observed in active campaigns delivering the sideloading package via curl downloads or silent MSI installations from C2 infrastructure.
name: Microsoft Defender Browser Protection Native Messaging Host Execution from Non-Standard Directory

description: Detects the creation of Microsoft Management Console (.msc) file outside of its standard, trusted directories. Adversaries may craft a malicious .msc file and place it in a user-writable directory such as ProgramData or a user's profile directory. This technique is often used as part of a Hijack Execution Flow or 'Evil Twin' attack, where an adversary aims to have their malicious file executed instead of a legitimate one, often by manipulating a shortcut or path. The presence of a newly created .msc file in an unexpected location is a strong indicator of an attempt to establish persistence or achieve arbitrary code execution by masquerading as a legitimate administrative tool.
name: Microsoft Management Console File Created in a Non-Standard Location
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'File Creation' and tgt.file.name matches '\\.msc$' and tgt.file.path matches '\\\\[a-z]{2}-[A-Z]{2}\\\\' and not (tgt.file.path matches '\\\\Windows\\\\System32\\\\|\\\\Windows\\\\SysWOW64\\\\')

description: Detects user clicks on anonymizer URLs classified by Mimecast that arrived in an email. An adversary can abuse anonymizer services to mask their identity and hide the origin of malicious traffic, making it harder to trace their activities. These anonymizer URLs may link to phishing sites, malware-hosting pages, or other malicious content. By leveraging anonymizers, attackers can bypass security controls, evade detection, and deliver malicious payloads to unsuspecting users while concealing their true infrastructure. This activity could indicate an attempt to deceive users into visiting harmful destinations or downloading compromised content.
name: Mimecast Anonymizer URL Accessed
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Mimecast' AND actor.invoked_by = 'User Click' AND unmapped.category = 'Anonymizers' AND NOT (unmapped.action contains ('hold', 'block', 'bounce') OR unmapped.actions contains ('hold', 'block', 'bounce') OR unmapped.adminOverride contains ('hold', 'block', 'bounce'))

description: Detects user clicks on compromised websites classified by Mimecast, which were delivered via email. Adversaries can exploit these links to direct users to malicious sites hosting malware, phishing pages, or spam content. Such sites may be used to steal credentials, deliver malware, or compromise endpoints by leveraging malicious downloads or scripts. By embedding these links in seemingly legitimate emails, attackers can deceive users into inadvertently enabling malicious activity, resulting in unauthorized access, data theft, or further lateral movement within an environment.
name: Mimecast Compromised Site URL Accessed
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Mimecast' AND actor.invoked_by = 'User Click' AND unmapped.category in ('Compromised', 'Botnets', 'Attacker Controlled Infrastructure') AND NOT (unmapped.action contains ('hold', 'block', 'bounce') OR unmapped.actions contains ('hold', 'block', 'bounce') OR unmapped.adminOverride contains ('hold', 'block', 'bounce'))

description: Detects emails containing executable file attachments that have been delivered to a user's mailbox, as identified by Mimecast. An adversary can abuse this delivery method to distribute malicious payloads, including malware, ransomware, or other executables designed to compromise the recipient's system.
name: Mimecast Executable File Attachment Delivered
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Mimecast' and unmapped.route = 'inbound' and file.type in:anycase ('application/x-dosexec', 'application/x-msdownload', 'application/x-executable', 'application/vnd.microsoft.portable-executable') and not (unmapped.actionTriggered contains ('hold', 'block', 'bounce'))

description: Detects when a high volume of emails containing large archived attachments are sent to the same recipient. Sending multiple emails with large archived files to a single recipient may indicate an attempt to extract confidential or proprietary data out of the organization in a stealthy manner.
name: Mimecast High Volume Archived Attachments to Recipient

description: Detects unblocked email messages flagged by Mimecast as potential impersonation attempts. These emails are strong indicators of Business Email Compromise (BEC), a sophisticated phishing tactic in which attackers impersonate trusted entities to deceive recipients and manipulate them into performing malicious actions.
