s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (tgt.process.name contains 'attrib.exe' or tgt.process.displayName in:anycase ('Attribute Utility')) and tgt.process.cmdline matches 'attrib.*?(\\s\\+R\\s|\\s\\+S\\s|\\s\\+H\\s).*(\\.php\\b|\\.phtml\\b|\\.asp\\b|\\.ashx\\b|\\.asmx\\b|\\.cer\\b|\\.jsp\\b|\\.pl\\b|\\.cgi\\b|\\.py\\b|\\.rb\\b|\\.cfm\\b|\\.exe\\b|\\.vbs\\b|\\.js\\b|\\.ps1\\b|\\.bat\\b|\\.dll\\b|\\.inf\\b|\\.doc\\b|\\.xls\\b)'

description: Detects unauthorized or unusual file write operations targeting the SharePoint 'Layouts' directory. This directory is a critical component of SharePoint's web application infrastructure and is frequently targeted by adversaries seeking to introduce malicious web shells or tamper with application behavior. Unauthorized modifications in this location may indicate an attempt to gain persistence, escalate privileges, or facilitate lateral movement within the environment. Such activity aligns with known tactics where threat actors exploit web application directories to maintain access or execute arbitrary code.
name: Suspicious File Write Activity in SharePoint Layouts Directory
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type in ('File Creation', 'File Rename', 'File Modification') and (src.process.name in:anycase ('cmd.exe', 'powershell.exe', 'pwsh.exe') or src.process.displayName in ('Windows Command Processor', 'Windows PowerShell')) and tgt.file.path contains ('Program Files\\Common Files\\Microsoft Shared\\Web Server Extensions\\', 'Program Files (x86)\\Common Files\\Microsoft Shared\\Web Server Extensions\\') and tgt.file.path contains '\\TEMPLATE\\LAYOUTS\\' and (tgt.file.extension in:anycase ('asax', 'ascx', 'ashx', 'asmx', 'asp', 'aspx', 'bat', 'cmd', 'cer', 'config', 'hta', 'jsp', 'jspx', 'php', 'ps1', 'vbs'))

description: Detects abnormal file write operations targeting the Volume Shadow Copy Service (VSS) directory. Threat actors may attempt to write files into VSS locations to conceal malicious activity from standard monitoring and forensic tools, since these directories are rarely accessed by legitimate applications. Such behavior deviates from normal system operations and may indicate attempts to hide tools and payloads within shadow copies for anti-forensic purposes.
name: Suspicious File Write Activity in Volume Shadow Copy (VSS) Directory
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type in ('File Creation', 'File Modification') and tgt.file.path contains '\\Windows\\vss\\' and tgt.file.extension in ('bat', 'cmd', 'dat', 'dll', 'exe', 'hta', 'js', 'jse', 'pif', 'ps1', 'scr', 'vbe', 'vbs', 'wsh', 'zip')

description: Detects the Microsoft Installer (msiexec.exe) launching a browser instance (likely Chromium-based) with flags for headless mode and remote debugging. This is an anomalous behavior often used by malicious MSI files to execute hidden browser-based payloads or establish C2 channels.
name: Suspicious Headless Browser via Msiexec
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and src.process.cmdline contains 'msiexec.exe' and tgt.process.cmdline contains '--no-first-run' and tgt.process.cmdline contains '--headless=true' and tgt.process.cmdline contains '--remote-debugging-port='

subQueries.subQuery: src.process.name in ('curl', 'wget', 'fetch', 'scp', 'sftp') and (tgt.file.name matches '^\\.' or tgt.file.path contains '/.') and not (tgt.file.path contains ('/opt/homebrew/', '/.venv', '/.cache'))
tgt.process.name = 'xattr' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'xattr -c' and tgt.process.cmdline contains '/.'

description: Detects the creation of hidden files and attempts to bypass quarantine mechanisms on a system. While hidden files may be used for legitimate purposes, their creation in conjunction with quarantine bypass techniques can indicate malicious activity such as malware persistence, evasion of security controls, or unauthorized data storage. Monitoring these behaviors helps identify potential threats and unauthorized actions on endpoints.
name: Suspicious Hidden File Creation and Quarantine Bypass

description: Detects when a user enables the Hyper-V role and disables the Hyper-V management tools within a short time frame, which may indicate an attempt to hide malicious virtualization activities. Adversaries may leverage Hyper-V for defense evasion or to isolate malicious workloads while intentionally removing management visibility to evade detection. This suspicious sequence suggests potential abuse of virtualization capabilities while deliberately limiting administrative oversight and monitoring capabilities.
name: Suspicious Hyper-V Execution

description: Detects instances of rundll32.exe executing INF installation scripts via system libraries like syssetup.dll or setupapi.dll. While rundll32.exe is a legitimate Windows utility for executing code within DLLs, adversaries can abuse it to run INF files, effectively executing malicious commands under the guise of trusted system activity. This technique is used to bypass traditional security controls by proxying execution through signed Windows binaries. By leveraging rundll32 in this manner, attackers may execute payloads, establish persistence, or escalate privileges without triggering standard behavioral detections.
name: Suspicious INF Execution via Rundll32
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe', 'cmd.exe') or src.process.displayName in:anycase ('Windows PowerShell', 'Windows Command Processor')) and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('rundll32.exe') or tgt.process.displayName = 'Windows host process (Rundll32)') and tgt.process.cmdline matches '(syssetup|setupapi)\\.dll' and tgt.process.cmdline matches '(SetupInfObjectInstallAction|InstallHinfSection)' and not (src.process.parent.image.path contains '\\Program Files' OR src.process.user contains ('SYSTEM', 'AUTORITE NT', 'SISTEMA'))

description: Detects the execution of suspicious inline JavaScript commands using Node.js on Windows endpoints, which may indicate malicious activity. This rule identifies processes where 'node.exe' is used to execute commands that include 'http', 'execsync', 'spawn', 'path', and 'zlib', suggesting an attempt to leverage Node.js for unauthorized actions or to evade traditional security measures. By monitoring these specific command-line parameters, the detection focuses on identifying potential script-based attacks that exploit Node.js capabilities to perform harmful operations. This behavior is significant as it can lead to unauthorized access, data exfiltration, or system compromise. The detection utilizes process creation events from 'SentinelOne' to ensure comprehensive monitoring of suspicious Node.js activities.
name: Suspicious Inline JavaScript Command Execution via Node.js

description: Detects the dropping of a consent.exe binary into user-writable locations via an interpreter chain. Adversaries can abuse this technique by placing a trojanized consent.exe or companion DLL in user space and invoking it to perform DLL sideloading or UAC-related elevation, enabling execution of arbitrary code with higher privileges. This method can be used to bypass application allowlists, evade detection by blending with a legitimate Windows executable, achieve persistence, and facilitate privilege escalation or lateral movement across the environment.
name: Suspicious Interpreter Chain Dropping Consent.exe to Userspace or ProgramData

description: Detects cmd.exe spawned from explorer.exe that combine a for loop construct with network download utilities within a single command line, a pattern strongly associated with ClickFix-style social engineering campaigns that trick users into manually executing malicious commands. Adversaries leverage the for /f loop to automate iterative payload staging, repeated network callbacks, or sequential post-compromise actions within a single command execution, reducing operator effort while reliably progressing the intrusion chain through multiple stages without requiring additional user interaction.
name: Suspicious Iterative Payload Staging via FOR Loop and Download Utilities
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and ((src.process.parent.name = 'explorer.exe' or src.process.parent.displayName contains 'Windows Explorer') and (src.process.name = 'cmd.exe' or src.process.image.originalFileName contains 'Cmd.exe') and ((src.process.cmdline contains ('iwr ', ' iwr', 'invoke-webrequest', 'curl ', ' curl', 'irm ', ' irm', 'invoke-restmethod', 'Net.WebClient', 'Net.http', 'wget ', ' wget', 'certutil') or src.process.cmdline matches '[A-Za-z]{1,64}\\^{1,64}[A-Za-z]{1,64}\\^{1,64}[A-Za-z]{1,64}') and src.process.cmdline contains 'for /f'))

subQueries.subQuery: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type in ('File Modification','File Creation') and tgt.file.extension = 'jar' and tgt.file.path matches '(:\\\\Users\\\\Public\\\\|:\\\\Users\\\\.{1,64}\\\\(Pictures|Videos|Music)\\\\|:\\\\PerfLogs\\\\|:\\\\ProgramData\\\\)'

dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('javaw.exe') or tgt.process.displayName contains 'OpenJDK Platform binary') and tgt.process.image.path matches '(:\\\\Users\\\\Public\\\\|:\\\\Users\\\\.{1,64}\\\\(Pictures|Videos|Music)\\\\|:\\\\PerfLogs\\\\|:\\\\ProgramData\\\\)' and tgt.process.cmdline matches '(:\\\\Users\\\\Public\\\\|:\\\\Users\\\\.{1,64}\\\\(Pictures|Videos|Music)\\\\|:\\\\PerfLogs\\\\|:\\\\ProgramData\\\\).{1,64}\\.jar'

description: Detects the creation or modification of a JAR file in suspicious directories such as Public folders, user media directories (Pictures, Videos, Music), PerfLogs, or ProgramData, followed by execution via Java runtime (javaw.exe or OpenJDK). Adversaries commonly drop malicious JAR files in non-standard directories to evade detection and achieve initial access or execute payloads. This correlation identifies both the file staging and subsequent execution, which may indicate malware deployment, persistence mechanisms, or post-exploitation activity using Java-based malicious code.
name: Suspicious JAR File Creation and Execution from Uncommon Directory

description: Detects the execution of JavaScript files via scheduled tasks that are registered with suspicious paths. This behavior may indicate an attempt to execute malicious scripts or maintain persistence on the system. Monitoring for such activity is crucial to identify and respond to potential security threats.
name: Suspicious Javascript Execution via Registered Scheduled Task
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.category = 'scheduled_task' and event.type contains ('Task Register') and task.path contains ('appdata', 'temp') and task.path matches '\\.js$'

description: Detects file rename events where a DAT file is converted to a JavaScript file using Wscript, a technique often employed by the Gootloader malware. This tactic is often employed in the later stages of a compromise, allowing attackers to evade detection by security products that may overlook file extensions or rely solely on signature-based detection. The conversion of file types is an attempt to obfuscate the true nature of the attack, making it harder for defenders to identify malicious activity.
name: Suspicious JavaScript File Rename via Wscript

description: Detects execution of a suspicious KeePass binary, which may indicate the presence of a trojanized or fake password manager application. Adversaries may use modified KeePass executables to harvest credentials, monitor user activity, or bypass security controls. This tactic is often used to impersonate trusted software in order to gain user trust and exfiltrate sensitive information such as stored passwords and secrets.
name: Suspicious KeePass Executable Detected

description: Detects potential Kerberoasting activity by monitoring script block commands. Kerberoasting is a method where attackers request service tickets for Kerberos accounts, then extract and attempt to crack the hashed passwords offline to gain unauthorized access to service accounts. This activity is often seen in post-exploitation scenarios, where attackers seek to escalate privileges or move laterally within the network.
name: Suspicious Kerberoasting Command Script
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' AND endpoint.os = 'windows' AND event.type in ('Command Script', 'Process Creation') AND ((cmdScript.content contains 'kerberoast' AND cmdScript.content matches '^.{1,1000}$') or (cmdScript.content contains ('$krb5tgs$23$', '$krb5asrep$23$', 'asreproast')) or (tgt.process.cmdline contains ('$krb5tgs$23$', '$krb5asrep$23$', 'asreproast')))

description: Detects attempts to modify critical kernel parameters related to memory management and system security on Linux endpoints. Adversaries may abuse this behavior to alter system performance, disable security controls, or evade detection by changing how the operating system manages memory, handles debugging, or enforces process isolation. Modifying these parameters can allow attackers to increase the number of huge pages for more efficient memory usage during malicious operations, clear system caches to remove forensic evidence, reduce the system’s ability to monitor or respond to hardware faults, or weaken restrictions on process tracing and performance monitoring. Such changes can undermine the integrity and security of the operating system, potentially enabling privilege escalation, persistence, or stealthy execution of malicious code. This activity is uncommon in standard user workflows and is often associated with advanced threat actors attempting to manipulate the underlying system environment for malicious purposes.
name: Suspicious Kernel Parameter Modification
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and ((event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.name = 'sysctl' and tgt.process.cmdline contains (' -w ', ' --write ') and tgt.process.cmdline contains (' vm.nr_hugepages', ' vm.swappiness', ' kernel.nmi_watchdog', ' kernel.perf_event_paranoid', ' kernel.yama.ptrace_scope')) or (event.type = 'File Modification' and (tgt.file.path matches '/proc/sys/vm/(nr_hugepages|swappiness|drop_caches)' or tgt.file.path matches '/sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/(enabled|defrag)') and not (src.process.name in ('dockerd', 'containerd', 'docker', 'executor', 'buildkitd')))) and not (src.process.image.path matches '/opt/(cinc|chef|puppetlabs)/') and not (src.process.cmdline contains '/usr/sbin/sosreport')

description: Detects attempts to archive or export macOS Keychain items, which may indicate an attempt to exfiltrate sensitive credentials such as passwords, certificates, or private keys. Attackers may use this technique to gain unauthorized access to stored credentials for lateral movement or privilege escalation. Monitoring for Keychain archival activity helps identify potential credential theft attempts.
name: Suspicious Keychain Archival
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'osx' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (tgt.process.image.path matches 'bin/zip$' or tgt.process.image.path matches 'bin/tar$' or (tgt.process.image.path matches 'bin/ditto$' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ' -c ') or tgt.process.image.path matches 'bin/cpio$' or tgt.process.image.path matches 'bin/7z$' or tgt.process.image.path matches 'bin/gzip$' or tgt.process.image.path matches 'bin/bzip2$' or tgt.process.image.path matches 'bin/bzip$' or tgt.process.image.path matches 'bin/xz$' or tgt.process.image.path matches 'bin/hdiutil$' or tgt.process.image.path matches 'bin/rar$')  and tgt.process.cmdline contains "/Library/Keychains/"

description: Detects execution of known Linux enumeration and privilege escalation tools. These tools are often used by attackers during the post-exploitation phase to identify system weaknesses, gather credentials, or escalate privileges. Monitoring their use can help identify unauthorized access or lateral movement attempts on Linux systems.
name: Suspicious Linux Enumeration Scripts Executed
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' AND endpoint.os = "linux" event.category = "process" and tgt.process.cmdline contains ("/linpeas.sh", "/lse.sh", "/linuxprivchecker", "/mimipenguin", "/3snake")

description: Detects processes that access LSASS memory while loading debugging-related libraries typically used for creating process dumps. This behavior is frequently associated with credential dumping tradecraft, where adversaries attempt to extract authentication material from LSASS. Such access is uncommon for standard applications and may indicate post-compromise activity, though legitimate forensic or security tools can occasionally exhibit similar patterns.
name: Suspicious LSASS Access via Debugging Libraries
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Open Remote Process Handle' and tgt.process.name in:anycase ('lsass.exe') and src.process.image.path contains (':\\Perflogs\\', ':\\Temp\\', ':\\Users\\Public\\', '\\$Recycle.Bin\\', '\\AppData\\Roaming\\', '\\Contacts\\', '\\Desktop\\', '\\Documents\\', '\\Downloads\\', '\\Favorites\\', '\\Favourites\\', '\\inetpub\\wwwroot\\', '\\Music\\', '\\Pictures\\', '\\Start Menu\\Programs\\Startup\\', '\\Users\\Default\\', '\\Videos\\', '\\Windows\\Temp\\') and src.process.cmdline contains ('dbgcore.dll', 'dbghelp.dll')

description: Detects use of the legitimate Windows debugging tool adplus.exe to create a memory dump of the lsass.exe process. Since adplus.exe is a trusted binary, its abuse can bypass traditional security controls. Monitoring its execution against sensitive processes like LSASS is critical to identifying credential dumping attempts.
name: Suspicious LSASS Memory Dump via Adplus
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.name = 'adplus.exe' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'lsass'

description: Detects command-line activity on macOS systems indicative of attempts to access or exfiltrate browser cookie data. Such behavior is often associated with credential theft or session hijacking, as adversaries seek to compromise user authentication tokens stored in browser cookies. This technique is leveraged in post-exploitation scenarios to escalate privileges or maintain unauthorized access. Unusual command-line access to browser storage locations may signal malicious intent or unauthorized data collection.
name: Suspicious macOS Browser Cookie Access via Command-Line Execution
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'osx' and event.type in ('File Modification', 'File Creation') and src.process.name in:anycase ('cp', 'mv', 'rsync', 'ditto', 'tar') and src.process.cmdline matches ('/Users/[^/]+/Library/Application Support/Google/Chrome/Default/Cookies', '/Users/[^/]+/Library/Application Support/Firefox/Profiles/.+/cookies.sqlite', '/Users/[^/]+/Library/Application Support/Slack/Cookies', '/Users/[^/]+/Library/Application Support/Slack/storage/', '/Users/[^/]+/Library/Containers/com.tinyspeck.slackmacgap/Data/Library/Application Support/Slack/storage', '/Users/[^/]+/Library/Application Support/Arc/User Data/Default/Cookies')

description: Detects the creation of memory dump files for Microsoft Office and collaboration applications. Adversaries may create these dumps to extract sensitive information such as credentials or encryption keys. Unexpected dump file creation may indicate malicious activity targeting credential access or data exfiltration.
name: Suspicious Memory Dump File Creation for Microsoft App
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'File Creation' and not (src.process.name in:anycase ('werfault.exe')) and tgt.file.path matches ('winword(\\.exe)?\\.dmp$', 'outlook(\\.exe)?\\.dmp$', 'onenote(\\.exe)?\\.dmp$', 'teams(\\.exe)?\\.dmp$', 'excel(\\.exe)?\\.dmp$', 'powerpnt(\\.exe)?\\.dmp$', 'onedrive(\\.exe)?\\.dmp$')

description: Detects command shell activity where a new process is launched with its window state set to minimized. This behavior can indicate an attempt to reduce user visibility of interactive execution and blend malicious activity into normal desktop operations. Adversaries commonly leverage minimized window execution as a form of defense evasion to conceal execution during post-compromise actions.
name: Suspicious Minimized CMD Execution with Delayed Variable Expansion
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and (tgt.process.name = 'cmd.exe' or tgt.process.displayName contains 'Windows Command Processor') and (tgt.process.cmdline contains '/c' and tgt.process.cmdline contains '/v:on' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'start' and tgt.process.cmdline contains '/min')

description: Detects suspicious modification to the contents of Kace log files, which may indicate attempts to tamper with audit trails or conceal malicious activity. Adversaries often target log files to disrupt forensic investigations and hinder incident response efforts. This behavior is consistent with log manipulation techniques used to evade detection and obscure evidence of compromise within managed environments.
name: Suspicious Modification of Kace Log File
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Command Script' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe') or src.process.displayName = 'Windows PowerShell') and cmdScript.content contains 'stop-service' and cmdScript.content contains 'konea' and cmdScript.content contains 'Get-Content' and cmdScript.content contains 'Where-Object' and cmdScript.content contains 'notmatch' and cmdScript.content contains 'Set-Content' and cmdScript.content contains 'Move-Item' and cmdScript.content contains 'Destination'

description: Detects suspicious file operations such as mv, cp, or touch targeting macOS launch agent and daemon property list files (*.plist) within the /System/Library/LaunchAgents and /System/Library/LaunchDaemons directories. This rule identifies attempts to create, modify, or manipulate persistence configurations by unauthorized or untrusted processes, which may indicate malware establishing or maintaining persistence on the system. Such activity may signify stealthy persistence installation, system compromise, or post-exploitation behavior. It is essential to monitor and analyze the involved processes, their sources, and the details of the plist modifications for signs of malicious activity
name: Suspicious Modification of Launch Plists
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = "osx" and event.type = "Process Creation" and tgt.process.image.path in:anycase ("/bin/cp", "/bin/mv", "/bin/mkdir", "/bin/touch") and tgt.process.cmdline contains ("/Library/LaunchAgents/com.", "/Library/LaunchDaemons/com.") and src.process.parent.name in:anycase ('bash', 'dash', 'ash', 'zsh', 'csh', 'ksh', 'sh', 'tcsh', 'osascript') and not (src.process.user = 'root')

description: Detects Windows process creations where a process with mshta.exe in it's ancestry runs python using Powershell. This likely indicates mshta/PowerShell-based retrieval or staging of a Python script.
name: Suspicious Mshta Web Download Of Python Script

description: Detects the execution of rundll32.exe invoking mshtml.dll, PrintHTML. This technique is commonly abused to execute malicious HTML or script content via a legitimate Windows binary (LOLBin), enabling adversaries to bypass application controls, deliver payloads, or execute code without dropping traditional executables. Monitoring for this behavior can help identify potential exploitation, initial access, or defense evasion activities.
name: Suspicious Mshtml PrintHTML Execution via Rundll32
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and src.process.name in:anycase ('rundll32.exe') AND src.process.cmdline matches 'Mshtml.dll,\\s*PrintHTML' and not (src.process.parent.image.path contains 'C:\\program files') and not (tgt.process.image.path contains 'C:\\program files') and not (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('splwow64.exe', 'PrintIsolationHost.exe', 'prpipe.exe')) and not (tgt.process.displayName contains ('Canon Advanced Printing Technology', 'Canon PageComposer Queue Manager')) and not (tgt.process.image.path contains '\\WINDOWS\\System32\\spool\\drivers\\')

description: Detects the execution of Windows Installer (msiexec.exe) to install an .msi package directly from a remote server. This behavior is uncommon in typical enterprise environments and may indicate initial access or lateral movement by an attacker attempting to install malware or tools without dropping files to disk. This behavior is often associated with initial access, lateral movement, or the deployment of tools during post-exploitation stages. Successful execution may allow an attacker to silently install backdoors, downloaders, or credential theft utilities, potentially leading to persistence, privilege escalation, or data exfiltration. Organizations should monitor for this type of activity as it deviates from standard software installation practices and could indicate a compromised system or an ongoing attack.
name: Suspicious MSI Installation from Remote URL
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and src.process.name = 'msiexec.exe' and src.process.cmdline matches 'msiexec\\.exe"?.*http(s):[\\/A-Za-z0-9.]+\\.msi' and src.process.cmdline matches '\\/(quiet|passive|qn)'

description: Detects suspicious network connections originating from unsigned CPL (Control Panel) files. An adversary can abuse this to deliver an initial-access payload by executing a malicious CPL file that establishes a network connection, which can be used for command-and-control communication, to download further stages of an attack, or to exfiltrate sensitive data.
name: Suspicious Network Activity from Unsigned CPL Files
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and src.process.displayName = 'Windows host process (Rundll32)' and src.process.activeContent.signedStatus = 'unsigned' and (event.type in ('GET', 'POST', 'IP Connect')) and src.process.activeContent.path contains '.cpl' and not (src.process.activeContent.path contains 'C:\\Windows\\System32' or src.process.activeContent.path contains 'C:\\Windows\\Syswow64' or src.process.activeContent.path contains 'C:\\Program Files')

description: Detects suspicious command-line execution of the Node.js interpreter indicative of credential-harvesting logic targeting Chromium-based browser credential stores, extension data, wallet artifacts, and key material. The rule identifies Node.js processes invoking browser password extraction, DPAPI decryption routines, and wallet exfiltration functions that are not characteristic of legitimate development workflows.
name: Suspicious Node.js Interpreter Credential Harvesting Execution Patterns

description: Detects obfuscated PowerShell that downloads a file from the internet and then executes it on the host. Adversaries commonly abuse this download cradle pattern as an initial-access or stager step — pulling a payload into a user-writable location and immediately running or by dropping a script-host file that auto-executes. Heavy use of environment-variable expansion or for /f token substitution is treated as a strong obfuscation signal.
name: Suspicious Obfuscated PowerShell Web Download and Execution

description: Detects instances where the OneDrive Standalone Updater is executed from the Windows Command Prompt alongside exploitation-related behavioral indicators. While the OneDrive updater is a legitimate Microsoft process, adversaries may abuse it to execute malicious payloads, establish persistence, or facilitate unauthorized network communication. Observing abnormal execution patterns may indicate command-and-control (C2) activity, privilege escalation attempts, or defense evasion techniques. Monitoring this behavior helps identify potential abuse of trusted processes in post-exploitation scenarios.
name: Suspicious OneDrive Updater Execution with Exploitation Behavior

description: Detects processes using command-line utilities such as copy, robocopy, or PowerShell cmdlets like Get-ChildItem and Copy-Item to access or copy Outlook OST files from the user’s profile directory. Outlook OST files contain locally cached mailbox data and are a valuable target for attackers seeking to collect sensitive email content. Under normal conditions, these files are accessed primarily by OUTLOOK.EXE, making command-line interaction with them uncommon. Adversaries may copy OST files to stage mailbox data for offline analysis, credential harvesting, or further exfiltration. Monitoring command-line activity targeting OST file paths helps detect mailbox data collection before data is compressed or transferred off the system. This behavior may indicate early-stage data collection or preparation for exfiltration.
name: Suspicious OST File Enumeration or Copy Activity
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (tgt.process.name matches '(copy|robycopy)' or tgt.process.cmdline contains '(copy-item|get-childitem)') and (tgt.process.cmdline contains '\\AppData\\Local\\Microsoft\\Outlook\\' and tgt.process.cmdline contains '.ost') and src.process.integrityLevel != 'SYSTEM'

description: Detects modifications to package manager configuration files by processes other than legitimate package management utilities on Linux systems. Package manager configurations control software sources and repository settings, making them high-value targets for supply chain attacks. Adversaries may modify these files to redirect package installations to malicious repositories, enabling the deployment of trojanized packages or backdoored software. By monitoring for configuration changes made by unauthorized processes, this detection helps identify potential supply chain compromise attempts, where attackers manipulate trusted software distribution channels to deliver malicious code through seemingly legitimate update mechanisms.
name: Suspicious Package Manager Configuration Files Modification

description: Detects Lanman or Spooler impersonation from a Microsoft-signed worker server process. This indicates an attacker exploited an internet-facing application and is now attempting to elevate privileges using various exploits such as BadPotato or PetitPotato. Please enable the 'LanmanImpersonation' and 'SpoolerImpersonation' Behavioral Indicators make this rule work.
name: Suspicious Pipe Impersonation in Server Process
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Behavioral Indicators' and indicator.name in ('LanmanImpersonation', 'SpoolerImpersonation') and src.process.displayName in ('IIS Worker Process', 'SQL Server Windows NT - 64 Bit') and src.process.signedStatus = 'signed' and src.process.verifiedStatus = 'verified' and src.process.publisher = 'MICROSOFT WINDOWS'

description: Detects registry modifications to Windows Port Monitor settings where attackers configure malicious DLLs to be loaded by the print spooler service (spoolsv.exe) at system startup. This lesser-known persistence technique leverages the print spooler's SYSTEM-level privileges to execute attacker-supplied DLLs stealthily during boot, ensuring persistence and potential privilege escalation. Monitoring these registry changes helps uncover adversaries blending malicious activity into normal system operations and maintaining unauthorized access across reboots.
name: Suspicious Port Monitor Registry Modification
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' AND endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Registry Value Modified' and registry.keyPath matches "\\\\system\\\\(currentcontrolset[0-9]*|controlset[0-9]*)\\\\control\\\\print\\\\monitors\\\\" and registry.value contains '.dll'

description: Detects suspicious PowerShell commands that download a payload from a remote location, assign it a randomized filename, and execute the content. An adversary can abuse this technique to bypass static signature detection and establish an initial foothold within a network. By leveraging PowerShell to fetch secondary-stage malware or scripts, an attacker can automate the delivery of malicious tools and execute code without user interaction or visibility, often masking the activity behind legitimate system processes.
name: Suspicious PowerShell Download and Execute with Randomized Filename
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'File Creation' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe', 'pwsh.exe') or src.process.displayName in:anycase ('Windows PowerShell', 'pwsh')) and src.process.cmdline contains 'GetRandomFileName' and src.process.cmdline contains ('-Uri', 'Invoke-WebRequest', 'IWR ', 'Invoke-RestMethod', 'IRM ') and src.process.cmdline contains '-OutFile' and src.process.cmdline contains ('Invoke-Expression', 'IEX ', 'Start-Process')

description: Detects PowerShell command-line to set TLS 1.2 followed by invocation of a custom program entry point within the same process. This pattern is characteristic of in-memory payload staging, where attackers retrieve code over encrypted channels and execute it without touching disk to evade traditional controls.
name: Suspicious PowerShell Download and In-memory Program Invocation
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Command Script' and (src.process.name contains ('powershell.exe') or src.process.displayName = 'Windows PowerShell') and (cmdScript.content contains '[Net.SecurityProtocolType]::' and cmdScript.content contains 'Tls12' and cmdScript.content contains 'iwr' and cmdScript.content contains '-Uri' and cmdScript.content contains 'http') and cmdScript.content matches '\\[[A-Za-z0-9]{1,32}\\.Program\\]::Main\\(\\)'

description: Detects when a JavaScript file, such as update.js, executed via wscript.exe initiates a PowerShell process. This behavior is commonly associated with the SocGholish malware campaign, where adversaries use malicious JavaScript to silently download and execute additional payloads via PowerShell. By leveraging this technique, attackers can bypass traditional defenses and gain control over the system, often leading to further exploitation such as data theft, remote access, or persistence.
name: Suspicious PowerShell Download via JavaScript Execution

description: Detects instances where PowerShell is executed by the Windows Task Scheduler service process with potentially malicious command line parameters. This behavior is often used by attackers to execute obfuscated or encoded PowerShell payloads via scheduled tasks, enabling persistence or remote code execution. The rule focuses on identifying PowerShell executions originating specifically from the Task Scheduler service to reduce false positives from other legitimate PowerShell activities. This detection can help uncover stealthy malware that abuses scheduled tasks to run malicious scripts or commands under the guise of normal system operations.
name: Suspicious PowerShell Execution from Task Service
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and src.process.cmdline contains ('svchost.exe -k netsvcs -p -s Schedule') and tgt.process.displayName contains ('Windows PowerShell') and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('Invoke-Expression', 'iex ', '-e ', '-ec ', '-enc ', '-enco ', '-encodedcommand ')

description: Detects the execution of PowerShell scripts containing command-line arguments associated with DNSCat, a tool used for establishing command-and-control (C2) channels over DNS. While DNS is a legitimate protocol for network communication, adversaries may abuse DNSCat to create covert communication tunnels, exfiltrate data, or execute remote commands without detection. The presence of arguments such as dnsserver, domain, presharedsecret, and exec strongly indicates the use of DNSCat for malicious activity. Monitoring for these patterns is crucial to detect DNS tunneling attempts, disrupt potential C2 channels, and protect against data exfiltration.
name: Suspicious PowerShell Execution of DNSCat Tool
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Command Script' and cmdScript.content contains 'dnsserver' and cmdScript.content contains 'domain' and cmdScript.content contains 'presharedsecret' and cmdScript.content contains 'exec'

description: Detects the execution of PowerShell scripts that perform screen capture using the .NET System.Drawing API to capture desktop contents. Adversaries employ this technique during post-exploitation to silently collect visual intelligence from compromised endpoints, capturing credentials, sensitive documents, or user activity without triggering visible windows. The captured image is subsequently encoded to Base64 or written directly to disk under the temp directory, staging it for exfiltration.
name: Suspicious PowerShell Screenshot Capture
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Command Script' and cmdScript.content contains '.CopyFromScreen' and cmdScript.content contains 'Drawing.Bitmap' and cmdScript.content contains '::FromImage' and src.process.cmdline contains 'WindowStyle Hidden' and cmdScript.content contains ('::ToBase64String', '::WriteAllText') and cmdScript.content contains '\\temp\\' and src.process.integrityLevel != 'SYSTEM'

description: Detects potential data exfiltration activity where PowerShell scripts or commands utilize web upload methods to send data to remote HTTP/FTP endpoints. These techniques are commonly used by attackers to exfiltrate sensitive data over the network using built-in PowerShell and .NET capabilities. While such behavior may also occur in legitimate administrative scenarios, monitoring this activity can help distinguish between authorized use and potential misuse.
name: Suspicious PowerShell Upload Activity to Remote Host
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Command Script' and src.process.displayName contains ('Windows PowerShell') and cmdScript.content contains ('.UploadFile', '.UploadData', '.UploadString', '.OpenWrite', 'ftp:') and (src.process.parent.cmdline contains 'svchost.exe -k netsvcs -p -s Schedule' OR src.process.parent.image.path contains ('\\AppData\\', '\\Windows\\Temp\\', '\\ProgramData\\')) and not (src.process.activeContent.path matches '\\\\program files')

description: Detects unsigned or untrusted privileged helper tools exhibiting unusual or unauthorized access to Keychain services. This behavior may indicate an attempt to extract sensitive credentials or manipulate authentication data, which is a common tactic in credential theft and privilege escalation attacks. Adversaries often target Keychain to gain access to stored secrets and further compromise system security. Monitoring for this activity helps identify early stages of credential access and potential lateral movement within the environment.
name: Suspicious Privileged Helper Tool Access to Keychain Services
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'osx' and event.type = 'Behavioral Indicators' and indicator.name = 'KeychainAccess' and indicator.metadata contains 'login.keychain-db' and src.process.image.path contains '/Library/PrivilegedHelperTools' and (src.process.signedStatus = 'unsigned' or src.process.reasonSignatureInvalid = *)

description: Detects suspicious child processes spawned by remote management and monitoring (RMM) tools. Adversaries frequently abuse legitimate RMM software for initial access and hands on keyboard activity, using it to execute commands that disable security controls or inhibit system recovery. These behaviors are uncommon in normal administrative workflows and strongly indicate malicious use of RMM tooling.
name: Suspicious Process Activity Spawned via RMM Tool
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.parent.displayName contains ('AnyDesk','screenconnect','Atelier ','AteraAgent','dwagent','meshcentral','N-able ','Pulseway','rustdesk','Supremo','Syncro.','Kabuto.','Tactical RMM','ToolsIQ','Quick Assist','Splashtop','PDQ Connect','GoTo Resolve','LogMeIn ','TeamViewer','Bomgar Support','Impero Client','Getscreen','Ninja RMM','Remote control tool','RCClient','Remote Desktop Manager','RemotePC ','Remote Utilities','Parallels Client','UltraViewer','Zoho Assist','XEOX Agent') or (src.process.parent.publisher contains ('LEVEL SOFTWARE, INC.','Superops Inc.'))) and ((tgt.process.name in:anycase ('reg.exe') and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('DisableAntiSpyware','DisableRealtimeMonitoring','DisableRealtimeProtection')) or (tgt.process.cmdline contains 'vssadmin' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'delete' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'shadows') or (tgt.process.cmdline contains 'bcdedit' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'recoveryenabled' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'no') or (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('wmic.exe') and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'shadowcopy' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'delete'))

description: Detects instances of rundll32.exe executing with the argument shell32.dll,Control_RunDLL, which is typically used to launch Control Panel applets. While this technique is a legitimate function within Windows, adversaries can abuse it to proxy execution through a trusted system binary, enabling the launch of malicious payloads or unauthorized system configuration tools. This method allows attackers to mask their activity as benign system behavior, potentially bypassing security controls and evading detection. By leveraging rundll32.exe in this way, threat actors may execute code, maintain persistence, or interact with system utilities in a stealthy manner.
name: Suspicious Process Creation via shell32.dll
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe', 'cmd.exe') or src.process.displayName in:anycase ('Windows PowerShell', 'Windows Command Processor')) and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('rundll32.exe') or tgt.process.displayName = 'Windows host process (Rundll32)') and tgt.process.cmdline matches 'shell32.dll[ ,]+(Control_RunDLL|ShellExec_RunDLL|#44)' and not (src.process.parent.image.path contains 'C:\\Windows\\ImmersiveControlPanel\\SystemSettings.exe' OR src.process.user contains ('SYSTEM', 'AUTORITE NT', 'SISTEMA'))

description: Detects a process enumerating or querying installed security tools such as antivirus, endpoint protection, or monitoring software. Unauthorized or unexpected enumeration can indicate reconnaissance activity by malware or attackers attempting to identify and evade security defenses. Monitoring this behavior helps identify potential threats and enhances endpoint security visibility.
name: Suspicious Process Enumerating Security Tools
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'osx' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.image.path contains ('/tmp/', '/.') or src.process.parent.image.path contains ('/tmp/', '/.'))  and (tgt.process.name in ('pgrep', 'grep', 'awk', 'sed', 'strings', 'find') and tgt.process.cmdline matches '(?i)(lulu|knockknock|blockblock|xprotect|SentinelAgent|sentinel_helper|crowdstrike|avast|\\seset|defender)') and not(tgt.process.cmdline contains 'sentinelone-token') and not(src.process.parent.cmdline contains '/Application Support/JAMF/')

description: Detects the execution of suspicious child processes initiated by .lnk (shortcut) files containing padded whitespace characters in their command-line arguments. An adversary can abuse this technique to obscure malicious arguments from user inspection, enabling the execution of malicious payloads while evading visual detection and security controls.
name: Suspicious Process Execution from LNK File with Hidden Arguments
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type in ('Process Creation', 'Behavioral Indicators') and (src.process.parent.image.path matches '\\.lnk$' or indicator.name = 'ProcessStartedFromLnk') and src.process.cmdline matches '\\s{20,}' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('cmd.exe', 'powershell.exe', 'pwsh.exe', 'bash.exe', 'sh.exe', 'wscript.exe', 'cscript.exe') or src.process.displayName in ('Windows PowerShell', 'Windows Command Processor', 'pwsh', 'bash.exe', 'sh.exe', 'Microsoft ® Windows Based Script Host', 'Microsoft ® Console Based Script Host'))

description: Detects Adobe Acrobat process execution with anomalous command-line parameters indicative of non-standard document handling or external content interaction. This behavior is security-relevant because it can signal attempts to abuse a trusted PDF reader to trigger follow-on execution, retrieve additional content, or manipulate user context. Such activity aligns with common adversary tradecraft that leverages legitimate applications to proxy malicious actions and evade straightforward process-based detections.
name: Suspicious Process Spawned by Adobe Acrobat
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and src.process.publisher = 'ADOBE INC.' and src.process.displayName = 'Adobe Acrobat' and (((tgt.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe', 'powershell_ise.exe', 'pwsh.exe') or tgt.process.displayName contains ('Windows PowerShell', 'pwsh')) and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('Invoke-Expression', 'invokeexpression', ' iex', 'iex ', 'Invoke-Command', 'invokecommand', ' icm', 'icm ', 'ScriptBlock', ' start', '-c ', '-command')) or ((tgt.process.name = 'cmd.exe' or tgt.process.displayName = 'Windows Command Processor') and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('/c')))

description: Detects the execution of suspicious child processes originating from EQNEDT32.exe. An adversary can abuse this by exploiting vulnerabilities in the Equation Editor to execute malicious code. The abuse typically begins when an adversary crafts a malicious Microsoft Office document embedded with an exploit targeting one of these vulnerabilities.
name: Suspicious Process Spawned by EQNEDT32.exe
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('EQNEDT32.exe') or src.process.displayName = 'Microsoft Equation Editor') and tgt.process.name in:anycase ('cscript.exe', 'powershell.exe', 'pwsh.exe', 'wscript.exe', 'cmd.exe', 'rundll32.exe', 'regsvr32.exe', 'mshta.exe', 'certutil.exe', 'bitsadmin.exe')

description: Detects process creation events where Microsoft Management Console launches child processes in a manner consistent with abnormal administrative console execution chains. This behavior is security-relevant because it can indicate an adversary leveraging a trusted management interface to execute code, stage payloads, or blend malicious activity into normal system administration telemetry. Adversaries commonly abuse signed, trusted system consoles as an execution proxy to evade controls and support post-compromise operations.
name: Suspicious Process Spawned by Microsoft Management Console (MMC)
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.parent.displayName = 'Microsoft Management Console' or src.process.parent.name = 'mmc.exe') and ((src.process.name contains ('cmd.exe', 'powershell.exe', 'pwsh.exe') or src.process.displayName contains ('Windows Command Processor', 'Windows PowerShell', 'pwsh')) and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('systeminfo.exe', 'nltest.exe', 'netstat.exe', 'netstat', 'ipconfig.exe', 'route.exe', 'route', 'arp', 'ping', 'tracert', 'nslookup', 'whoami.exe', 'whoami', 'quser.exe', 'qwinsta.exe', 'nbtstat.exe') or (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('wmic.exe') and tgt.process.cmdline contains ' get') or tgt.process.cmdline contains ('curl', 'invoke-webrequest', ' iwr', 'iwr ', 'invoke-restmethod', ' irm', 'irm ', 'Net.WebClient', 'wget', 'certutil'))) and not (src.process.cmdline contains 'AppDeployToolkit' or src.process.parent.cmdline contains '.msc')

description: Detects the spawning of suspicious child processes by OpenClaw, Moltbot, or Clawdbot AI coding agents. An adversary can abuse the functional design of these AI agents which are often granted extensive file system and network access through techniques such as prompt injection or the distribution of malicious skills via public registries like ClawHub. The rule specifically identifies instances where these agents initiate command shells, scripting interpreters, or known Living off the Land Binaries (LOLBins).
name: Suspicious Process Spawned By OpenClaw Agent
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and src.process.cmdline contains ('openclaw', 'moltbot', 'clawbot') and tgt.process.name in:anycase ('cmd.exe', 'powershell.exe', 'pwsh.exe', 'quser.exe', 'ipconfig.exe', 'whoami.exe', 'net.exe', 'tasklist.exe', 'taskkill.exe', 'reg.exe', 'netstat.exe', 'systeminfo.exe', 'hostname.exe', 'query.exe', 'arp.exe', 'route.exe', 'ping.exe', 'curl.exe', 'cscript.exe', 'pwsh.exe', 'wscript.exe', 'cmd.exe', 'rundll32.exe', 'regsvr32.exe', 'mshta.exe', 'certutil.exe', 'bitsadmin.exe', 'python', 'python3', 'base64', 'xattr', 'bash', 'dash', 'ash', 'zsh', 'csh', 'ksh', 'sh', 'tcsh', 'osascript', 'curl', 'cat', 'cp', 'chmod')

description: Detects the creation of a suspicious child process, such as a command shell or common reconnaissance tool, by the Internet Information Services (IIS) worker process (w3wp.exe) running a SharePoint application pool. This behavior is highly anomalous and is a strong indicator of post-exploitation activity. An attacker, after successfully exploiting a remote code execution (RCE) vulnerability like CVE-2025-53770, will often spawn a shell to execute further commands on the compromised server.
name: Suspicious Process Spawned by SharePoint IIS Worker Process
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.name contains 'w3wp.exe' or src.process.displayName contains 'IIS Worker Process') and src.process.cmdline contains 'SharePoint' and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('cmd.exe', 'powershell.exe', 'pwsh.exe', 'quser.exe', 'ipconfig.exe', 'whoami.exe', 'net.exe', 'tasklist.exe', 'taskkill.exe', 'reg.exe', 'netstat.exe', 'systeminfo.exe', 'hostname.exe', 'query.exe', 'arp.exe', 'route.exe', 'ping.exe', 'curl.exe') or tgt.process.displayName contains ('Windows Command Processor', 'Windows PowerShell', 'pwsh', 'Query User Utility', 'IP Configuration Utility', 'whoami - displays logged on user information', 'Net Command', 'Lists the current running tasks', 'Terminates Processes', 'Registry Console Tool', 'TCP/IP Netstat Command', 'Displays system information', 'Hostname APP', 'MultiUser Query Utility', 'TCP/IP Arp Command', 'TCP/IP Route Command', 'TCP/IP Ping Command', 'The curl executable'))

description: Detects suspicious child processes spawned by SmarterMail MailService.exe, which may indicate exploitation or post-exploitation activity such as CVE-2026-23760. This rule looks for shells, scripting engines, and other living-off-the-land binaries being launched by the mail service.
name: Suspicious Process Spawned by SmarterMail Service

description: Detects abnormal child processes of Veeam services, which may indicate exploitation. Successful exploitation could enable adversaries to execute arbitrary code on the targeted system, potentially compromising the affected environment and facilitating further malicious activities.
name: Suspicious Process Spawned by Veeam Service

description: Detects child processes launched by the Velociraptor agent that deviate from normal collection or monitoring activity. Adversaries can abuse Velociraptor, a trusted DFIR and monitoring tool, to execute arbitrary binaries or scripts, enabling them to expand control of a compromised system while blending into legitimate activity. This technique allows attackers to bypass security controls by misusing trusted software, potentially facilitating post-exploitation actions such as credential theft, lateral movement, persistence, or data exfiltration.
name: Suspicious Process Spawned by Velociraptor Agent
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and src.process.parent.name in:anycase ('Velociraptor.exe') and ((src.process.cmdline contains 'code.exe' and src.process.cmdline contains 'tunnel' and src.process.cmdline contains '--accept-server-license-terms') or (src.process.cmdline contains 'msiexec' and src.process.cmdline contains '/i' and src.process.cmdline contains 'http') or (src.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe', 'powershell_ise.exe', 'pwsh.exe') and (src.process.cmdline contains 'Invoke-WebRequest ' or src.process.cmdline contains 'IWR ' or src.process.cmdline contains '.DownloadFile' or src.process.cmdline contains '.DownloadString')))

description: Detects instances where WinRAR spawns an uncommon or suspicious child process. Such behavior may indicate adversary abuse of WinRAR for code execution, lateral movement, or payload deployment. Threat actors often leverage legitimate utilities like WinRAR to bypass security controls and execute unauthorized actions. Monitoring for unusual child processes originating from WinRAR helps identify potential post-exploitation or living-off-the-land techniques.
name: Suspicious Process Spawned by WinRAR
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.parent.name in:anycase ('winrar.exe', 'winrar') or src.process.parent.displayName in ('WinRAR archiver', 'WinRAR.exe', 'WinRAR')) and (src.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe', 'cmd.exe', 'mshta.exe', 'cscript.exe', 'wscript.exe', 'rundll32.exe') or src.process.displayName in ('Windows PowerShell', 'Windows Command Processor', 'Microsoft (R) HTML Application host', 'Microsoft ® Console Based Script Host', 'Microsoft ® Windows Based Script Host', 'Windows host process (Rundll32)'))

description: Detects potential proxy execution of commands or payloads using rundll32.exe to invoke exported functions from legitimate Windows libraries. Although rundll32.exe is designed to execute DLL functions, adversaries can exploit obscure or non-standard exports to launch external executables or perform malicious actions under the guise of a trusted system binary. This technique allows for execution through a signed Windows process, helping evade security controls and blend in with normal system activity. This method supports stealthy execution paths often used in initial access, persistence, and post-exploitation stages.
name: Suspicious Proxy Execution via Rundll32
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe', 'cmd.exe') or src.process.displayName in:anycase ('Windows PowerShell', 'Windows Command Processor')) and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('rundll32.exe') or tgt.process.displayName = 'Windows host process (Rundll32)') and tgt.process.cmdline matches '(pcwutl|zipfldr)\\.dll[ ,]+(launchapplication|routethecall)' and not (src.process.user contains ('SYSTEM', 'AUTORITE NT', 'SISTEMA'))

description: Detects Python pip execution using a custom package index URL combined with output redirection in the same process. Legitimate pip usage rarely combines these, whereas attackers redirect to a malicious PyPI mirror and suppress output to hide the installation. This technique is associated with dependency confusion and supply chain attacks targeting development environments and CI/CD pipelines.
name: Suspicious Python Pip Execution with Custom Package Index and Output Redirection
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Behavioral Indicators' and indicator.name = 'OutputRedirection' and src.process.cmdline matches 'python\\\.exe.*pip\\\.exe.*(--extra-index-url|--index-url|-i[= ])'

description: Detects execution of RegAsm.EXE without any command-line arguments or specified target files. This behavior deviates from typical usage, where RegAsm.EXE is invoked with explicit assemblies or flags, and may indicate adversary attempts to probe system defenses or execute the binary in an unintended manner. Such anomalous executions can be associated with process masquerading, process injection, defense evasion, or initial access techniques. Monitoring for this pattern helps identify potential misuse of native Windows utilities in attack chains.
name: Suspicious RegAsm.EXE Execution Without Arguments or Target Files
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('RegAsm.exe') or src.process.displayName = 'Microsoft .NET Assembly Registration Utility' or src.process.image.originalFileName = 'RegAsm.exe') and (src.process.cmdline matches '\\\\Windows\\\\Microsoft.NET\\\\Framework.*RegAsm\\.exe"$' or src.process.cmdline matches '\\\\Windows\\\\Microsoft.NET\\\\Framework.*RegAsm\\.exe$') and tgt.file.path != '*'

description: Detects a registry add operation initiated by a headless process context. This behavior is security-relevant because modifying registry values without interactive user activity can indicate covert system configuration changes used to maintain access or alter security settings. Adversaries commonly leverage registry modifications for persistence, defense evasion, or execution flow manipulation during post-compromise activity.
name: Suspicious Registry Modification with Headless Execution Flag
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type contains 'Registry' and ((src.process.name = 'reg.exe' or src.process.displayName = 'Registry Console Tool') and src.process.cmdline contains '/f' and registry.value contains '--headless')

description: Detects unauthorized modification of Windows Registry Run keys associated with persistence techniques attributed to TamperedChef activity. This behavior enables adversaries to maintain access by configuring malicious executables to launch at system startup. Such registry changes are a common tactic for establishing long-term footholds and evading standard security controls. Monitoring for these modifications helps identify potential intrusion or malware persistence attempts linked to TamperedChef operations.
name: Suspicious Registry Run Key Modification for Persistence by TamperedChef
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Registry Value Create' and (registry.keyPath contains 'Software\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\Run\\PDFEditorUpdater' or (registry.keyPath contains 'Software\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\Run\\' and registry.value contains ('1DDC0100000000000000000000000002000000', '--cm=')))

description: Detects regsvr32.exe loading a COM scriptlet from a remote location. This is the Squiblydoo technique. It abuses a trusted and signed Windows binary to execute code controlled by the attacker, delivered over the network rather than dropped to disk first, and frequently evades detection that relies on signatures.
name: Suspicious Remote Scriptlet Execution via Regsvr32

description: Detects the execution of a suspicious resource file generator initiated via a batch file launched by the command prompt. Adversaries may abuse this technique to process and compile files from the internet, potentially creating malicious payloads, such as executable files or scripts, that are used for further exploitation.
name: Suspicious Resource File Generator Execution

description: Detects suspicious execution of the Restic backup utility, often categorized as a potentially unwanted application (PUA) within an enterprise environment. Adversaries may abuse backup tooling to stage, encrypt, or exfiltrate data under the guise of legitimate backups, increasing impact and evasion potential. Activity may be legitimate during sanctioned backup operations by IT or DevOps teams, but warrants review when observed on atypical hosts or outside approved maintenance windows.
name: Suspicious Restic Backup Utility Execution
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and ((tgt.process.cmdline contains '--password-file' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'init' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ' -r ') or (tgt.process.cmdline contains '--use-fs-snapshot' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'backup' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ' -r ') or (tgt.process.cmdline contains ('sftp:', 'rest:http', 's3:s3.', 's3.http', 'azure:', ' gs:', 'rclone:', 'swift:', ' b2:') and tgt.process.cmdline contains ' init ' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ' -r '))

description: Detects execution of the built-in rpcping.exe utility, which can be abused to trigger authentication attempts to remote systems. This behavior may be used by attackers to coerce a host into sending NTLM credentials to a controlled endpoint, potentially leading to credential theft. Monitoring usage of this tool helps identify attempts to perform credential access using living-off-the-land techniques.
name: Suspicious RPCping Credential Capture Attempt
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' AND (src.process.name contains 'rpcping.exe' or src.process.displayName contains 'RPC Ping Utility') and src.process.cmdline contains 'privacy' and src.process.cmdline contains 'ntlm'

description: Detects usage of the Windows odbcconf.exe binary to load or execute response files (.rsp) or DLL payloads—techniques adversaries use to proxy execution through a signed/trusted binary (a LOLBin). Attackers abuse odbcconf to call REGSVR, or load an .rsp that in turn references a malicious DLL, enabling code execution or persistence while evading signature/process-based defenses. Monitoring this behavior helps identify living-off-the-land abuse, code injection/persistence attempts, and suspicious binary usage that normal allowlisting may miss
name: Suspicious RSP or DLL execution via odbcconf.exe
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.name contains 'odbcconf.exe' and (tgt.process.cmdline contains '.dll' or tgt.process.cmdline contains '.rsp') and not (src.process.name in:anycase ('acmsetup.exe', 'csrss.exe', 'InstallManager.exe'))

description: Detects rundll32.exe being used to invoke DLL functions by ordinal number, triggered from a command interpreter. This technique is often leveraged in post-exploitation to run payloads in a stealthy manner, as ordinals do not provide visibility into the specific function being executed. Monitoring such behavior is important because it indicates potential abuse of native Windows utilities to execute malicious code while evading detection mechanisms that rely on readable function names or standard execution flows. The regex in the query matches execution of rundll32 with the character # after the filename in the command.
name: Suspicious Rundll32 Execution by Ordinal Values
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe', 'cmd.exe') or src.process.displayName in:anycase ('Windows PowerShell', 'Windows Command Processor')) and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('rundll32.exe') or tgt.process.displayName = 'Windows host process (Rundll32)') and tgt.process.cmdline matches 'rundll32(.exe)?.*,(\\s+)?#([+0]+)?\\d' and not (tgt.process.cmdline contains ('\\Program Files', ':\\ProgramData\\FastTrack Software\\Admin By Request\\') OR src.process.user contains ('SYSTEM', 'AUTORITE NT', 'SISTEMA'))

description: Detects the use of Rundll32.exe to execute a file that does not have a .dll extension. While Rundll32.exe is intended to load and call functions from DLL files, adversaries may use it to launch files with other extensions (e.g., .dat, .tmp, .txt) as a method to disguise payloads, evade static detection, or bypass extension-based filters. Monitoring this technique is crucial, as it may indicate attempts to execute non-standard or obfuscated payloads using a trusted system binary. The regex in the query matches executions of Rundll32.exe where the filename ends with a non-DLL extension.
name: Suspicious Rundll32 Execution with Non-DLL File Extensions
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe', 'cmd.exe') or src.process.displayName in:anycase ('Windows PowerShell', 'Windows Command Processor')) and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('rundll32.exe') or tgt.process.displayName = 'Windows host process (Rundll32)') and tgt.process.cmdline matches 'rundll32(.exe)?["\\s]+[A-Za-z0-9-_]+\\.(?!dll)' and not (tgt.process.cmdline matches ' (inetcpl|sysdm|inetcol|imdisk)\\.cpl' OR src.process.user contains ('SYSTEM', 'AUTORITE NT', 'SISTEMA'))

description: Detects the execution of a process masquerading as RV-Tools, a legitimate VMware environment inventory tool. The parent process may be unsigned or falsely signed by known suspicious publishers, which could indicate an attempt to disguise malicious behavior under the guise of trusted administrative software, potentially for reconnaissance or payload staging.
name: Suspicious RV-Tools Execution
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.parent.cmdline contains:matchcase '\\RV-Tools' or src.process.parent.image.path contains ('RV-Tool', 'RVTool')) and (src.process.parent.signedStatus = 'unsigned' or not (src.process.parent.publisher contains ('Dell Technologies', 'DELL INC.'))) and (tgt.process.name in ('csc.exe', 'whoami.exe'))

description: Detects scheduled task executions where the target binary resides in potentially suspicious user or temporary directories such as AppData, Public, or Temp. Adversaries commonly use these locations to maintain persistence or evade detection by blending into legitimate system activity.
name: Suspicious Scheduled Task Execution via Uncommon Locations

description: Detects the creation of a scheduled task where the task name or associated binary mimics svchost.exe, a legitimate Windows system process. Adversaries often use masquerading techniques to make malicious tasks appear benign, reducing the likelihood of detection during manual inspection or automated analysis. Identifying such suspicious scheduled task creations is critical to uncovering persistence mechanisms and evasion techniques.
name: Suspicious Scheduled Task Masquerading as Svchost
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'Task Register' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('schtasks.exe') or src.process.displayName = 'Task Scheduler Configuration Tool') and src.process.cmdline contains 'svchost.exe'

description: Detects the registration of a scheduled task on a Windows system that mimics the name of a legitimate system process (e.g., “Windows Update Security”, “Microsoft Telemetry”) while referencing suspicious file paths such as AppData, Temp, ProgramData, or Startup. This technique is commonly used by adversaries to establish persistence while evading user suspicion by masquerading as trusted components or services. Monitoring for such activity can help identify early signs of compromise or malware persistence.
name: Suspicious Scheduled Task Masquerading as Windows Service
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os == 'windows' and event.type == 'Task Register' and (src.process.name = 'schtasks.exe' or src.process.displayName = 'Task Scheduler Configuration Tool') and task.name matches '(?i)(Windows\\s+Update\\s+Security.*|Windows\\s+Security.*|Windows\\s+Defender.*|Security\\s+Patches.*|System\\s+Maintenance.*|Microsoft\\s+Telemetry.*)' and src.process.cmdline matches '(?i)(programdata|appdata|temp|public|downloads|users|tmp|startup|recycle\\.bin|debug|fonts|inetpub|windows\\help|perflogs|roaming)'

description: Detects suspicious child process creation on Windows systems where ScreenConnect is the parent process. Adversaries may abuse ScreenConnect remote access sessions to execute unauthorized processes, escalate privileges, or move laterally within a network. This can lead to the deployment of backdoors, ransomware, or other malicious payloads, allowing for complete system compromise.
name: Suspicious ScreenConnect Child Process
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and src.process.parent.name contains 'ScreenConnect' and ((src.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe') and src.process.cmdline contains ('-enc', '-ec', 'downloadstring', 'Reflection.Assembly', 'http') and not (src.process.cmdline contains '\\SystemTemp\\ScreenConnect\\')) or (src.process.name in:anycase ('cmd.exe') and src.process.cmdline contains '/c' and src.process.cmdline contains 'run.cmd' and (tgt.process.cmdline contains ('-enc', '-ec', '-EncodedCommand', 'downloadstring', 'Reflection.Assembly') or (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('net.exe', 'net1.exe') and tgt.process.cmdline contains '/add' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('Administrators', 'Domain Admins')) or (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('schtasks.exe') and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'create') or (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('sc.exe') and (tgt.process.cmdline contains 'create' or (tgt.process.cmdline contains 'stop' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('WinDefend', 'Sense', 'SentinelAgent')))) or (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('reg.exe') and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('DisableAntiSpyware', 'DisableRealtimeMonitoring', '\\Exclusions\\')) or (tgt.process.cmdline contains 'vssadmin' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'delete') or tgt.process.cmdline contains 'bcdedit' or ((tgt.process.cmdline contains (':\\Temp\\', ':\\Users\\Public\\', ':\\PerfLogs\\', ':\\Windows\\', ':\\ProgramData\\') or (tgt.process.cmdline contains ':\\' and tgt.process.cmdline matches ':\\\\Users\\\\.{1,64}\\\\(Pictures|Videos|Music|Documents|Downloads|AppData)\\\\')) and not (tgt.process.cmdline contains (':\\Windows\\System32\\', ':\\ProgramData\\Microsoft\\Windows Defender\\', ':\\Windows\\SysWOW64\\', ':\\Windows\\SystemTemp\\ScreenConnect\\', ':\\Windows\\LTSvc\\', 'dotnet-sdk')) and not (tgt.process.name contains 'dotnet-sdk')))) or (src.process.name in:anycase ('net.exe') and src.process.cmdline contains '/add' and src.process.cmdline contains ('Administrators', 'Domain Admins')) or (src.process.name in:anycase ('schtasks.exe') and src.process.cmdline contains 'create') or (src.process.name in:anycase ('sc.exe') and (src.process.cmdline contains 'create' or (src.process.cmdline contains 'stop' and src.process.cmdline contains ('WinDefend', 'Sense', 'SentinelAgent')))) or (src.process.name in:anycase ('rundll32.exe') and not (src.process.cmdline contains ('url.dll', 'FileProtocolHandler', 'cryptext.dll'))) or (src.process.name in:anycase ('msiexec.exe') and src.process.cmdline contains 'i' and src.process.cmdline contains ('/q', '/quiet', '/qn', '-q', '-quiet', '-qn', '-Q+')) or (src.process.name in:anycase ('mshta.exe', 'certutil.exe', 'bitsadmin.exe', 'certreq.exe', 'curl.exe', 'ssh.exe', 'scp.exe', 'wget.exe')) or (src.process.name in:anycase ('wevtutil.exe') and not (src.process.cmdline contains ('install-manifest', 'uninstall-manifest'))) or (src.process.name in:anycase ('wscript.exe', 'cscript.exe') and not (src.process.cmdline contains ('gatherNetworkInfo.vbs', 'ScreenConnect.ClientUninstall.vbs'))) or (src.process.name in:anycase ('wmic.exe') and src.process.cmdline contains ('shadowcopy', 'delete', 'process call', 'useraccount')))

description: Detects the unauthorized modification of the SentinelOne AutoRepair scheduled task action. This behavior may indicate an attempt to escalate privileges to SYSTEM by running commands that masquerade as legitimate SentinelOne auto-repair tasks.
name: Suspicious SentinelOne AutoRepair Task Modification

description: Detects the use of script interpreters for creating symbolic links targeting shadow copies. Adversaries can abuse this technique to gain unauthorized access to sensitive data stored within shadow copies, even if access to the original files is restricted. By creating a symbolic link, an attacker can bypass file system permissions and directly access the contents of the shadow copy. This can be used to exfiltrate sensitive information, recover deleted files, or tamper with system configurations stored within the shadow copies, facilitating data breaches, privilege escalation, and persistence within a compromised environment.
name: Suspicious Shadow Copy Manipulation via Symlink
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' AND event.type = 'Process Creation' AND (src.process.name in:anycase ('cmd.exe', 'wt.exe') or src.process.displayName in ('Windows Command Processor', 'wt.exe')) AND src.process.cmdline contains 'mklink' AND src.process.cmdline contains 'HarddiskVolumeShadowCopy' AND NOT (src.process.cmdline contains 'C:\\shadowcopy')

description: Detects when a Redis server process spawns a shell on Linux systems. This behavior is highly unusual in normal Redis operations and often indicates exploitation activity. Redis servers should not typically spawn interactive shells and this pattern may indicate an attacker has successfully exploited a vulnerability in Redis, such as CVE-2025-49844, which allows for remote code execution. Adversaries can leverage such vulnerabilities to execute arbitrary commands, establish persistence, or pivot to other systems within the network. Monitoring for this behavior helps identify potential Redis server compromises at an early stage, allowing for rapid investigation and mitigation of the threat.
name: Suspicious Shell Spawned by Redis Server
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.name in ('bash', 'dash', 'ash', 'zsh', 'csh', 'ksh', 'sh', 'tcsh') and src.process.name = 'redis-server'

description: Detects potential exploitation of a vulnerability in VMware Tools (CVE-2025-41244) that could lead to local privilege escalation on Linux systems. This vulnerability exists in the VMware Tools service (vmtoolsd) and its service discovery scripts, which may execute programs to probe version strings with elevated privileges. Adversaries can exploit this vulnerability by placing malicious executables in writable locations (such as /tmp) that get executed by the VMware Tools service with higher privileges than the attacker initially possessed. This rule monitors for suspicious process creation events where vmtoolsd or its service discovery scripts spawn shell processes, which may indicate an attempt to leverage this vulnerability to gain unauthorized elevated access to the system. Early detection of such activity can help prevent successful privilege escalation attacks in virtualized environments.
name: Suspicious Shell Spawned by VMWare Tools
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and ((src.process.name = 'vmtoolsd') or (src.process.name in ('bash', 'dash', 'ash', 'zsh', 'csh', 'ksh', 'sh', 'tcsh') and src.process.cmdline contains '/open-vm-tools/serviceDiscovery/scripts/') or (src.process.image.path contains '/open-vm-tools/serviceDiscovery/scripts/')) and tgt.process.name in ('bash', 'dash', 'ash', 'zsh', 'csh', 'ksh', 'sh', 'tcsh') and not (tgt.process.cmdline contains '/usr/bin/lsb_release') and tgt.process.cmdline = *

description: Detects network retrieval activity initiated by the curl utility on macOS systems, specifically identifying executions configured to silently download content or instructions from external web endpoints. An adversary can abuse this native tool to facilitate stealthy command-and-control (C2) communications, retrieve secondary malicious payloads, or download configurations required to stage follow-on actions.
name: Suspicious Silent Curl External Network Activity
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'osx' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.name contains 'curl' and tgt.process.cmdline contains '-k -s' and tgt.process.cmdline contains '-H api-key:' and tgt.process.cmdline contains '/dynamic?txd'

description: Detects instances where the legitimate Windows DNS Server process (dns.exe) spawns a child process. Under normal circumstances, dns.exe does not create additional processes. Such behavior can indicate malicious activity, such as exploitation of the DNS service or abuse of the DNSAdmins group to load and execute arbitrary code.
name: Suspicious Subprocess Spawned by dns.exe
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and src.process.name = 'dns.exe' and NOT ((tgt.process.name in:anycase ('werfault.exe', 'conhost.exe', 'dns.exe')) or tgt.process.displayName in:anycase ('Windows Problem Reporting', 'Console Window Host') or (src.process.image.path contains 'Plesk\\bin\\dns.exe'))

subQueries.subQuery: indicator.metadata contains 'select * from win32_computersystem' and not (src.process.parent.name in:anycase ('AgentExecutor.exe', 'OSDRunPowerShellScript.exe') or src.process.parent.displayName in:anycase ('AgentExecutor', 'OSDRunPowerShellScript'))

indicator.metadata contains 'DsRoleGetPrimaryDomainInformation' and not (src.process.parent.name in:anycase ('AgentExecutor.exe', 'OSDRunPowerShellScript.exe') or src.process.parent.displayName in:anycase ('AgentExecutor', 'OSDRunPowerShellScript'))

event.type = 'DNS Resolved' and not (src.process.name in:anycase ('chrome.exe', 'msedge.exe', 'firefox.exe', 'ZSATunnel.exe', 'brave.exe', 'msedgewebview2.exe') or src.process.image.originalFileName in:anycase ('chrome.exe', 'msedge.exe', 'firefox.exe', 'ZSATunnel.exe', 'brave.exe', 'msedgewebview2.exe') or src.process.displayName in ('Google Chrome', 'Microsoft Edge', 'Firefox', 'ZscalerTunnel', 'Brave Browser', 'Microsoft Edge WebView2')) and event.dns.request matches '(?<![a-z0-9\\-\\.])(?=[a-z0-9]*\\d)[a-z0-9]{11,13}\\.life$'

description: Detects suspicious use of system and domain enumeration which is commonly used by attackers during reconnaissance. This behavior may indicate an attempt to gather domain or system information as a precursor to lateral movement or privilege escalation. The source process and outbound network activity can provide further context to determine if the activity is authorized or malicious.
name: Suspicious System and Domain Enumeration with Outbound DGA Connection Behavior

description: Detects the creation of a scheduled task configured to execute known credential access utilities or scripts. Adversaries may use scheduled tasks to persistently run tools designed to dump credentials, access password stores, or perform other forms of credential theft. This behavior is indicative of attempts to establish long-term access or harvest sensitive authentication data over time.
name: Suspicious Task Creation for Credential Harvesting
