s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Task Register' and src.process.name in ('schtasks.exe', 'powershell.exe') and src.process.cmdline contains ('/create', 'New-ScheduledTask') and src.process.cmdline matches '(?i)\\b(sekurlsa|mimikatz|lsass|sam|security|logonpasswords|dcsync|hashdump|kerberos|vault|dpapi|credential|creddump|gsecdump|wdigest|tspkg|ssp|netntlm|ntlm|logonpassword|cleartext|cachedump|enumcred|lsa|token|pth|pass-the-hash|aikatz|nanodump|procdump|secretsdump|lsadump|minidump|dumpcred|ntds|ntdsutil|system.bak|%SYSTEMROOT%\\\\ntds|Invoke-Mimikatz|Invoke-NanoDump|Invoke-CredentialPhisher|SharpDump|SharpSecDump|Rubeus|Tokenvator|Out-Minidump|Invoke-TokenManipulation|Find-Password|DecryptPassword|LaZagne|ADRecon)\\b' and src.process.cmdline contains '>'

description: Detects PowerShell scripts leveraging .NET Reflection, such as Reflection.Assembly, and methods like DownloadString to load executables or DLLs in memory from remote sources. This behavior, commonly seen in groups like AsyncRAT enables attackers to execute payloads without writing to disk, bypassing traditional security controls. Monitoring for commands using obfuscated URLs, hidden execution, or bypassed execution policies can help identify advanced malware or post-exploitation frameworks utilizing in-memory techniques.
name: Suspicious Usage of .NET Reflection via PowerShell
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type in ('Command Script', 'Process Creation') and src.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe', 'powershell_ise.exe') and ((cmdScript.content contains 'Reflection.Assembly]::Load' and cmdScript.content contains 'DownloadString' and cmdScript.content contains 'hidden') or (src.process.cmdline contains 'Reflection.Assembly]::Load' and src.process.cmdline contains 'DownloadString' and src.process.cmdline contains 'hidden'))

description: Detects suspicious use of Windows utilities commonly abused for dumping LSASS memory or the Active Directory database (NTDS.dit), indicating potential attempts to access credentials on Windows systems. This stolen credential data can then be used for lateral movement within the network, privilege escalation, and data exfiltration, potentially leading to complete domain compromise. By dumping LSASS memory, adversaries can obtain credentials currently in use. Dumping the NTDS.dit file provides offline access to all domain user credentials.
name: Suspicious Usage of Windows Credential Dumping Utilities

description: Detects the creation of a Volume Shadow Copy for the system drive using vssadmin.exe or Volume Shadow Copy Service-related processes. This behavior is commonly abused by adversaries to access locked system files (such as NTDS.dit or registry hives) for credential theft, offline analysis, or data exfiltration. The rule excludes known legitimate backup and system processes to reduce false positives, highlighting potentially malicious attempts to snapshot the system drive.
name: Suspicious VSS Shadow Copy Creation
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('vssadmin.exe') or tgt.process.displayName contains 'Volume Shadow Copy Service') and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'create' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'shadow' and tgt.process.cmdline matches '(?i)(?:\\/|\\b)for\\s*(?:=|:|\\s)\\s*c:' and not(src.process.name in:anycase ('svchost.exe', 'taskeng.exe', 'iperius.exe', 'wbengine.exe'))

description: Detects suspicious attempts to install or modify Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC) and Code Integrity (CI) policies. Adversaries may tamper with these policies to disable security controls or execute unsigned code. This rule monitors file creation and modification events related to policy files in CodeIntegrity.
name: Suspicious WDAC or CI Policy Modification

description: Detects wscript.exe command-line arguments commonly associated with the Qakbot malware campaign. Adversaries can abuse wscript.exe, a legitimate Windows utility for executing JScript files, to execute malicious scripts as part of Qakbot infections. Once executed, it can enable persistence, credential theft, and lateral movement within the network. Malicious actors leverage wscript.exe to bypass defenses by masquerading as legitimate activity, making it an effective method for deploying payloads, downloading additional modules, or establishing communication with command-and-control (C2) servers.
name: Suspicious Wscript Command Arguments

description: Detects the execution of the Windows Update Standalone Installer (wusa.exe) utilizing the /uninstall and /quiet command-line flags. Adversaries and malicious scripts utilize this technique to silently roll back applied security updates without alerting the user, thereby impairing system defenses and reintroducing previously patched vulnerabilities for subsequent exploitation. Monitoring this activity is critical because identifying unauthorized patch rollbacks allows defenders to catch defense evasion attempts early and prevent attackers from deliberately downgrading a system's security posture to leverage known, legacy exploits.
name: Suspicious WUSA Windows Update Rollback
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (((src.process.name in:anycase ('cmd.exe', 'powershell.exe', 'pwsh.exe') or src.process.displayName in ('Windows Command Processor', 'Windows PowerShell')) and not (src.process.cmdline matches '\\\\Windows\\\\(IMECache\\\\HealthScripts|netlogon)')) or not (src.process.image.path matches '\\\\Windows\\\\(System32|SysWOW64|CCM)'))and (tgt.process.cmdline contains ('wusa.exe') and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'quiet' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'uninstall') and  src.process.integrityLevel != 'SYSTEM'

description: Detects execution of the legitimate Symantec SSHelper Register Tool from suspicious or non-standard locations outside of typical Symantec installation directories. This tool can be abused by attackers for defense evasion and persistence mechanisms. This execution from an atypical location is often a strong indicator of this malicious use of the legitimate Symantec tool.
name: Symantec SSHelper Execution from Non-Standard Location
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.displayName in:anycase ('Symantec SSHelper Register Tool') and tgt.process.publisher in:anycase ('SYMANTEC CORPORATION') and not (tgt.process.image.path contains ('\\ProgramData\\Symantec', '\\Program Files (x86)\\Symantec', '\\Program Files\\Symantec'))

description: Detects potential misuse of SyncAppvPublishingServer to run arbitrary PowerShell code. Typically used for managing and synchronizing App-V (Application Virtualization) deployments, adversaries may abuse this tool to execute PowerShell commands for malicious purposes, such as persistence, credential theft, or lateral movement within the network. The legitimate use of SyncAppvPublishingServer is limited to application virtualization environments, so any activity involving this tool outside of its normal context could indicate an attempt to execute unauthorized code, potentially bypassing security controls and monitoring systems. By leveraging this legitimate system utility, adversaries can evade detection and further their objectives while maintaining a low profile.
name: SyncAppvPublishingServer PowerShell Code Execution
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.parent.name contains 'explorer.exe' and src.process.name contains 'wscript.exe' and tgt.process.name contains 'powershell.exe' and src.process.cmdline matches '(?:SyncAppvPublishingServer\\.vbs)(?:.+)(?:;)(?:.+)') or ((tgt.process.name contains 'SyncAppvPublishingServer.exe' or tgt.process.displayName = 'Microsoft Application Virtualization Sync Utility' or tgt.process.cmdline contains 'SyncAppvPublishingServer.vbs') and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'n;')

description: Detects attempts to unload the Sysmon filter driver, which terminates Sysmon's data collection and disrupts security monitoring. Adversaries may exploit this action to evade logging and detection, obscuring their activities and enabling further malicious actions, such as privilege escalation or lateral movement, without being monitored.
name: Sysmon Filter Driver Unload Attempt
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' AND endpoint.os = 'windows' AND event.type = 'Process Creation' AND tgt.process.name in:anycase ('fltmc.exe') AND tgt.process.cmdline contains 'unload' AND tgt.process.cmdline contains 'SysmonDrv'

description: Detects reconnaissance and discovery commands executed from a Node.js process chain including user enumeration, group queries, and Kerberos ticket manipulation. Legitimate Node.js applications do not typically spawn system discovery utilities. Adversaries may abuse Node.js as a runtime to execute malicious scripts for post-exploitation reconnaissance and credential access.
name: System and User Discovery via Node.js
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.parent.name in:anycase ('node.exe') or src.process.parent.displayName = 'Node.js JavaScript Runtime') and src.process.integrityLevel != 'SYSTEM' and ((tgt.process.name in:anycase ('klist.exe') and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('clear', 'purge')) or (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('net.exe') and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('user', 'group', 'localgroup') and not (src.process.cmdline contains ('Lionguard', 'PoleDigital', 'Kofax', 'VersaApp', 'Saaslio'))) or (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('nltest.exe') and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('dsget')) or (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('whoami.exe') and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('/group', '/all', '/priv', '/fo')) or (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('quser.exe', 'nslookup.exe')))

description: Detects the execution of the csrutil utility on macOS to disable System Integrity Protection (SIP). SIP is a macOS security feature that prevents the modification of system-critical files and settings. Disabling SIP can indicate an attempt to weaken macOS defenses, potentially leading to privilege escalation or persistence by attackers. Monitoring for csrutil usage can help identify unauthorized system modification attempts.
name: System Integrity Protection Disabled via Csrutil
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'osx' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.image.path matches 'bin/csrutil$' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'disable'

description: Detects the use of the unlink utility to delete system logs. Adversaries can abuse this tool to remove critical log files in an attempt to erase evidence of unauthorized activities, such as privilege escalation, lateral movement, or execution of malicious commands. By deleting logs, attackers can conceal their tracks, making it more difficult for defenders to trace their actions or identify the source of a compromise. This tactic is often used to hinder forensic analysis, disrupt incident response efforts, and ensure malicious activities remain undetected, allowing attackers to persist in the environment without raising suspicion.
name: System Log Deletion via Unlink Utility
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type in ('File Modification', 'File Deletion') and src.process.name = 'unlink' and tgt.file.path contains '/var/log/'

subQueries.subQuery: event.type in ('File Modification', 'File Creation') and tgt.file.path contains ('/lib/systemd/system/', '/etc/systemd/system/', '.config/systemd/user') and tgt.file.extension = 'service'

(tgt.process.name in ('systemctl', 'service', 'initctl') and tgt.process.cmdline contains (' restart',' enable', ' start', ' daemon-reload')) or (tgt.process.name = 'update-rc.d' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ' defaults') or (tgt.process.name = 'chkconfig' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ' on')

description: This rule identifies potential persistence activity involving the creation or modification of systemd service definitions followed by the use of systemctl commands to enable, start, or reload services. This sequence may indicate an attacker attempting to register and activate a new or altered service to achieve execution at boot or login. The correlation of these two behaviors increases confidence in detecting malicious service installation.
name: System Service Deployed

description: Detects system shutdown, reboot, or power-off events initiated on Linux endpoints. This includes both direct execution of system control utilities and indirect methods that modify system states. Monitoring these activities is essential for identifying unauthorized or unexpected system restarts, which may indicate an attempt to disrupt operations, erase forensic evidence, or perform unauthorized maintenance. Exclusions are applied for benign administrative status checks to reduce false positives.
name: System Shutdown or Reboot Commands
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (((tgt.process.name in ('shutdown', 'reboot', 'halt', 'poweroff') or tgt.process.displayName in ('shutdown', 'reboot', 'halt', 'poweroff')) and not (tgt.process.cmdline contains ' status')) or (tgt.process.cmdline matches '^(init|telinit)\\s(0|6)'))

description: Detects DNS requests originating from Node.js processes executing out of user-writable profile directories commonly associated with 'TamperedChef'. This behavior is security-relevant because adversaries often stage portable runtimes in non-standard locations to bypass application controls and perform command-and-control resolution or domain reconnaissance. In benign cases, developer tooling or bundled application runtimes may generate similar activity, but DNS activity from these locations typically warrants triage for potential malware staging.
name: TamperedChef Node.js DNS Requests from User AppData Directory
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'DNS Resolved' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('node.exe') or src.process.displayName = 'Node.js: Server-side JavaScript') and src.process.cmdline contains 'appdata' and src.process.cmdline matches '[a-z0-9]{8}-[a-z0-9]{4}-[a-z0-9]{4}-[a-z0-9]{4}-([a-z0-9]{12}|[a-z0-9]{14})\\.js(?!\\w)' and event.dns.request matches '[a-zA-Z0-9]{16,}\\.'

description: Detects creation of a registry startup value associated with 'TamperedChef' to establish auto-start persistence. This behavior enables code to run automatically at logon or boot, supporting long-term footholds and reducing visibility to endpoint controls. In legitimate environments, some software may set similar startup entries, but entries linked to 'TamperedChef' are atypical and merit investigation.
name: TamperedChef Registry-Based Startup Persistence
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Registry Value Create' and (registry.keyPath matches 'Software\\\\Microsoft\\\\Windows\\\\CurrentVersion\\\\(Run|RunOnce)\\\\sys_compatibility_check' or registry.keyPath contains '\\ConvertMateTask\\')

description: Detects file write activity to a directory masquerading as the legitimate Windows system folder, 'System32'. This behavior is associated with the Tangerine Turkey VBScript worm, which spreads via USB drives and employs DLL hijacking techniques to deploy cryptomining payloads. The malware utilizes printui.exe to facilitate this process, creating a rogue directory with an extra trailing space in its name to evade detection. Monitoring such activity is crucial for identifying potential threats and preventing unauthorized modifications to system files.
name: Tangerine Turkey File Write Activity

description: Detects the execution of commands that write files to a directory masquerading as the legitimate Windows system folder, 'System32'. This behavior is associated with the Tangerine Turkey VBScript worm, which spreads via USB drives and employs DLL hijacking techniques to deploy cryptomining payloads. The malware utilizes printui.exe to facilitate this process, creating a rogue directory with an extra trailing space in its name to evade detection. Monitoring such activity is crucial for identifying potential threats and preventing unauthorized modifications to system files.
name: Tangerine Turkey File Write Commands

description: Detects the execution of a command that adds an exclusion path to Microsoft Defender using PowerShell. The command typically includes 'Add-MpPreference -ExclusionPath' and targets a directory masquerading as the legitimate Windows system folder, 'System32'. This behavior is associated with the Tangerine Turkey VBScript worm, which spreads via USB drives and employs DLL hijacking techniques to deploy cryptomining payloads. Monitoring such activity is crucial for identifying potential threats and preventing unauthorized modifications to security settings.
name: Tangerine Turkey Microsoft Defender Exclusion Path via Powershell

description: Detects the registration of a scheduled task using a PowerShell script file with suspicious characteristics, such as uncommon file paths, obfuscated content, or known malicious patterns. While task registration via PowerShell can be legitimate, adversaries often leverage this technique to establish persistence or automate malicious activities. Monitoring this behavior helps identify potential abuse of task scheduling for unauthorized or harmful purposes.
name: Task Registered via Suspicious PowerShell Script File
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Task Register' and src.process.name = 'powershell.exe' and src.process.activeContent.path matches '.*\\.(log|txt|dat|csv|inf|cfg|ini|json|xml)$' and src.process.activeContent.signedStatus = 'unsigned' and not(src.process.activeContent.path contains 'ZscalerFix.xml')

description: Detects attempts to bypass Transparency, Consent, and Control (TCC) on macOS by accessing a mounted APFS snapshot. TCC enforces app restrictions on accessing sensitive user data, and mounting an APFS snapshot can enable unauthorized access to restricted files without user consent. Attackers may leverage this technique to extract private data, modify security settings, or escalate privileges while avoiding detection. Monitoring access to mounted APFS snapshots helps detect potential TCC bypass attempts and unauthorized system modifications.
name: TCC Bypass via Mounted APFS Snapshot Access
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'osx' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.name = 'mount_apfs' and (tgt.process.cmdline contains '/System/Volumes/Data' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'noowners')

description: Detects the execution of Kaspersky's signed TDSSKiller utility, which is used to delete Windows services. While TDSSKiller is a legitimate anti-rootkit tool, adversaries have been observed abusing it to disable security or monitoring services as part of ransomware and post-compromise activity. Monitoring this behavior can help identify unauthorized service removal, defense evasion, or malicious cleanup actions on compromised hosts.
name: TDSSKiller Service Deletion
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' AND src.process.cmdline contains '-dcsvc' AND src.process.publisher = 'KASPERSKY LAB' AND src.process.signedStatus = 'signed'

description: Detects potential exploitation of CVE-2026-24061, a critical authentication bypass vulnerability in GNU InetUtils telnetd (versions 1.9.3 through 2.7). The vulnerability allows unauthenticated remote attackers to inject the -f flag into the login command by setting a malicious USER environment variable through the Telnet protocol's NEW_ENVIRON option. The -f flag instructs login to skip authentication entirely and grant immediate shell access as the specified user, resulting in instant unauthorized access without credentials. This technique has been actively exploited in the wild and was added to CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog. Monitoring for telnetd spawning login with the -f flag is critical to detect exploitation attempts targeting legacy systems, embedded devices, and OT infrastructure still running Telnet services.
name: Telnetd Authentication Bypass via Login Flag Injection (CVE-2026-24061)
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.name in:anycase ('login') and tgt.process.cmdline matches ('\\s+-f\\s+\\w+') and src.process.name in:anycase ('telnetd', 'in.telnetd', 'inetutils-telnetd')

description: Detects activity that matches known threat indicators, including malicious IPs, domains, or file hashes. These matches signify potential security threats, enabling timely identification and response to mitigate risks and safeguard systems from malicious activity. A threat intelligence integration must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: Threat Intelligence Indicator Match
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'Threat Intelligence Indicators' and event.externalThreatValue > 80

description: Detects modifications to the TokenLeakDetectDelaySecs registry value, which could signal attempts to tamper with token leak detection settings. Adversaries may exploit this by altering the detection delay to evade security mechanisms designed to identify and respond to leaked or misused authentication tokens. By extending or disabling token leak detection, attackers can increase their dwell time within a compromised environment, allowing for prolonged lateral movement, privilege escalation, or persistence without triggering automated defenses. This tactic may be part of a broader strategy to avoid detection while conducting malicious activities such as data exfiltration or unauthorized resource access.
name: Token Leak Manipulation
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' AND event.type = 'Registry Value Modified' AND registry.keyPath contains 'TokenLeakDetectDelaySecs'

description: Detects Tor hidden service (.onion) URLs being referenced within process command-line arguments. By leveraging .onion domains, adversaries can successfully evade standard network defenses such as DNS monitoring, domain reputation filters, and IP based blocking because these addresses do not resolve through the public Domain Name System, making tracking and attribution significantly more difficult.
name: Tor Hidden Service URL Referenced in Command Line
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'IP Connect' and src.process.cmdline matches '(?i:\\b[a-z2-7]{16}(?:[a-z2-7]{40})?\\.onion\\b)'

description: Detects the execution of the Tor utility on macOS endpoints, which is used to route network traffic through the Tor anonymity network. While Tor is often used for privacy, attackers may leverage it to anonymize their activities, evade detection, or establish command and control channels. Monitoring Tor utility execution can help identify potential evasion or malicious communication attempts.
name: Tor Utility Execution
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'osx' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.image.path matches "bin/tor$"

description: Detects activity associated with indicators of compromise linked to the Trivy supply chain attack. Adversaries compromised the Trivy vulnerability scanner distribution to deliver malicious payloads, leveraging trust in the legitimate software supply chain to gain initial access to target environments.
name: Trivy Supply Chain Compromise
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and ((event.type = 'Process Creation' and src.process.cmdline contains 'trivy-action' and src.process.cmdline contains '.sh' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'curl' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'POST' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'scan.aquasecurtiy.org') or (event.type = 'File Creation' and tgt.file.path contains 'trivy-action' and tgt.file.path contains 'entrypoint.sh' and tgt.file.sha1 contains '4fed54d88f919c675ee2f575f70698a8d3649287'))

description: Detects potential User Account Control (UAC) bypass attempts leveraging the ICMLuaUtil COM interface via the COM Surrogate process. Attackers commonly abuse this technique to escalate privileges without triggering a UAC prompt. This activity is suspicious and warrants investigation for privilege escalation or persistence techniques.
name: UAC Bypass via ICMLuaUtil COM Surrogate
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.parent.name in:anycase ('dllhost.exe') or src.process.parent.displayName = 'COM Surrogate') and src.process.parent.cmdline contains ('Processid:{3E5FC7F9-9A51-4367-9063-A120244FBEC7}', 'Processid:{D2E7041B-2927-42FB-8E9F-7CE93B6DC937}') and src.process.image.originalFileName != 'WerFault.exe'

description: Detects the registry modification that disables User Account Control remote restrictions by setting the LocalAccountTokenFilterPolicy value to 1. An adversary can abuse this to bypass UAC protections during remote access, enabling privilege escalation and unauthorized system control.
name: UAC Remote Restrictions Disabled
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' AND endpoint.os = 'windows' AND event.type in ('Registry Key Create', 'Registry Value Modified', 'Registry Value Create') AND registry.keyPath matches '\\\\SOFTWARE\\\\Microsoft\\\\Windows\\\\CurrentVersion\\\\Policies\\\\System\\\\LocalAccountTokenFilterPolicy$' and registry.value in ('1', '0x00000001') and (src.process.name in:anycase ('reg.exe', 'cmd.exe', 'powershell.exe', 'pwsh.exe') or src.process.displayName in ('Registry Console Tool', 'Windows Command Processor', 'Windows PowerShell', 'pwsh')) and src.process.integrityLevel != 'SYSTEM'

description: Detects processes attempting to access the macOS System Key file, a critical component of FileVault encryption that stores system encryption keys. Unauthorized access to this file may indicate attempts to bypass disk encryption, steal cryptographic keys, or escalate privileges. Adversaries targeting this file may seek to decrypt protected data or manipulate system security settings. Monitoring access attempts helps detect potential encryption bypass, credential theft, or unauthorized tampering with macOS security mechanisms.
name: Unauthorized Access Attempt to macOS System Key File

description: Detects the use of uncommon encoding techniques in PowerShell, such as XOR operations or Base64 encoding invoked through method indexing rather than standard function calls. While encoding and Base64 are legitimate techniques, their invocation in these unusual ways is often linked to obfuscation and defense evasion. Adversaries leverage such methods to conceal malicious payloads, bypass detections, or hinder analysis. Monitoring for these patterns is critical to identify obfuscated scripts, uncover hidden payload delivery, and disrupt evasion attempts.
name: Uncommon PowerShell Encoding Pattern
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type in ('Process Creation') and (tgt.process.cmdline contains (' -BXor ' , '.GetMethods()[306]') or cmdScript.content contains (' -BXor ' ,'.GetMethods()[306]')) and not (src.process.integrityLevel='SYSTEM')

description: This rule detects suspicious attempts to escape container environments by monitoring events related to the loading of unsigned kernel modules. Adversaries often attempt to exploit kernel vulnerabilities to escape the isolated container and gain control of the host system. Unsigned kernel modules are not digitally signed and can be loaded by any user, making them highly suspicious in a containerized environment. This rule helps identify potential security threats and prevent container compromise.
name: Unsigned Kernel Module Loaded in Container
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Behavioral Indicators' and indicator.name = 'UnsignedKernelModuleLoad' and k8sCluster.containerId = * and not (src.process.cmdline contains ('nvidia', 'dell-rbu', 'dell_rbu', 'sysdigcloud-probe'))

description: Detects the loading of an unsigned YDArk kernel driver, a tool commonly associated with rootkit-style capabilities and kernel-level manipulation. YDArk drivers are frequently abused by adversaries to gain elevated privileges, bypass security controls, hide malicious activity, or facilitate credential access. Monitoring for unsigned YDArk driver loads can help identify advanced persistence, privilege escalation, or defense evasion techniques targeting the Windows kernel.
name: Unsigned YDArk Driver Load Detected
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'Driver Load' and (tgt.file.internalName in:anycase ('YDArk', 'YDArkDrv') OR tgt.file.originalFileName in:anycase ('YDArkDrv.sys', 'YDArk.sys') OR tgt.file.name in:anycase ('ydarkdrv.sys', 'ydark.sys')) AND tgt.file.signedStatus != 'signed'

description: Detects anomalous ADRS token requests initiated by the Microsoft Authenticator Broker application. Such activity may indicate attempts to abuse authentication mechanisms or bypass standard access controls within the environment. Unusual token requests can be associated with credential theft, session hijacking, or lateral movement by adversaries. Monitoring for this behavior helps identify potential unauthorized access or misuse of authentication services.
name: Unusual ADRS Token Requests via Microsoft Authenticator Broker
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Azure Platform' and unmapped.properties.appId = '29d9ed98-a469-4536-ade2-f981bc1d605e' and unmapped.properties.resourceId = '01cb2876-7ebd-4aa4-9cc9-d28bd4d359a9' and metadata.log_name = 'NonInteractiveUserSignInLogs' and unmapped.properties.authenticationProcessingDetails contains 'adrs_access' and unmapped.properties.incomingTokenType = 'refreshToken' and actor.user.type = 'Member'

description: Detects instances where 'wermgr.exe', the Windows Error Reporting process, spawns an unexpected or atypical child process. Such behavior may indicate process injection, privilege escalation, or abuse of legitimate system processes by adversaries to evade detection. Threat actors often leverage trusted system binaries like 'wermgr.exe' to execute malicious payloads or establish persistence. This rule highlights deviations from normal process hierarchies that could signify malicious activity.
name: Unusual Child Process Spawned by Windows Error Reporting Manager
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and src.process.parent.name in ('wermgr.exe') and (src.process.name in:anycase ('cmd.exe', 'cscript.exe', 'ipconfig.exe', 'mshta.exe', 'net.exe', 'net1.exe', 'netstat.exe', 'nslookup.exe', 'powershell_ise.exe', 'powershell.exe', 'pwsh.exe','regsvr32.exe', 'rundll32.exe', 'systeminfo.exe', 'whoami.exe', 'wscript.exe') or src.process.displayName in ('Windows Command Prompt','Microsoft ® Console Based Script Host', 'Windows PowerShell', 'Registry Console Tool', 'Microsoft (R) HTML Application host', 'Windows host process (Rundll32)', 'Microsoft ® Windows Based Script Host')) and not (src.process.parent.cmdline contains ('-upload', '-outproc')) and not ((src.process.name in:anycase ('rundll32.exe')) and not (src.process.cmdline contains '\\Windows\\system32\\WerConCpl.dll') and not (src.process.cmdline contains 'LaunchErcApp ') and not (src.process.cmdline contains ('-queuereporting', '-responsepester')))

description: Detects the creation of unusual dump files on Windows systems. Adversaries can abuse legitimate Windows functionality to create memory dumps of processes, including those containing sensitive information like credentials, encryption keys, and proprietary data. These dumps can be created using tools like procdump.exe, Task Manager, or even built-in Windows error reporting. By capturing these dumps, adversaries can then exfiltrate and analyze them offline to gain unauthorized access to confidential data. This technique can be used in various attack scenarios, including credential theft, privilege escalation, and data exfiltration.
name: Unusual Memory Dump File Creation
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'File Creation' and tgt.file.extension in:anycase ('dmp', 'mdmp') and not (src.process.name in:anycase ('SqlDumper.exe', 'WerFault.exe', 'wermgr.exe', 'cbengine.exe', 'UserProfileManager.exe', '7zG.exe', 'syncappw.exe', 'Engine.exe', 'crashpad_handler.exe', 'FXServer.exe')) and tgt.file.path contains ('lsass', 'dumpert', 'andrew.dmp', 'sqldmpr', 'coredump', 'livedump') and not (tgt.file.path contains ('BarTenderLicensing', 'MSSQLLocalDB')) and not (src.process.parent.name contains 'sqlservr.exe')

description: Detects suspicious or atypical device registration events within Microsoft Entra ID cloud environments. Such activity may indicate attempts to register unauthorized devices, potentially enabling adversaries to gain persistent access or bypass security controls. Unusual registration patterns can be associated with credential misuse, lateral movement, or initial access techniques. Monitoring these events helps reduce the risk of unauthorized device onboarding and potential compromise of cloud resources.
name: Unusual Microsoft Entra ID Cloud Device Registration Activity

description: Detects Microsoft OAuth authorization flows where the Auth Broker redirects to the Device Registration Service (DRS), which is atypical for standard authentication processes. Such activity may indicate attempts to abuse OAuth mechanisms for unauthorized device registration or privilege escalation. This behavior can be leveraged by adversaries to gain persistent access or register rogue devices within an organization's environment. Monitoring for these patterns helps identify potential misuse of OAuth flows that could lead to security breaches.
name: Unusual Microsoft OAuth Authorization via Auth Broker Redirect to Device Registration Service
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Azure Platform' and api.operation = 'Sign-in activity' and unmapped.resultType = '0' and unmapped.properties.appId = '29d9ed98-a469-4536-ade2-f981bc1d605e' and unmapped.properties.resourceId = '01cb2876-7ebd-4aa4-9cc9-d28bd4d359a9' and actor.user.type = 'Member' and unmapped.properties.userPrincipalName = * and unmapped.properties.sessionId = * and api.service.name contains 'Microsoft Authentication Broker' and not (unmapped.properties.riskState contains ('confirmedSafe', 'remediated', 'dismissed')) and (unmapped.properties.riskEventTypes_v2 contains ('unlikelyTravel', 'anonymizedIPAddress', 'maliciousIPAddress', 'malwareInfectedIPAddress', 'suspiciousIPAddress', 'leakedCredentials', 'investigationsThreatIntelligence') or unmapped.properties.riskLevelDuringSignIn contains ('medium', 'high') or unmapped.properties.riskLevelAggregated contains ('medium', 'high'))

description: Detects the loading of the 'Veeam.Backup.Common.dll' module by processes that are atypical or potentially unauthorized, such as PowerShell-related processes or those with untrusted or missing code signatures. This behavior may indicate an attempt to misuse Veeam backup components for malicious purposes, such as data exfiltration or system compromise. Monitoring this activity is crucial as it can reveal attempts by adversaries to exploit backup systems, which are critical for data integrity and recovery. Identifying such anomalies helps in mitigating risks associated with unauthorized access and potential data breaches.
name: Unusual Process Loading Veeam Backup DLL
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe', 'pwsh.exe', 'powershell_ise.exe') or src.process.displayName in ('Windows PowerShell') or src.process.signedStatus = 'unsigned') and (tgt.file.name = 'Veeam.Backup.Common.dll' or tgt.file.originalFileName = 'Veeam.Backup.Common.dll')

description: Detects instances where rundll32.exe initiates network connections without accompanying command-line arguments and spawns a child process. By invoking rundll32.exe without command-line arguments, attackers can evade traditional security mechanisms, making it harder to track the purpose of the executed DLL. Furthermore, spawning a child process may allow malicious code to further propagate within the network or establish persistence. This technique can be used to disguise the true intent of the malicious activity, facilitating command-and-control communication or payload execution without raising immediate alarms.
name: Unusual RunDLL32 Child Processes
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.category in ('dns','url','ip') and (src.process.parent.name contains 'rundll32.exe' or src.process.parent.displayName = 'Windows host process (Rundll32)') and src.process.parent.cmdline matches '^rundll32\\.exe$' and not (src.process.name in:anycase ('WerFault.exe'))

description: Detects instances where a non-system user uses the Service Control Manager to add a user to the local administrators group. This activity may indicate an attempt to escalate privileges, enabling unauthorized access to sensitive resources, modification of system configurations, or execution of malicious code with elevated privileges.
name: User Addition to Administrator Group via Service Control Manager
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('sc.exe') or src.process.displayName in:anycase ('Service Control Manager Configuration Tool')) and not (src.process.user contains ('SYSTEM', 'AUTORITE NT', 'SISTEMA')) and src.process.cmdline contains 'create' and src.process.cmdline contains 'group' and src.process.cmdline contains 'admin'

description: Detects the use of WMIC (Windows Management Instrumentation Command-line) from command-line tools to list all user accounts on a system. Adversaries may abuse this command to enumerate local and domain user accounts as part of their reconnaissance efforts. By gathering account names, attackers can identify potential targets for privilege escalation, lateral movement, or credential dumping.
name: User Enumeration via WMIC
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('wmic.exe') or tgt.process.displayName = 'WMI Commandline Utility') and src.process.name in:anycase ('cmd.exe', 'powershell.exe', 'pwsh.exe', 'powershell_ise.exe', 'wt.exe') and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'wmic' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'useraccount' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'get' and tgt.process.cmdline contains '/all'

description: Detects the creation or modification of the UserInitMprLogonScript registry value, a technique used to achieve persistence by abusing the Windows logon process. This value allows a standard user to execute code automatically upon login without requiring administrative privileges or triggering UAC. During session initialization, userinit.exe executes the path stored in this variable before the desktop shell loads. Analysts should investigate the registry-writing process and the target script path, as legitimate use of this environment variable is extremely rare in modern environments.
name: UserInitMprLogonScript Registry Modification
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Registry Value Create' and registry.keyPath matches 'UserInitMprLogonScript' and src.process.integrityLevel != 'SYSTEM'

description: This rule detects system-level actions related to user and group account management on Linux endpoints. Such operations may include the creation of new user accounts, modification of existing accounts or their credentials, and adjustments to group memberships. While these activities are common in administrative workflows, they can also indicate malicious behavior when performed unexpectedly or by unauthorized users. Adversaries may leverage these actions to establish persistence, escalate privileges, or maintain covert access within the environment.
name: User Management Event
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.name in ('useradd', 'adduser', 'usermod', 'passwd', 'chpasswd', 'groupadd', 'groupmod', 'gpasswd', 'deluser', 'userdel') and not (tgt.process.cmdline contains (' -h', ' --help')) and not (tgt.process.name = 'passwd' and tgt.process.cmdline contains (' -S ', ' --status')) and not (tgt.process.cmdline matches 'useradd -D(\\s|$)')

description: Detects process execution events where the Windows Script Host uses the engine override switch to execute files with abnormal or missing script extensions. This behavior is security-relevant because adversaries frequently disguise malicious scripts as benign file types to evade file-based preventative controls and extension filters. Such activity typically supports initial access, payload delivery, or follow-on execution within a compromised host. Abusing native script interpreter workflows to force the execution of hidden code is a common technique for defense evasion.
name: VBScript Execution Using Non-Standard File Extensions
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('wscript.exe') or tgt.process.displayName contains 'Windows Based Script Host') and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('e:vbscript') and not (tgt.process.cmdline contains ('capainstaller', 'thumb.db')) and not (tgt.process.cmdline matches '.*\\.(vbs|vbe|wsf|mdb)"?(\\s+.*)?$')

description: Detects the execution of mshta.exe in combination with the VBScript protocol handler to interpret code as VBScript. Adversaries can abuse this functionality to execute malicious VBScript payloads, often delivered via phishing emails, malicious websites, or crafted files. By leveraging mshta.exe, attackers bypass traditional defenses, as it is a legitimate Windows utility capable of executing scripts embedded in HTML or other files. This technique enables the execution of arbitrary code, often used for downloading additional payloads, establishing persistence, or conducting reconnaissance on the compromised system. The abuse of mshta.exe is a common tactic in initial access or execution phases of an attack, providing a stealthy mechanism for adversaries to achieve their objectives while evading detection.
name: VBScript Execution via Mshta
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('mshta.exe') or tgt.process.displayName = 'Microsoft (R) HTML Application host') and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'vbscript:' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('GetObject', 'CreateObject')

description: Detects the execution of rundll32.exe using the VBScript protocol handler to interpret and execute code as VBScript. Adversaries can abuse this technique to execute malicious VBScript payloads, enabling them to run arbitrary code under the context of the current user or process. This method is often used to bypass application whitelisting or security controls that may not block legitimate system utilities like rundll32.exe. By leveraging the VBScript protocol handler, attackers can load and execute code from local or remote locations, potentially establishing persistence, performing lateral movement, or executing additional malicious activities. This technique is particularly effective in evading detection due to its reliance on a trusted Windows utility and scripting language.
name: VBScript Execution via RunDLL32
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('rundll32.exe') or tgt.process.displayName = 'Windows host process (Rundll32)') and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'vbscript:'

description: Detects the execution of wscript.exe in conjunction with the vbscript protocol handler to interpret code as VBScript. Adversaries can abuse this functionality to execute malicious VBScript payloads, often in the context of a spearphishing attack or exploiting a vulnerability in a system. By using VBScript, attackers can deliver various types of malware, ranging from remote access tools to ransomware, leveraging this scripting engine to execute arbitrary code on the victim’s system. Additionally, VBScript is commonly used in social engineering attacks where unsuspecting users are tricked into executing the script by opening a malicious email attachment or visiting a compromised website.
name: VBScript Execution via Wscript
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('wscript.exe') or tgt.process.displayName contains 'Windows Based Script Host') and tgt.process.cmdline contains '/e:vbscript' and not (tgt.process.cmdline contains ('PSAppDeployToolkit', 'ResetStandBy')) and not (src.process.name in:anycase ('gpscript.exe', 'CPAU.exe')) and tgt.process.cmdline matches ('\\.js$', '\\.vbs$')

description: Detects the execution of a VBScript file via wscript.exe that spawns an executable from a public user directory. An adversary can abuse this by placing malicious executables in this easily accessible directory and then using a VBScript to launch them, enabling initial access, persistence, or the deployment of further malware, potentially leading to system compromise or data exfiltration.
name: VBScript Spawning Executable in Public Directory
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('wscript.exe') OR src.process.displayName contains 'Windows Based Script Host') and tgt.process.cmdline matches '\\\\Users\\\\Public\\\\.*\\.exe' and src.process.cmdline contains '.vbs'

description: Detects suspicious execution of the VbsEdit Script Launcher invoked via msiexec.exe, a technique frequently associated with ClickFix campaigns. An adversary can abuse this method to bypass application allowlisting, AppLocker policies, and traditional static defenses by leveraging a trusted, native operating system binary to proxy the execution of malicious code.
name: VbsEdit Script Launcher Executed via Msiexec

description: Detects attempts to access or manipulate Veeam Backup credential data using SQL command-line tools or PowerShell commands on Windows systems. This activity is significant as it may indicate an adversary's effort to exfiltrate sensitive backup credentials, potentially leading to unauthorized data access or system compromise. The rule focuses on identifying suspicious interactions with the VeeamBackup database, which could be part of a broader attack strategy to infiltrate backup systems and exploit stored data.
name: VeeamBackup Database Credential Access via SQL or PowerShell
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type in ('Process Creation', 'Command Script') and (src.process.name in:anycase ('sqlcmd.exe', 'psql.exe') or src.process.displayName in:anycase ('Microsoft® SQL Server®', 'PostgreSQL') or src.process.cmdline contains ('Invoke-Sqlcmd', 'Invoke-SqlExecute', 'Invoke-DbaQuery', 'Invoke-SqlQuery') or cmdScript.content contains ('Invoke-Sqlcmd', 'Invoke-SqlExecute', 'Invoke-DbaQuery', 'Invoke-SqlQuery')) and ((src.process.cmdline contains '[VeeamBackup].[dbo].[Credentials]' or cmdScript.content contains '[VeeamBackup].[dbo].[Credentials]') or ((src.process.cmdline contains '[Credentials]' or cmdScript.content contains '[Credentials]') and (src.process.cmdline contains '[Veeam.Backup.Common.ProtectedStorage]::GetLocalString' or cmdScript.content contains '[Veeam.Backup.Common.ProtectedStorage]::GetLocalString') and (src.process.cmdline contains 'Veeam Backup and Replication' or cmdScript.content contains 'Veeam Backup and Replication')) or ((src.process.cmdline contains 'postgres' or cmdScript.content contains 'postgres') and (src.process.cmdline contains 'veeambackup' or cmdScript.content contains 'veeambackup') and (src.process.cmdline contains 'user_name' or cmdScript.content contains 'user_name') and (src.process.cmdline contains 'credentials' or cmdScript.content contains 'credentials') and (src.process.cmdline contains 'password' or cmdScript.content contains 'password')))

subQueries.subQuery: indicator.name in ('AddVehHandler', 'KernelExploitAttempt')
indicator.name in ('MultipleHookRemovals', 'HookRemovalAttempt')
indicator.name = 'BreakpointHookBypassWithoutDebugger'

description: Detects either the registration of a vectored exception handler (VEH) or behavioral indicators associated with kernel exploit attempts. Adversaries may abuse VEH to hijack exception handling for code injection, debugger evasion, or covert payload execution. Similarly, kernel exploits may manipulate low-level system functionality to escalate privileges or bypass user-mode defenses. This rule also considers the removal of existing hooks and anti-debugging behavior, which often accompanies these techniques. Detection of these patterns can indicate advanced evasion methods, including process hollowing, reflective injection, kernel-level privilege escalation, or execution of unsigned drivers. Please enable the 'HookRemovalAttempt', 'BreakpointHookBypassWithoutDebugger' and 'MultipleHookRemovals' Behavioral indicators to make this rule work.
name: VEH Abuse or Kernel Exploit Attempt with Hook Removal and Debugger Evasion

subQueries.subQuery: indicator.name = 'FilelessProcessExecution' and not (src.process.parent.name in ('buildah'))
indicator.name in ('UnsignedKernelModuleLoad', 'NewKernelModule', 'AnomalousUnsignedKernelModuleLoad', 'SystemCallHook', 'eBPFProgramLoad', 'SuspiciousEbpfProgramLoad', 'EbpfProgramAttached')

description: Detects the multi-stage infection chain of the VoidLink malware framework, a Chinese-developed Linux threat targeting cloud environments. The framework is notably the first documented Chinese-language malware written in the Zig programming language. This detection covers the progression from initial fileless 'dropper' execution to deep kernel-level persistence via Loadable Kernel Modules (LKM) and eBPF programs.
name: VoidLink Malware Activity

description: Detects the initiation of a backup operation targeting the NTDS (Active Directory database) using WBAdmin. Unauthorized or unexpected NTDS backups could indicate attempts to exfiltrate sensitive directory data or prepare for lateral movement. Monitoring this activity helps protect critical domain controller assets and detect potential privilege abuse or data theft.
name: WBAdmin NTDS Backup Initiation
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('wbadmin.exe') or tgt.process.displayName = 'Command Line Interface for Microsoft® BLB Backup') and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('backup', 'recovery') and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'ntds' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'start'

description: Detects instances where encoded PowerShell commands are used to create connections to URLs via Behavioral Indicators. This technique is commonly employed by adversaries to obfuscate malicious payloads, evade detection mechanisms, and execute code directly in memory, thereby bypassing traditional file-based defenses. Such behavior is indicative of initial access attempts, malware staging, or post-exploitation activities. Please enable the 'WebConnectionFromEncodedPowershellCommand' Behavioral Indicator to make this rule work.
name: Web Connection From Encoded PowerShell Behavior
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Behavioral Indicator' and indicator.name = 'WebConnectionFromEncodedPowershellCommand' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe') or src.process.displayName = 'Windows PowerShell') and url.address contains ('.bat', '.cmd', '.dll', '.exe', '.hta', '.js', '.jse', '.pif', '.ps1', '.rar', '.scr', '.vbe', '.vbs', '.wsh', '.zip')

description: Detects the creation or modification of a suspicious aspx file, within the critical SharePoint LAYOUTS directory. This activity is a definitive indicator of a web shell being planted on the server. The LAYOUTS directory is a common target for attackers because files placed there can be executed by the web server. The file spinstall0.aspx is a known indicator of compromise associated with post-exploitation activity following a successful SharePoint vulnerability exploit.
name: Web Shell File Detected in LAYOUTS Directory
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type in ('File Creation','File Rename','File Modification') and tgt.file.path contains 'microsoft shared\\Web Server Extensions\\' and tgt.file.path contains '\\TEMPLATE\\LAYOUTS' and (tgt.file.path contains 'spinstall0.aspx' or (tgt.file.path contains '.aspx' and (src.process.parent.name in:anycase ('cmd.exe') or src.process.parent.displayName in:anycase ('Windows Command Processor') or src.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe') or src.process.displayName in:anycase ('Windows PowerShell'))))

description: Detects successful account logons performed using Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI). Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Account Login via WMI
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.id = '4624' AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.processName matches '\\\\wmiprvse\\.exe$'

description: Detects the creation of security-enabled global administrative groups (4727) in Active Directory. This may indicate unauthorized modifications to privileged group memberships, posing a security risk. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Active Directory Global Admin Group Created
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.channel = 'Security' AND winEventLog.id = 4727 AND ((winEventLog.data.event.eventData.targetUserName matches '^Admin') OR (winEventLog.data.event.eventData.targetSid = 'S-1-5-32-544'))

description: Detects changes to security-enabled global groups (4737) in Active Directory, focusing on administrative groups. This can indicate unauthorized modifications to privileged group memberships, potentially compromising system security. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Active Directory Global Admin Group Modified
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.channel = 'Security' AND winEventLog.id = 4737 AND ((winEventLog.data.event.eventData.targetUserName matches '^Admin') OR (winEventLog.data.event.eventData.targetSid = 'S-1-5-32-544'))

description: Detects an attempt being made to enable the always install with elevated privileges setting. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL AlwaysInstallElevated Policy Enabled
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.id = '4657' AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.objectName matches 'software\\\\policies\\\\microsoft\\\\windows\\\\installer$' AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.objectValueName = 'AlwaysInstallElevated' AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.newValue in ('0x1', '0x00000001', '1') AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.oldValue in ('0', '0x0', '0x00000000')

description: Detects changes to the Windows registry that disable the Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI) by setting the AmsiEnable value to 0. Threat actors commonly use this technique to impair security defenses and evade detection. Disabling AMSI allows malicious payloads to execute without interference, significantly increasing the risk of system compromise, privilege escalation, and data exfiltration.
name: WEL AMSI Disabled via Registry Change
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.id = '4657' AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.objectName matches '\\\\SOFTWARE\\\\Microsoft\\\\Windows Script\\\\Settings\\\\AmsiEnable$' AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.newValue in ('0x0', '0x00000000', '0')

description: Detects the use of wbadmin.exe to delete backup catalogs. Attackers may delete backup catalogs to prevent organizations from restoring systems to a pre-attack state, thus increasing the impact and damage of their campaigns. This tactic is often used in ransomware attacks or destructive campaigns to ensure that recovery options are limited or unavailable, forcing victims to face longer downtime or data loss. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Backup Catalog Deletion via Wbadmin
s1ql: dataSource.name = "Windows Event Logs" AND winEventLog.id = 4688 AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.newProcessName matches "\\\\wbadmin\\.exe$" AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.commandLine contains "catalog" AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.commandLine contains "delete"

description: Detects access attempts to the backup key secret object (BCKUPKEY) via Windows Event Log 4662, indicating potential unauthorized access or modification activities. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Backup Key Secret Access
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.channel = 'Security' AND winEventLog.id = 4662 AND winEventLog.description.objectType='SecretObject' AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.accessMask = '0x2' AND winEventLog.description.objectName contains 'BCKUPKEY'

description: Detects the use of PowerViews Add-DomainObjectAcl cmdlet to grant DCSync extended rights to a standard user or machine account in Active Directory. This action effectively backdoors a domain object, enabling the retrieval of password hashes for any user or computer account. Adversaries may exploit this technique to establish persistence, escalate privileges, or compromise the domain. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL DCSync Rights Granted via PowerView
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' and winEventLog.channel = 'Security' and winEventLog.id = 5136 and winEventLog.data.event.eventData.attributeLDAPDisplayName = 'ntSecurityDescriptor' and winEventLog.data.event.eventData.attributeValue contains ('1131f6aa-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2', '89e95b76-444d-4c62-991a-0facbeda640c', '1131f6ad-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2') and not (winEventLog.data.event.eventData.objectClass in:anycase ('dnsNode', 'dnsZone', 'dnsZoneScope'))

description: Detects attempts to set the Directory Services Restore Mode (DSRM) administrator password, indicating potential unauthorized changes to a critical recovery account. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL DSRM Password Change Attempt
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.channel = 'Security' AND winEventLog.id = 4794

description: Detects disabling of the Windows event logging auto start service which can be an indication of an adversary attempting to disable a critical service to avoid detection. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Event Logging Service Auto Start Disabled
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.id = '7040' AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.param1 contains ('event log', 'eventlog') AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.param2 = 'auto start' AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.param3 = 'disabled'

description: Detects the creation of a hidden scheduled task, a tactic often used by attackers to maintain persistence on a compromised system. Hidden tasks can evade detection by administrators and standard monitoring tools, allowing adversaries to execute malicious activities stealthily. This technique is commonly employed to run malicious scripts, download payloads, or maintain remote access without raising suspicion. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Hidden Scheduled Task Creation
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' and winEventLog.id = 4698 and winEventLog.data.event.eventData.taskContent contains '<Hidden>true</Hidden>' and not (winEventLog.data.event.eventData.subjectUserName matches '\\$$') and not (winEventLog.data.event.eventData.taskContent matches ('<Command>C:\\\\Program\\sFiles\\\\AMD\\\\.*</Command>', '<Command>.*\\\\Google\\\\GoogleUpdater\\\\.*</Command>', '<Command>.*\\\\Microsoft\\sVisual\\sStudio\\\\Installer\\\\.*</Command>', '<Command>C:\\\\Windows\\\\system32\\\\msfeedssync\\.exe</Command>', '<Command>C:\\\\Program\\sFiles(\\s\\(x86\\))?\\\\Google\\\\Chrome\\\\Application\\\\chrome\\.exe</Command>', '<Command>C:\\\\Program\\sFiles(\\s\\(x86\\))?\\\\Microsoft\\\\Edge\\\\Application\\\\msedge\\.exe</Command>'))

description: Detects the disabling of the Kerberos pre-authentication flag within a user account's UserAccountControl attribute, as recorded in Windows Security Event 4738. An adversary can abuse this by exploiting the AS-REP Roasting technique to retrieve Kerberos tickets that can be brute-forced offline, allowing them to compromise the user's account, escalate privileges, and potentially gain unauthorized access to sensitive data and critical systems. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Kerberos Pre-Authentication Disabled For User Account
s1ql: dataSource.name = "Windows Event Logs" and winEventLog.id = 4738 and winEventLog.description contains "'Don't Require Preauth' - Enabled"

description: Detects attempts to add a suspicious DNS entry to the DNS server, which may indicate attempts to conduct Kerberos relay attacks. The Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Kerberos Relay Account Added to DNS Server

description: Detects changes to the MSSQL xp_cmdshell stored procedure setting. Enabling or modifying this setting allows command execution at the operating system level via SQL Server, which could be exploited by adversaries for privilege escalation, lateral movement, or system compromise. Monitoring this activity helps identify potential security risks.
name: WEL MSSQL xp_cmdshell Option Modification
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' and winEventLog.channel = 'Application' and winEventLog.id = 15457 and winEventLog.description contains 'xp_cmdshell' and winEventLog.data.event.system.provider.name contains 'MSSQL'

description: Detects when a new external device, such as a USB drive, is recognized by the system, potentially indicating unauthorized device connections. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL New External Device Recognized
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.channel = 'Security' AND winEventLog.id = 6416 AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.className = 'DiskDrive'

description: Detects when a user account is modified to have a password that never expires, potentially increasing the risk of long-term account compromise. Adversaries often exploit such accounts to maintain persistent access without triggering password expiration policies, reducing the likelihood of detection. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Password Expiration Disabled For User Account
s1ql: dataSource.name = "Windows Event Logs" AND winEventLog.data.event.system.eventID = 4738 AND winEventLog.description contains "'Don't Expire Password' - Enabled"

description: Detects the creation of scheduled tasks that match characteristics typically associated with the Impacket tool, including specific commands found in the task content. Attackers often use tools like atexec.py from Impacket to create stealthy, system-scheduled tasks that execute payloads or commands on remote systems. These tasks allow adversaries to maintain persistence, execute arbitrary code, or spread laterally within a network. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Potential Impacket Scheduled Task Creation
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.id = 4698 AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.taskName matches "^\\\\[A-Za-z]{8}$" AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.taskContent contains '/c ' and winEventLog.data.event.eventData.taskContent matches '\\\\[A-Za-z]{8}\\.tmp'

description: Detects multiple access attempts to privileged Active Directory objects, potentially indicating an LDAP reconnaissance attempt. These objects are infrequently accessed by regular users or processes, and unauthorized access attempts may signal attacker enumeration efforts or lateral movement within the domain. Such activity poses risks to domain security by exposing sensitive information or enabling further compromise. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Potential LDAP Enumeration

description: Detects remote desktop authentication attempts originating from a localhost source address, which may indicate a tunneled login. Adversaries often use tunneling techniques to route network communications through victim systems, allowing them to evade detection and bypass network security controls. By tunneling Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) sessions, attackers can mask their true source and gain unauthorized access to systems. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Potential Remote Desktop Tunneling
s1ql: dataSource.name = "Windows Event Logs" AND winEventLog.id = 4624 AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.logonType = "10" AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.ipAddress in ("127.0.0.1", "::1") and not (winEventLog.data.event.eventData.subjectUserName matches '\\$$')

description: Detects the creation of scheduled tasks on Windows systems that include HTTP command arguments. An adversary can abuse this by using scheduled task service to create tasks that download and execute malicious payloads via HTTP, potentially leading to data exfiltration or malware propagation. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Potential Remote Payload Execution via Scheduled Task
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' and winEventLog.id = 4698 and winEventLog.data.event.eventData.taskContent matches '<Arguments>.*https?:.*</Arguments>'

description: Detects the creation of user accounts (event ID 4720) where the username ends with $. Normally, accounts ending with $ are computer accounts, and their creation generates event ID 4741 instead. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Potential Rogue User Account Created
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.id = '4720' AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.targetUserName matches '\\$$'

description: Detects PowerShell scripts being registered as a service via the COMSPEC environment variable for persistence. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL PowerShell Script Installed as Service
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.id = '7045' AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.imagePath matches 'COMSPEC.*powershell.exe'

description: Detects attempts to reset passwords for sensitive or privileged accounts in Active Directory. Privileged accounts and groups are assigned extensive rights, privileges, and permissions, enabling them to perform virtually any action within Active Directory and on domain-joined systems. An adversary may attempt to reset the password of a privileged account to gain unauthorized access, escalate privileges, or take control of critical systems. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Privileged Account Password Reset Attempt
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.id = 4724 AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.targetSid matches '^S-1-5-' AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.targetSid matches ('-512$', '-518$', '-519$', '-526$', '-527$', '-544$', '-548$', '-549$', '-550$', '-551$')

description: Detects PsExec service installation. PsExec can be used maliciously by threat actors to gain remote access to other machines. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL PsExec Service Installed
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.id = '7045' AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.serviceName in:anycase ('PSEXESVC') AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.imagePath contains 'psexesvc.exe'

description: Detects modifications to registry keys commonly used for persistence mechanisms. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Registry Run Keys Persistence
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.id = '4657' AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.objectName matches ('.*software\\\\microsoft\\\\windows\\\\currentversion.*\\\\run.*', '.*software\\\\microsoft\\\\windows\\\\currentversion\\\\.*shell folders.*', '.*software\\\\microsoft\\\\windows\\\\currentversion\\\\win.*') and winEventLog.data.event.eventData.objectName != '\\REGISTRY\\MACHINE\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\WindowsUpdate\\Auto Update'

description: Detects potential application whitelist bypass attempt by looking for regsvr32.exe process creation events that load scrobj.dll via the command line. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Regsvr32 Application Whitelist Bypass
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.id = '4688' AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.newProcessName matches 'regsvr32\\.exe$' AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.commandLine contains 'scrobj.dll'

description: Detects an attempt to execute code or command on a remote host using winrm. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Remote Code Execution via WinRM
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.id = '4688' AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.commandLine matches '.*winrm.*invoke create wmicimv2/win32_.* -r:.*'

description: Detects attempts to read the Security Account Manager (SAM) database control information via Windows Event Log 4661, indicating potential unauthorized access or reconnaissance activity. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL SAM Read Control Requested
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.channel = 'Security' winEventLog.id = 4661 AND winEventLog.description.objectServer = 'Security Account Manager' AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.accessList contains '%%5392' !(winEventLog.data.event.eventData.subjectUserName matches '\\$$')

description: Detects Windows security event logs deletion by looking for event ID 1102. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Security Event Log Cleared
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.id = '1102' AND winEventLog.providerName='Microsoft-Windows-Eventlog'

description: Detects modifications to sensitive LDAP attributes (ntSecurityDescriptor) while excluding common DNS objects. This helps identify potential unauthorized changes in Active Directory security settings. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Sensitive LDAP Attribute Modification
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.channel = 'Security' AND winEventLog.id=5136 AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.attributeLDAPDisplayName = 'ntSecurityDescriptor' AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.attributeValue in ('1131f6ad-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2', '1131f6aa-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2', '89e95b76-444d-4c62-991a-0facbeda640c') AND NOT (winEventLog.data.event.eventData.objectClass in ('dnsNode', 'dnsZoneScope', 'dnsZone'))

description: Detects the creation of a default Windows service associated with the Sliver Command and Control (C2) framework. This activity is indicative of potential adversary persistence or remote command execution attempts. Sliver C2 is often used by attackers to maintain control over compromised systems, allowing them to execute commands, exfiltrate data, or escalate privileges. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Sliver C2 Service Creation
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.data.event.system.eventID = 7045 AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.serviceName in:anycase ('sliver')

description: Detects 100 or more failed login attempts followed by a successful login, indicating a potential brute force attack. An adversary may use brute force techniques to guess passwords, repeatedly attempting to log in until they succeed. This behavior is a strong indicator of unauthorized access attempts and can lead to compromised accounts, data breaches, and further exploitation of organizational resources.
name: WEL Successful Brute Force Attack

description: Detects computer account name changes in Active Directory that do not end with a '$'. An adversary can abuse this to perform domain controller impersonation, privilege escalation, and potentially achieve full domain control. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Suspicious Computer Account Name Change
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' and winEventLog.id = 4781 and winEventLog.data.event.eventData.oldTargetUserName matches '\\$$' and not (winEventLog.data.event.eventData.newTargetUserName matches '\\$$')

description: Detects the creation of executable files within Windows administrative SMB shares. An adversary can abuse this to execute arbitrary code remotely by staging binaries with tools like PsExec, potentially leading to widespread network compromise and data exfiltration. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Suspicious Executable Written to Administrative SMB Share
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' and winEventLog.id = 5145 and winEventLog.data.event.eventData.relativeTargetName matches ('\\.exe$', '\\.dll$', '\\.msi$') and winEventLog.data.event.eventData.shareName in ("\\\\*\\C$","\\\\*\\IPC$","\\\\*\\admin$") and winEventLog.data.event.eventData.accessMask = '0x2'

description: Detects modifications to Microsoft Office macro security settings in the registry. Adversaries may enable Trust access to the VBA project object model or set VbaWarnings to 1, effectively bypassing macro security protections. These changes allow Microsoft Office to trust all macros, enabling the execution of malicious code without security warnings or user consent, thereby increasing the risk of compromise through phishing or malware delivery
name: WEL Suspicious Microsoft Office Macro Security Tampering
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.id = '4657' AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.objectName matches ('\\\\Software\\\\Microsoft\\\\Office\\\\.+\\\\Security\\\\AccessVBOM', '\\\\Software\\\\Microsoft\\\\Office\\\\.+\\\\Security\\\\VbaWarnings') AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.newValue in ('1', '0x1', '0x00000001')

description: Detects the registration of scheduled tasks that are configured to execute potentially malicious commands, as logged by Windows Security EventCode 4698. An adversary can abuse this by creating scheduled tasks that maintain persistent access, execute arbitrary code, or escalate privileges, enabling them to control the compromised system and perform further malicious actions. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Suspicious Scheduled Task Created
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' and winEventLog.id = 4698 and winEventLog.data.event.eventData.taskContent contains ('wscript', 'cscript', 'rundll32', 'regsvr32', 'mshta', 'certutil', 'bitsadmin')

description: Detects UAC bypass attempts by identifying suspicious registry modifications to the path used by Windows Event Viewer. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL UAC Bypass via Eventvwr
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.id = '4657' AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.objectName contains 'Software\\Classes\\mscfile\\shell\\open\\command'

description: Detects registry value changes to bypass User Access Control (UAC) protection. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL UAC Bypass Via Registry Modification
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.id = '4657' AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.objectName matches ('\\\\SOFTWARE\\\\Microsoft\\\\Windows\\\\CurrentVersion\\\\Policies\\\\System\\\\EnableLUA$', '\\\\SOFTWARE\\\\Microsoft\\\\Windows\\\\CurrentVersion\\\\Policies\\\\System\\\\ConsentPromptBehaviorAdmin$', '\\\\SOFTWARE\\\\Microsoft\\\\Windows\\\\CurrentVersion\\\\Policies\\\\System\\\\PromptOnSecureDesktop$') AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.oldValue in ('1', '0x1', '0x00000001') AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.newValue in ('0', '0x0', '0x00000000')

description: Detects modifications to the msDS-KeyCredentialLink attribute in an Active Directory (AD) computer or user object. An adversary can exploit this by generating a key pair and appending the raw public key to the attribute, allowing them to gain persistent, stealthy access to the targeted user or computer object. By manipulating this attribute, attackers can bypass traditional authentication methods, maintaining long-term unauthorized access and potentially escalating their privileges within the environment. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Unusual Credential Association with AD Object
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.id = 5136 AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.attributeLDAPDisplayName = 'msDS-KeyCredentialLink' AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.attributeValue matches '^B:828:'

description: Detects user additions (4732) to the Administrators group (S-1-5-32-544), excluding changes made by computer accounts, to identify potential unauthorized privilege escalation. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL User Added as Admin to Local Security Group
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.channel = 'Security' AND winEventLog.id = 4732 AND (winEventLog.data.event.eventData.targetSid='S-1-5-32-544' OR winEventLog.data.event.eventData.targetUserName matches '^Admin') AND NOT (winEventLog.data.event.eventData.subjectUserName matches '\\$$')

description: Detects user additions to a highly privileged Active Directory global security group, such as Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, Schema Admins, or DnsAdmins, excluding changes made by computer accounts, to identify potential unauthorized privilege escalation. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
